Spinal anaesthesia (or spinal anesthesia), also called spinal block, subarachnoid block, intradural block and intrathecal block, is a form of neuraxial regional anaesthesia involving the injection of a local anaesthetic with or without an opioid into the subarachnoid space. Usually a single-shot dose is administrered through a fine needle, alternatively continuous spinal anaesthesia through a intrathecal catheter can be performed. It is a safe and effective form of anesthesia usually performed by anesthesiologists that can be used as an alternative to general anesthesia commonly in surgeries involving the lower extremities and surgeries below the umbilicus. The local anesthetic with or without an opioid injected into the cerebrospinal fluid provides locoregional anaesthesia: true anaesthesia, motor, sensory and autonomic (sympathetic) blockade.
Administering analgesics (opioid, alpha2-adrenoreceptor agonist) in the cerebrospinal fluid without a local anaesthetic produces locoregional analgesia: markedly reduced pain sensation (incomplete analgesia), some autonomic blockade (parasympathetic plexi), but no sensory or motor block.
Locoregional analgesia, due to mainly the absence of motor and sympathetic block may be preferred over locoregional anaesthesia in some postoperative care settings.
The tip of the spinal needle has a point or small bevel. Recently, pencil point needles have been made available (Whitacre, Sprotte, Gertie Marx and others).


== Indications ==
Spinal anaesthesia is a commonly used technique, either on its own or in combination with sedation or general anaesthesia. It is most commonly used for surgeries below the umbilicus, however recently its uses have extended to some surgeries above the umbilicus as well as for postoperative analgesia. Procedures which use spinal anesthesia include:

Orthopaedic surgery on the pelvis, hip, femur, knee, tibia, and ankle, including arthroplasty and joint replacement
Vascular surgery on the legs
Endovascular aortic aneurysm repair
Hernia (inguinal or epigastric)
Haemorrhoidectomy
Nephrectomy and cystectomy in combination with general anaesthesia
Transurethral resection of the prostate and transurethral resection of bladder tumours
Hysterectomy in different techniques used
Caesarean sections
Pain management during vaginal birth and delivery
Urology cases
Examinations under anaesthesia
Spinal anaesthesia is the technique of choice for Caesarean section as it avoids a general anaesthetic and the risk of failed intubation (which is probably a lot lower than the widely quoted 1 in 250 in pregnant women). It also means the mother is conscious and the partner is able to be present at the birth of the child. The post operative analgesia from intrathecal opioids in addition to non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs is also good.
Spinal anesthesia may be favored when the surgical site is amenable to spinal blockade for patients with severe respiratory disease such as COPD as it avoids the potential respiratory consequences of intubation and ventilation. It may also be useful in patients where anatomical abnormalities may make tracheal intubation relatively difficult.
In pediatric patients, spinal anesthesia is particularly useful in children with difficult airways and those who are poor candidates for endotracheal anesthesia such as increased respiratory risks or presence of full stomach.
This can also be used to effectively treat and prevent pain following surgery, particularly thoracic, abdominal pelvic, and lower extremity orthopedic procedures.


== Contraindications ==
Prior to receiving spinal anesthesia, it is important to provide a thorough medical evaluation to ensure there are no absolute contraindications and to minimize risks and complications. Although contraindications are rare, below are some of them:

Patient refusal
Local infection or sepsis at the site of injection
Bleeding disorders, thrombocytopaenia, or systemic anticoagulation (secondary to an increased risk of a spinal epidural hematoma)
Severe aortic stenosis
Increased intracranial pressure
Space occupying lesions of the brain
Anatomical disorders of the spine such as scoliosis (although where pulmonary function is also impaired, spinal anaesthesia may be favored)
Hypovolaemia e.g. following massive haemorrhage, including in obstetric patients
Allergy
Relative Contraindication

Ehlers–Danlos syndrome, or other disorders causing resistance to local anesthesia


== Risks and complications ==
Complications of spinal anesthesia can result from the physiologic effects on the nervous system and can also be related to placement technique. Most of the common side effects are minor and are self-resolving or easily treatable while major complications can result in more serious and permanent neurological damage and rarely death. These symptoms can occur immediately after administration of the anesthetic or be delayed.
Common and minor complications include:

Mild hypotension
Bradycardia
Nausea and vomiting
Transient neurological symptoms (lower back pain with pain in the legs) 
Post-dural-puncture headache or post-spinal headache – Associated with the size and type of spinal needle used. A 2020 meta analysis recommended use of the 26G atraumatic spinal needle to lower the risk of PDPH – specifically, the Braun Atraucan 26G needle.
Serious and permanent complications are rare but are usually related to physiologic effects on the cardiovascular system and neurological system or when the injection has been unintentionally at the wrong site. The following are some major complications:

Nerve injuries: Cauda equina syndrome, radiculopathy
Cardiac arrest
Severe hypotension
Spinal epidural hematoma, with or without subsequent neurological sequelae due to compression of the spinal nerves.
Epidural abscess
Infection (e.g. meningitis)


== Technique ==
Regardless of the anaesthetic agent (drug) used, the desired effect is to block the transmission of afferent nerve signals from peripheral nociceptors. Sensory signals from the site are blocked, thereby eliminating pain. The degree of neuronal blockade depends on the amount and concentration of local anaesthetic used and the properties of the axon. Thin unmyelinated C-fibres associated with pain are blocked first, while thick, heavily myelinated A-alpha motor neurons are blocked moderately. Heavily myelinated, small preganglionic sympathetic fibers are blocked last. The desired result is total numbness of the area. A pressure sensation is permissible and often occurs due to incomplete blockade of the thicker A-beta mechanoreceptors. This allows surgical procedures to be performed with no painful sensation to the person undergoing the procedure.
Some sedation is sometimes provided to help the patient relax and pass the time during the procedure, but with a successful spinal anaesthetic the surgery can be performed with the patient wide awake.


=== Anatomy ===
In spinal anesthesia, the needle is placed past the dura mater in subarachnoid space and between lumbar vertebrae. In order to reach this space, the needle must pierce through several layers of tissue and ligaments which include the supraspinous ligament, interspinous ligament, and ligamentum flavum. Because the spinal cord (conus medullaris) is typically at the L1 or L2 level of the spine, the needle should be inserted below this between L3 and L4 space or L4 and L5 space in order to avoid injury to the spinal cord.


=== Positioning ===
Patient positioning is essential to the success of the procedure and can affect how the anesthetic spreads following administration. There are three different positions which are used: sitting, lateral decubitus, and prone. The sitting and lateral decubitus positions are the most common.
Sitting – The patient sits upright at the edge of the exam table with their back facing the provider and their legs hanging off the end of the table and feet resting on a stool. Patients should roll their shoulders and upper back forward.
Lateral decubitus – In this position, the patient lies on their side with their back at the edge of the bed and facing the provider. The patient should curl their shoulder and legs and arch out their lower back.
Prone – The patient is positioned face down and their back facing upwards in a jackknife position.


=== Limitations ===
Spinal anaesthetics are typically limited to procedures involving most structures below the upper abdomen. To administer a spinal anaesthetic to higher levels may affect the ability to breathe by paralysing the intercostal respiratory muscles, or even the diaphragm in extreme cases (called a "high spinal", or a "total spinal", with which consciousness is lost), as well as the body's ability to control the heart rate via the cardiac accelerator fibres. Also, injection of spinal anaesthesia higher than the level of L1 can cause damage to the spinal cord, and is therefore usually not done.


=== Differences with epidural anaesthesia ===

Epidural anaesthesia is a technique whereby a local anaesthetic drug is injected through a catheter placed into the epidural space. This technique is similar to spinal anaesthesia as both are neuraxial, and the two techniques may be easily confused with each other. Differences include:

A spinal anaesthetic delivers drug to the subarachnoid space and into the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), allowing it to act on the spinal cord directly. An epidural delivers drugs outside the dura (outside CSF), and has its main effect on nerve roots leaving the dura at the level of the epidural, rather than on the spinal cord itself.
A spinal gives profound block of all motor and sensory function below the level of injection, whereas an epidural blocks a 'band' of nerve roots around the site of injection, with normal function above, and close-to-normal function below the levels blocked.
The injected dose for an epidural is larger, being about 10–20 mL compared to 1.5–3.5 mL in a spinal.
In an epidural, an indwelling catheter may be placed that allows for redosing injections, while a spinal is almost always a one-shot only. Therefore, spinal anaesthesia is more often used for shorter procedures relative to procedures which require epidural anaesthesia.
The onset of analgesia is approximately 25–30 minutes in an epidural, while it is approximately 5 minutes in a spinal.
An epidural often does not cause as significant a neuromuscular block as a spinal, unless specific local anaesthetics are also used which block motor fibres as readily as sensory nerve fibres.
An epidural may be given at a cervical, thoracic, or lumbar site, while a spinal must be injected below L2 to avoid piercing the spinal cord.


=== Injected substances ===
Bupivacaine (Marcaine) is the local anaesthetic most commonly used, although lidocaine (lignocaine), tetracaine, procaine, ropivacaine, levobupivicaine, prilocaine, or cinchocaine may also be used. Commonly opioids are added to improve the block and provide post-operative pain relief, examples include morphine, fentanyl, diamorphine, and buprenorphine. Non-opioids like clonidine or epinephrine may also be added to prolong the duration of analgesia (although Clonidine may cause hypotension). In the United Kingdom, since 2004 the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence recommends that spinal anaesthesia for Caesarean section is supplemented with intrathecal diamorphine and this combination is now the modal form of anaesthesia for this indication in that country. In the United States, morphine is used for cesareans for the same purpose since diamorphine (heroin) is not used in clinical practice in the US.
Baricity refers to the density of a substance compared to the density of human cerebrospinal fluid. Baricity is used in anaesthesia to determine the manner in which a particular drug will spread in the intrathecal space. Usually, the hyperbaric, (for example, hyperbaric bupivacaine) is chosen, as its spread can be effectively and predictably controlled by the Anaesthesiologist, by tilting the patient. Hyperbaric solutions are made more dense by adding glucose to the mixture.
Baricity is one factor that determines the spread of a spinal anaesthetic but the effect of adding a solute to a solvent, i.e. solvation or dissolution, also has an effect on the spread of the spinal anaesthetic. In tetracaine spinal anaesthesia, it was discovered that the rate of onset of analgesia was faster and the maximum level of analgesia was higher with a 10% glucose solution than with a 5% glucose spinal anaesthetic solution. Also, the amount of ephedrine required was less in the patients who received the 5% glucose solution. In another study this time with 0.5% bupivacaine the mean maximum extent of sensory block was significantly higher with 8% glucose (T3.6) than with 0.83% glucose (T7.2) or 0.33% glucose (T9.5). Also the rate of onset of sensory block to T12 was fastest with solutions containing 8% glucose.


== History ==

The first spinal analgesia was administered in 1885 by James Leonard Corning (1855–1923), a neurologist in New York. He was experimenting with cocaine on the spinal nerves of a dog when he accidentally pierced the dura mater.
The first planned spinal anaesthesia for surgery on a human was administered by August Bier (1861–1949) on 16 August 1898, in Kiel, when he injected 3 ml of 0.5% cocaine solution into a 34-year-old labourer. After using it on six patients, he and his assistant each injected cocaine into the other's spine. They recommended it for surgeries of legs, but gave it up due to the toxicity of cocaine.

Admission Note
HPI:
- 52-year-old patient admitted electively for repair of symptomatic inguinal hernia under general anaesthesia.
- No active cardiorespiratory symptoms. Denies daily alcohol or illicit drug use. Last oral intake 8 hours prior to arrival.
- Pre-anaesthetic interview: understands plan for general anaesthesia, risks explained including airway management and need for endotracheal intubation.

Physical exam:
- Airway assessment: mouth opening adequate, Mallampati II, thyromental distance adequate, neck range of motion preserved, no loose dentures or crowns.
- Cardiovascular and respiratory exams: heart sounds dual, lungs clear to auscultation.
- Neurological: alert and oriented preoperatively.

Assessment and Plan:
- ASA classification: (documented based on clinical judgment).
- Anaesthetic goals reviewed: hypnosis/unconsciousness, analgesia, amnesia, immobility, muscle relaxation as required for surgery.
- Premedication: single dose benzodiazepine for anxiolysis and mild sedation prior to induction.
- Anaesthetic plan:
  - Intravenous induction followed by administration of neuromuscular blocking agent to facilitate tracheal intubation.
  - Endotracheal intubation and initiation of controlled mechanical ventilation.
  - Maintenance of anaesthesia with an inhaled volatile agent (isoflurane preferred) plus adjunctive analgesic.
  - Standard intraoperative monitoring (non-invasive and invasive as indicated) throughout.
- Contingencies: airway equipment for difficult intubation available; plan for postoperative monitoring in PACU with readiness to ventilate if spontaneous breathing inadequate.
- Proceed to theatre for planned repair.

Hospital Day 2 — Progress Note
- Immediate intraoperative course: induction, neuromuscular blockade, endotracheal intubation and controlled ventilation achieved without difficulty. Maintenance with isoflurane and adjunct analgesia. Surgical procedure completed as planned.
- Emergence: patient awakened and extubated in theatre once criteria for return of adequate spontaneous ventilation and protective airway reflexes met.
- PACU: hemodynamically stable; no evidence of awareness reported by patient; oriented after emergence consistent with expected amnestic effects of anaesthesia.
- Current status (Postoperative day 1): pain controlled with scheduled analgesia; respiratory effort adequate on room air; no airway complications, no requirement for reintubation or ventilatory support.
- Plan:
  - Continue routine postoperative analgesia and monitoring.
  - Encourage mobilization and oral intake as tolerated.
  - Monitor for delayed anaesthetic effects (respiratory depression, awareness concerns); none observed.

Hospital Day 3 — Progress Note
- Clinical course since prior note: uncomplicated recovery from general anaesthesia. No episodes of respiratory compromise, aspiration, or new neurological findings.
- Pain manageable with oral analgesics.
- Patient ambulant, tolerating oral fluids.
- Discharge planning underway.
- Plan:
  - Discharge later today if stable.
  - Advise outpatient follow-up with surgical team.
  - Reinforce instructions regarding activity limitations and analgesia.

Discharge Summary
- Admission: Elective admission for inguinal hernia repair under general anaesthesia.
- Procedure performed: Inguinal hernia repair (surgical details documented in operative note).
- Anaesthesia: General anaesthesia planned and administered. Premedication with benzodiazepine for anxiolysis. Intravenous induction followed by administration of neuromuscular blocking agent to facilitate endotracheal intubation. Endotracheal tube placed and controlled mechanical ventilation used intraoperatively. Anaesthesia maintained with inhaled volatile agent (isoflurane) and adjunctive analgesia. Standard monitoring applied throughout.
- Intraoperative course: Uncomplicated. Adequate ventilation and oxygenation maintained. No difficulty with airway management.
- Postoperative course: Extubated in theatre after return of spontaneous ventilation and airway protective reflexes. Observed in PACU without evidence of awareness or residual respiratory depression. Postoperative recovery uncomplicated; pain controlled on oral analgesics; mobilizing and tolerating diet.
- Discharge condition: Stable for discharge home.
- Discharge instructions: Routine postoperative care per surgical team; return precautions and outpatient follow-up arranged. Advised that general anaesthesia was administered to provide unconsciousness, analgesia, amnesia and immobility during surgery and that no immediate anaesthetic-related complications occurred.

Admission Note — Day 0

HPI:
62-year-old man with newly diagnosed bulky non‑Hodgkin lymphoma (large nodal masses on staging imaging) received first cycle of anthracycline‑containing induction chemotherapy 48 hours prior to presentation. Presented to ED with palpitations, generalized weakness, perioral paresthesias and decreased urine output over 12 hours. No chest pain or focal neurologic deficit. Past medical history notable for CKD stage 3 (baseline creatinine 1.2 mg/dL), hypertension. Meds: antihypertensive, no recent NSAID use reported. Denies fever.

Physical exam:
- Vitals: T 36.8°C, HR 110 irregular, BP 138/82, RR 18, SpO2 98% RA.
- General: uncomfortable, mildly diaphoretic.
- HEENT: perioral paresthesia reported, no focal deficit.
- Cardiovascular: tachycardic, irregular rhythm, no murmurs.
- Pulmonary: clear bilaterally.
- Abdomen: soft, nontender.
- Extremities: no focal weakness but generalized decreased muscle strength subjectively.
- Neuro: alert and oriented x3, no seizures.

Initial studies:
- ECG: peaked T waves consistent with hyperkalemia; no ST elevation; no Torsades.
- Labs on admission:
  - K+ 6.6 mmol/L (baseline pre-chemo 4.5)
  - Phosphorus 6.0 mg/dL (pre-chemo 3.4)
  - Calcium (ionized) low; total Ca 6.5 mg/dL (pre-chemo 9.2)
  - Uric acid 10.5 mg/dL (pre-chemo 6.0)
  - BUN 48 mg/dL, creatinine 2.0 mg/dL (baseline 1.2)
  - Urinalysis: dark urine, microscopy with amorphous urates/uric acid crystals
  - Urine uric acid : creatinine ratio >1.0
  - CBC: WBC elevated consistent with underlying disease; otherwise unremarkable.

Assessment:
Patient meets Cairo‑Bishop criteria for clinical tumor lysis syndrome: laboratory TLS (uric acid >8 mg/dL, K+ >6 mmol/L, phosphate >4.5 mg/dL, Ca <7 mg/dL) plus clinical TLS with rise in creatinine to 1.67× baseline and oliguria. High risk features present: bulky, chemosensitive NHL, anthracycline exposure, baseline CKD.

Plan:
- Admit to monitored unit with continuous cardiac monitoring for arrhythmia surveillance.
- Frequent serum electrolytes, BUN/creatinine, uric acid and phosphorus q4–6 hrs.
- Urine output monitoring with strict I&O.
- Initiate measures to correct metabolic abnormalities and reduce uric acid burden (uremic and gouty complications anticipated) and treat hyperkalemia and hypocalcemia as clinically indicated.
- Hold nephrotoxic agents (avoid NSAIDs).
- Notify oncology; hold further cytotoxic therapy pending stabilization.
- Consult nephrology for acute kidney injury in setting of TLS and consideration of renal support if progressive.
- Counsel patient/family regarding TLS, potential complications (acute kidney injury, arrhythmia, seizures) and plan.

Hospital Day 2 — Progress Note

Subjective:
Patient reports improvement in palpitations and paresthesias overnight. Urine output improved compared with admission.

Objective:
- Vitals stable, HR 84, rhythm regular.
- ECG: resolution of peaked T waves; no new arrhythmia.
- Labs this AM:
  - K+ 4.8 mmol/L
  - Phosphorus 4.0 mg/dL
  - Total Ca 7.4 mg/dL
  - Uric acid 6.2 mg/dL
  - BUN 36 mg/dL, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL
- Urine output improving to >0.5 mL/kg/hr.
- No seizure activity, neurologic exam unchanged.

Assessment:
Biochemical indices of TLS improving after interventions. Still has mild azotemia above baseline but trending down. Continues to meet laboratory TLS criteria historically but clinical status improved; no arrhythmia or seizure.

Plan:
- Continue cardiac monitoring for 24–48 hrs given recent severe hyperkalemia.
- Continue frequent labs q6 hrs until stable, then spacing as appropriate.
- Continue measures to lower uric acid and manage electrolyte abnormalities; maintain adequate urine output.
- Continue avoidance of nephrotoxins.
- Nephrology concurs with continued medical management; no dialysis at present given improving urine output and creatinine.
- Oncology to reassess plan for subsequent chemotherapy cycles and consider TLS risk mitigation strategies prior to next administration.
- Educate patient to report recurrence of palpitations, severe muscle weakness, oliguria, or neurologic symptoms.

Hospital Day 3 — Progress Note

Subjective:
Patient ambulatory, reports resolution of paresthesias and muscle weakness. No palpitations.

Objective:
- Vitals stable, HR 76 regular.
- ECG normal.
- Labs this AM:
  - K+ 4.4 mmol/L
  - Phosphorus 3.6 mg/dL
  - Total Ca 8.6 mg/dL
  - Uric acid 5.2 mg/dL
  - BUN 28 mg/dL, creatinine 1.3 mg/dL (near baseline)
- Urine output now adequate; urinalysis improved, no significant crystalluria on repeat.

Assessment:
Marked clinical and laboratory improvement. TLS effectively treated with reversal of hyperkalemia, reduction in uric acid and phosphate, and recovery of renal function toward baseline. No cardiac or neurologic complications during hospitalization.

Plan:
- Discontinue continuous cardiac monitoring.
- Continue daily labs x1–2 days then outpatient monitoring.
- Oncology to delay additional cytotoxic therapy until TLS risk mitigation plan in place; will discuss altered timing/dose and prophylactic measures prior to next cycle.
- Nephrology follow‑up arranged; recommend avoidance of NSAIDs and other nephrotoxins, maintain oral hydration.
- Provide patient education on signs of recurrent TLS (oliguria, muscle weakness, palpitations, paresthesias, seizures) and to seek immediate care if symptoms recur.

Discharge Summary

Admission diagnosis:
- Clinical tumor lysis syndrome secondary to anthracycline‑containing chemotherapy for bulky non‑Hodgkin lymphoma.

Hospital course:
62‑year‑old man developed symptomatic TLS 48 hours after first cycle of anthracycline‑containing induction chemotherapy, presenting with hyperkalemia (6.6 mmol/L), hyperphosphatemia (6.0 mg/dL), hypocalcemia (6.5 mg/dL), hyperuricemia (10.5 mg/dL), and acute kidney injury (creatinine rise from 1.2 to 2.0 mg/dL) with oliguria and ECG changes. He was admitted to a monitored unit, received targeted management to correct electrolyte abnormalities and reduce uric acid burden, strict urine output monitoring, and avoidance of nephrotoxins. Nephrology and oncology were consulted. Over 72 hours his electrolytes and uric acid improved, urine output normalized and creatinine returned near baseline (1.3 mg/dL). No arrhythmia, seizures, or need for renal replacement therapy occurred.

Discharge condition:
Stable, afebrile, ambulatory, normal cardiac rhythm, renal function near baseline.

Discharge medications and instructions:
- Continue home medications except NSAIDs (avoid NSAIDs).
- Maintain good oral hydration.
- Return precautions: seek immediate care for decreased urine output, recurrent palpitations, severe muscle weakness, perioral/fingertip numbness, or seizure.
- Outpatient follow-up: Oncology clinic appointment scheduled to reassess chemotherapy plan and implement TLS risk mitigation prior to next cycle; Nephrology follow‑up arranged for outpatient renal monitoring.
- Labs: obtain basic metabolic panel, uric acid and phosphorus as outpatient within 48–72 hours and then as directed by oncology/nephrology.

Final diagnoses:
1) Clinical tumor lysis syndrome — resolved without dialysis.
2) Acute kidney injury, likely secondary to TLS — improving to near baseline.
3) Newly diagnosed bulky non‑Hodgkin lymphoma — therapy on hold pending risk mitigation.

Admission Note
HPI: 46-year-old female presents with acute onset left flank pain of 12 hours' duration. Nonradiating, sharp, severe at onset with intermittent improvement. No fevers, no gross hematuria reported. No prior history of nephrolithiasis documented. Current medications: none. Not pregnant by history and point-of-care pregnancy test negative. No history of bleeding disorder and not taking platelet aggregation inhibitors. No known aortic aneurysm. Blood pressure at triage within patient's baseline (no severe untreated hypertension).

Imaging: CT KUB (noncontrast) demonstrates a 9 mm radiopaque calculus in the left renal collecting system. No imaging evidence reported of other stones.

Physical exam: Vitals stable. General: alert, comfortable at rest. Abdomen: soft, nondistended, no peritoneal signs. Left CVA tenderness present. Rest of exam without acute findings.

Assessment/Plan:
- Primary diagnosis: left renal calculus, 9 mm.
- Candidate for lithotripsy: stone size within guideline range for extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL) (AUA guidance: 4 mm–2 cm).
- Contraindication screen: pregnancy test negative; no coagulopathy or antiplatelet therapy; no known aortic aneurysm; blood pressure controlled; urinalysis/culture obtained and pending (no clinical concern for untreated UTI).
- Discussed treatment options and rationale: recommend extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL) as first-line for this stone size. Alternatives discussed if ESWL ineffective or fragments persist: intracorporeal endoscopic approaches including ultrasonic lithotripsy (noted to be safer for small stones <10 mm) and laser lithotripsy (holmium:YAG laser can be used endoscopically and produces smaller fragments; laser techniques are effective for larger stones as well). Also explained that electrohydraulic and pneumatic modalities are other intracorporeal options used in specific contexts.
- Plan: proceed with ESWL under appropriate analgesia/sedation tomorrow pending negative urine culture and continued absence of contraindications. Instructed patient to strain urine for stone fragments for analysis. Consent obtained for ESWL.

Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
Procedure: Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL) performed this morning with imaging localization of the left renal calculus. Shockwave energy focused at the stone with multiple treatment cycles. Procedure completed without intra-procedural complications.

Immediate post-procedure imaging: post-ESWL kidney radiograph demonstrates fragmentation of the known 9 mm left renal calculus into multiple smaller fragments.

Post-procedure course: patient returned to unit hemodynamically stable, pain improved compared with pre-procedure baseline. No signs of infection; urinalysis/culture remain negative. No anticoagulants or platelet inhibitors were given peri-procedurally. Will maintain observation overnight for pain control and to monitor for complications.

Plan:
- Continue observation; analgesia as needed.
- Strain urine and collect any fragments.
- Repeat plain radiograph/KUB tomorrow to document fragment passage or change in stone burden.
- If fragments remain large or obstructing on follow-up imaging, plan conversion to endoscopic intracorporeal lithotripsy (options: ureteroscopic holmium:YAG laser lithotripsy or ultrasonic intracorporeal lithotripsy) discussed with patient.

Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
Clinical status: patient afebrile, vitals stable. Flank pain markedly improved, tolerating oral intake. No hematuria reported beyond scant transient pink discoloration, no urinary urgency or dysuria. Urine culture negative.

Imaging: follow-up plain radiograph/KUB demonstrates interval reduction in size and number of visualized fragments compared with immediate post-procedure film; several small radiopaque fragments consistent with post-ESWL pulverization noted.

Assessment/Plan:
- Impression: successful fragmentation of left renal calculus after ESWL with interval radiographic improvement and clinical symptom relief. No evidence of obstruction or infection during observation.
- Continue to strain urine and collect fragments for analysis.
- Discharge planning for today with outpatient urology follow-up in 1–2 weeks for reassessment and repeat imaging as outpatient. Patient instructed to return earlier for fever, worsening flank pain, inability to tolerate oral intake, or new gross hematuria.

Discharge Summary
Admission diagnosis: Left renal calculus, 9 mm, treated with extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy.

Hospital course:
- Day 1: Admitted for acute left flank pain. CT KUB confirmed 9 mm left renal stone. Screened for ESWL contraindications: pregnancy negative, no antiplatelet/anticoagulant use, no known aortic aneurysm, blood pressure controlled, urine studies negative for infection. Patient consented for ESWL.
- Day 2: Underwent ESWL with imaging localization; procedure tolerated without complication. Immediate post-procedure radiograph showed fragmentation of the calculus.
- Day 3: Observed overnight and reassessed. Pain improved, urine culture negative, follow-up radiograph demonstrated interval decrease in stone burden with residual small fragments. No clinical or imaging evidence of obstruction or infection.

Procedures performed: Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL) to left renal calculus with documented fragmentation.

Discharge condition: stable; pain controlled on oral analgesia.

Discharge instructions:
- Strain all urine and bring any collected fragments to the urology clinic for analysis.
- Outpatient urology follow-up in 1–2 weeks for repeat imaging and assessment; earlier return if fever, worsening flank pain, inability to tolerate oral intake, or concerning urinary symptoms.
- If residual obstructing fragments persist on outpatient imaging, options include endoscopic intracorporeal lithotripsy (ureteroscopic holmium:YAG laser or ultrasonic lithotripsy) as discussed.

Provider: Attending physician (de-identified)

1. Admission Note
HPI:
- 26-year-old male, previously healthy, developed acute hypermetabolic crisis during induction of general anesthesia for elective outpatient procedure. Anesthetic regimen included a volatile agent (sevoflurane) and succinylcholine. Approximately 15–25 minutes after administration of triggering agents the patient developed generalized muscle rigidity (notably masseter rigidity), rapid rise in end-tidal CO2, tachycardia and increasing temperature. Rapid blue‑light cooling measures were initiated in the OR and dantrolene was given emergently. Patient was intubated and mechanically ventilated for hypercapnia and transferred to ICU for ongoing management.
- No prior personal history of similar events. Family history unknown at time of admission.

Physical exam (on arrival to ICU):
- General: intubated, sedated.
- Vitals: febrile, tachycardic. (Exact values recorded in ICU flow sheet.)
- HEENT: jaw/masseter rigidity noted earlier; now generalized skeletal muscle rigidity persists.
- Cardiac: tachycardic, regular.
- Respiratory: mechanically ventilated, high minute ventilation instituted for elevated CO2.
- Abdomen/Extremities: cool peripheries from active cooling measures; urine dark in color.
- Neuro: sedated, no focal deficits.

Assessment and Plan:
- Working diagnosis: malignant hyperthermia (MH) precipitated by exposure to sevoflurane and succinylcholine.
- Immediate management already performed in OR: dantrolene administered, active cooling initiated, endotracheal intubation and controlled ventilation, aggressive IV fluids started. Foley in place for urine output monitoring.
- Problem list / plan:
  1) Hypermetabolic crisis/MH — continue ICU monitoring. Have dantrolene immediately available; administer additional doses PRN per MH protocol.
  2) Hypercapnia/mixed acidosis — continue controlled mechanical ventilation; obtain serial ABGs and end‑tidal CO2 monitoring.
  3) Rhabdomyolysis — monitor CK, serum potassium, myoglobinuria; continue aggressive IV fluids to preserve renal function; monitor urine output and renal function.
  4) Hyperkalemia — monitor closely and treat if worsening per ICU protocol.
  5) Fever/thermoregulation — continue active cooling until normothermic.
  6) Counseling/notification — notify anesthesia and arrange genetics consultation and counseling for MH susceptibility and family testing (genetic testing and/or muscle biopsy).
- Orders: STAT labs (ABG, BMP, CK, serum myoglobin if available, CBC), continuous cardiac/ETCO2 monitoring, maintain dantrolene availability, IV fluids, cooling measures, urine output hourly, ICU transfer (completed).

2. Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
- Overnight course: patient remained sedated and mechanically ventilated. Additional small doses of dantrolene administered per ICU/MH protocol for control of recurrent rigidity/temperature. Temperature normalized overnight with cooling measures. End‑tidal CO2 and ventilatory parameters improved with increased minute ventilation and supportive care.
- Labs: serial CK markedly elevated on initial draw and peaked overnight; CK now trending downward. Initial laboratory panel demonstrated mixed metabolic and respiratory acidosis which has improved on serial ABGs. Mild hyperkalemia was documented early and corrected/normalized with supportive measures. Creatinine remained within normal limits; urine output adequate though urine noted to be dark consistent with myoglobinuria.
- Exam: afebrile, sedation lightened; residual decreased muscle rigidity. Hemodynamically stable.
- Assessment:
  1) MH — stabilized after dantrolene and cooling. No ongoing hyperthermia. Laboratory evidence of rhabdomyolysis with elevated CK and myoglobinuria; renal function preserved.
  2) Respiratory — improving gas exchange on ventilator; ETCO2 improved.
  3) Electrolytes — hyperkalemia resolved; continue monitoring.
- Plan:
  - Continue ICU monitoring, serial CK, CMP, ABGs, urine output.
  - Continue IV fluids to mitigate risk of myoglobinuric renal injury.
  - Keep dantrolene available for 24 hours and give additional doses per protocol if recurrence.
  - Discuss genetic testing and family screening with patient/family once patient more alert.
  - Daily anesthesia and genetics consults placed.

3. Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
- Clinical course: patient weaned from sedation and successfully extubated this morning after demonstrating adequate respiratory effort and gas exchange. No recurrence of fever or rigidity. CK and serum myoglobin continue to downtrend. Creatinine remains normal. No arrhythmias; electrolytes stable.
- Exam: awake, hemodynamically stable, afebrile, no muscle rigidity on exam, urine color improving.
- Assessment:
  1) MH — resolved acute phase after prompt dantrolene and cooling. Continued improvement without need for further dantrolene.
  2) Rhabdomyolysis — improving CK trend; renal function preserved.
- Plan:
  - Transition to ward-level care for monitoring and early discharge planning.
  - Provide counseling regarding MH susceptibility, avoidance of triggering agents (volatile anesthetics and succinylcholine) for all future anesthetics.
  - Arrange outpatient follow-up with clinical genetics and anesthesia preoperative clinic for formal evaluation and family testing (genetic testing and/or muscle biopsy as indicated).
  - Recommend medical alert identification noting MH susceptibility.
  - Continue IV fluids until adequate oral intake; serial labs today and prior to discharge.

4. Discharge Summary
- Admission diagnosis: Malignant hyperthermia precipitated by exposure to sevoflurane and succinylcholine; associated rhabdomyolysis.
- Hospital course summary:
  - Patient developed acute MH during induction of general anesthesia with signs of generalized rigidity, hypercarbia, tachycardia and hyperthermia. Immediate recognition in the OR prompted emergent administration of dantrolene and active cooling with airway control and ventilation. Patient transferred to ICU for continued care.
  - In ICU received additional dantrolene per MH protocol as needed, aggressive cooling measures, and supportive care including IV fluids, ventilatory support, and close electrolyte and renal monitoring.
  - Laboratory course: initial mixed metabolic/respiratory acidosis and mild hyperkalemia that improved with supportive care. Marked elevation of CK and myoglobinuria consistent with rhabdomyolysis; CK peaked and has trended downward. Renal function remained within normal limits throughout hospitalization.
  - Clinical course: patient stabilized over 48 hours, extubated on hospital day 3, afebrile and without recurrent rigidity. No cardiac arrhythmias or renal failure occurred.
- Condition at discharge: hemodynamically stable, afebrile, ambulatory, normal renal function, improving CK trend.
- Discharge medications: none related to MH (no ongoing dantrolene required). (Patient advised to continue any regular home meds as per PCP.)
- Discharge instructions and follow-up:
  - Avoid all known MH-triggering anesthetic agents (volatile inhalational anesthetics such as sevoflurane, desflurane, isoflurane, halothane, enflurane and depolarizing muscle relaxants such as succinylcholine/decamethonium).
  - Carry medical alert identifying malignant hyperthermia susceptibility; inform all future anesthesia teams.
  - Outpatient referral placed to Clinical Genetics for evaluation and family testing (genetic testing and/or muscle biopsy as appropriate) given autosomal dominant inheritance and common association with RYR1 mutations.
  - Follow-up with Anesthesiology Preoperative Clinic within 4 weeks for documentation of MH event and planning for future anesthesia.
  - Return precautions: seek immediate care for fever, muscle rigidity, dark urine, decreased urine output, palpitations or weakness.
- Prognosis: favorable with prompt treatment; counseling provided that mortality with appropriate treatment is substantially reduced compared with untreated cases.
- Physician signature: Attending physician (de-identified).

1. Admission Note (HPI, Physical Exam, A/P)

HPI:
20-year-old man with known transfusion-dependent beta-thalassemia major (diagnosed in infancy; on regular PRBC transfusions every ~3 weeks) presented to ED with 2 weeks of progressive fatigue, exertional dyspnea, palpitations and dark urine. Reports he missed his scheduled transfusion due to travel; last transfusion ~6 weeks ago. No fevers, chest pain, focal neuro deficits. History notable for chronic transfusion dependence; prior records note splenomegaly and prior elevated ferritin levels. No known drug allergies.

Physical exam:
General: small-statured male, fatigued but conversant.
Vital signs: T 36.8 C, HR 110 irregular, BP 118/72, RR 18, SpO2 98% RA.
HEENT: scleral icterus present.
Cardiac: tachycardic, irregular rhythm, no murmurs.
Lungs: clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Abdomen: hepatomegaly 3 cm below costal margin; spleen palpated 4 cm below costal margin.
Skin: pallor, mild jaundice.
Neurologic: nonfocal.

Relevant labs/imaging on admission:
- CBC: Hgb 6.2 g/dL, MCV 62 fL, WBC 5.8 x10^3/µL, platelets 120 x10^3/µL.
- Reticulocyte count 3.8%.
- Peripheral smear: marked microcytosis, target cells.
- Total bilirubin 3.2 mg/dL (indirect predominant).
- AST 65 U/L, ALT 58 U/L.
- Ferritin 3200 ng/mL.
- Hemoglobin electrophoresis (prior documentation): markedly elevated HbF with minimal/absent HbA, consistent with beta-thalassemia major.
- ECG: frequent premature ventricular complexes, otherwise no acute ischemic changes.
- Chest x-ray: no acute cardiopulmonary process.

Assessment and Plan:
1) Transfusion-dependent beta-thalassemia major — admitted for symptomatic anemia after prolonged interval since last transfusion.
   - Plan: transfuse 2 units packed red blood cells with monitoring for transfusion reaction. Post-transfusion Hgb target ~9–10 g/dL.
   - Monitor vitals and urine output during transfusion; repeat CBC post-transfusion.

2) Evidence of chronic transfusional iron overload (ferritin 3200) with biochemical liver injury and arrhythmia concerning for cardiac iron effect.
   - Plan: cardiac monitoring while inpatient. Obtain baseline cardiac evaluation (ECG performed). Discuss iron overload management with Hematology (outpatient/inpatient plan for iron overload to be arranged). Monitor LFTs and ferritin trends.
   - Consult Cardiology for assessment of PVCs and possible iron-related cardiomyopathy.

3) Splenomegaly and mild thrombocytopenia, likely hypersplenism related to underlying disease.
   - Plan: monitor CBC; hematology to advise on longitudinal management of splenomegaly/hypersplenism.

4) Patient education regarding signs of worsening anemia, infection, and when to present to ED. Arrange transfusion scheduling and specialty follow-up prior to discharge.

Orders:
- Transfuse 2 units PRBCs per protocol.
- Cardiac monitor.
- Labs: CBC q6h x 2 then q12h, CMP, bilirubin, ferritin q24h while inpatient.
- Hematology and Cardiology consults placed.

2. Hospital Day 2 Progress Note

Subjective:
Patient reports marked improvement in dyspnea and fatigue overnight after transfusion. No chest pain, fever, rigors. Still notes intermittent palpitations.

Objective:

Labs:

- Total bilirubin 2.6 mg/dL.

- Ferritin 3230 ng/mL.

Consults/Recommendations:

Notes chronic high ferritin consistent with transfusional iron overload; recommends arranging longitudinal iron overload management and monitoring. Discussed need for specialized iron-overload therapy planning with patient; will schedule outpatient hematology follow-up within 1 week to initiate iron management plan and to re-establish routine transfusion interval (recommended every 3–4 weeks).
- Cardiology: evaluated patient. PVCs noted on telemetry; no hemodynamic instability. Recommends outpatient cardiac follow-up for further evaluation of possible iron-related cardiac involvement and optimization of care. No acute cardiac intervention indicated inpatient.

Assessment:
- Status post 2 units PRBC with symptomatic improvement.
- Persistent laboratory evidence of iron overload with mild hepatic transaminitis.
- Isolated ventricular ectopy without hemodynamic compromise.

Plan:

- Repeat CBC in AM; monitor LFTs and bilirubin.
- Discharge planning: arrange hematology and cardiology follow-up appointments; schedule next transfusion in 3 weeks.

3. Hospital Day 3 Progress Note

Subjective:
Patient reports further improvement; ambulating without dyspnea at rest. Palpitations less frequent.

Objective:
Vitals within normal limits. Cardiac monitor: occasional PVCs only. Exam unchanged.

Labs:
- CBC AM: Hgb 10.5 g/dL, platelets 115 x10^3/µL.
- Total bilirubin 2.4 mg/dL.
- AST 58 U/L, ALT 54 U/L.

Assessment:

4. Discharge Summary

Admission diagnosis:

- Chronic transfusional iron overload with hepatic enzyme elevation and cardiac ectopy concerning for iron-related cardiomyopathy.

Hospital course:
Patient admitted for symptomatic anemia after missed routine transfusion. On admission Hgb 6.2 g/dL with clinical pallor, dyspnea, and palpitations. Transfused 2 units packed red blood cells without complication. Hemoglobin rose to 10.5 g/dL with corresponding clinical improvement. Telemetry demonstrated isolated premature ventricular complexes without hemodynamic instability. Laboratory evaluation notable for ferritin 3200–3250 ng/mL and mild transaminitis, consistent with chronic iron overload from long-term transfusions. Hematology and Cardiology consulted; both recommended outpatient specialty follow-up for definitive iron overload management planning and further cardiac evaluation. No transfusion reactions or infectious complications occurred.

Condition at discharge:
Stable, ambulating, symptoms improved. Hgb 10.5 g/dL.

Discharge medications:
