- Return precautions: recurrent or worsening headache, fever, signs of local infection at site, new neurologic symptoms (weakness, numbness, focal deficits), or concerns about newborn feeding or temperature regulation.
- Outpatient follow-up with obstetrics as scheduled. Contact L&D/obstetrics if symptoms recur or new neurologic/ infectious concerns arise.

Procedures performed: epidural catheter placement (L3–4), epidural blood patch.

Admission Note
Date/time: Hospital day 0

HPI:
28-year-old female presents with acute anterior epistaxis refractory to outpatient compressive measures, ongoing for ~6 hours prior to arrival. Reports lifelong easy bruising and menorrhagia since menarche with frequent soaking of pads; several prior episodes of epistaxis that were self-limited. No history of joint or deep tissue bleeding. Mother reportedly had “bleeding problems” but no formal diagnosis known. No recent illness, no new medications. No prior coagulation testing documented.

Physical exam:
T 36.8 C, HR 88, BP 118/72, RR 14, SpO2 99% RA.
General: alert, uncomfortable secondary to nasal bleeding.
HEENT: anterior nasal bleeding noted from left nare at presentation; bleeding controlled after local direct pressure in ED. No oropharyngeal bleeding.
Skin: multiple ecchymoses on bilateral forearms, non-tender.
Cardiac: regular, no murmurs.
Lungs: clear.
Abdomen: soft, nontender.
Neuro: nonfocal.

Initial laboratory data:
CBC: Hgb 9.5 g/dL, Hct 29%, platelets 245 K/µL.
Coagulation: PT/INR within normal limits, APTT mildly prolonged at 42 sec (lab reference upper limit 40 sec), fibrinogen normal.
VWF testing ordered stat: VWF antigen, ristocetin cofactor (RiCof) activity, factor VIII level. ABO blood group ordered.
BMP within normal limits.

Assessment/Plan:
1) Suspected von Willebrand disease (clinical history of mucocutaneous bleeding, menorrhagia, recurrent epistaxis). Differential favors hereditary VWD type 1 given lifelong mild-moderate bleeding phenotype and family history; will confirm with VWF antigen, RiCof and factor VIII assays and further specialized testing as needed.
2) Acute hemostasis: desmopressin administered in ED to promote release of endogenous VWF given suspected VWD and bleeding limited to mucosa. Hemostasis achieved; patient admitted for observation and diagnostic workup.
3) Labs: stat VWF antigen, RiCof, factor VIII, repeat CBC q12h, PT/INR, APTT, fibrinogen. ABO group measured (recognize blood group O lowers mean VWF levels).
4) Hematology consultation placed for diagnostic interpretation and disposition. Plan for additional specialized testing (VWF multimer analysis ± RIPA) at a specialized coagulation laboratory with on-site processing to reduce preanalytical error and clarify subtype if initial panel abnormal.
5) Counseling: explained that VWF is an acute-phase reactant and levels can be elevated by stress/illness; recommended repeat testing once clinically stable to confirm baseline. Genetic testing not required for initial diagnosis of suspected type 1 VWD.
6) Avoid NSAIDs/antiplatelet agents pending hematology recommendations (patient instructed verbally).
7) Admit to monitored bed for observation.

Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
Date/time: Hospital day 2

Interval history:
Overnight and through today patient has had no recurrent epistaxis or new mucosal bleeding. Reports persistent heavy menses consistent with baseline. No transfusions given. Tolerating oral intake.

Exam:
Stable vitals. HEENT: no active nasal bleeding. Skin: persistent scattered ecchymoses. Cardiopulmonary exam unchanged.

Labs/results:
CBC: Hgb 9.2 g/dL, platelets 238 K/µL.
Coagulation: PT/INR normal, APTT 41 sec (mildly prolonged).
VWF panel returned: VWF antigen 0.32 IU/mL (low), RiCof activity 0.30 IU/mL (low). Activity/antigen ratio ~0.94 (proportionate decrease). Factor VIII level 0.40 IU/mL (mildly reduced). ABO group O reported.
Interpretation by hematology: quantitative reduction in VWF with proportionate RiCof activity consistent with type 1 von Willebrand disease phenotype. Factor VIII reduction expected given VWF carrier role. Given blood group O, baseline VWF levels may be lower; however current values and bleeding history support diagnosis of VWD type 1. Hematology recommends confirmatory specialized testing (multimer analysis and repeat VWF testing when patient is clinically at baseline) at a coagulation lab with immediate on-site processing to minimize preanalytical error.

Assessment/Plan:
1) Diagnosis: Probable von Willebrand disease, type 1 (quantitative VWF deficiency) — supported by low VWF antigen and RiCof activity with proportionate reduction and compatible bleeding history.
2) Acute bleeding controlled after desmopressin in ED; no further hemostatic therapy required.
3) Continue observation. Repeat CBC in AM. No transfusion indicated at current hemoglobin.
4) Hematology plan: arrange outpatient VWF multimer analysis and possible RIPA at specialized lab; repeat baseline VWF antigen/RiCof/factor VIII outside of acute stress to confirm. Genetic testing deferred (not required for initial type 1 diagnosis).
5) Counseling reinforced: desmopressin is effective for minor bleeding and recommended pre-procedural prophylaxis; patient to notify clinicians of VWD for perioperative planning. Provided written instructions per hematology (documented in chart).
6) Anticipate discharge tomorrow if stable and no recurrent bleeding.

Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
Date/time: Hospital day 3

Interval history:
No recurrent epistaxis, no new mucocutaneous bleeding. Menstrual bleeding ongoing at baseline heavy rate. Patient reports understanding of diagnosis and instructions.

Exam:
Vitals normal. HEENT: nares clear. Skin: ecchymoses unchanged. No signs of active bleeding.

Labs:
CBC: Hgb 9.4 g/dL, platelets 240 K/µL.
Coagulation: PT/INR normal, APTT 40 sec.
No additional hemostatic interventions required.

Assessment/Plan:
1) Clinical course stable; bleeding controlled after single desmopressin administration on admission.
2) Hematology concurs with discharge with outpatient confirmatory testing and follow-up.
3) Plan discharge today with diagnosis of probable von Willebrand disease, type 1. Provide patient with instructions regarding use of desmopressin for minor bleeding and as perioperative prophylaxis; advise that VWF levels can be affected by blood group O and by physiologic stress and that definitive baseline testing will be arranged outpatient.
4) Schedule: outpatient hematology appointment arranged within 2 weeks; VWF multimer and repeat antigen/RiCof/factor VIII to be performed at specialized coagulation laboratory with on-site processing. Family members (mother) advised to consider evaluation given hereditary pattern of types 1/2 (autosomal dominant).
5) Activity: routine. Return precautions given for recurrent or heavy bleeding.

Discharge Summary
Admission dates: Hospital day 0–3

Reason for admission:
Acute anterior epistaxis in setting of lifelong mucocutaneous bleeding and menorrhagia.

Hospital course:
28-year-old female admitted for control of epistaxis and evaluation for bleeding diathesis. On admission, APTT mildly prolonged; CBC demonstrated Hgb 9.5 g/dL and normal platelet count. Desmopressin administered in ED with cessation of nasal bleeding. VWF studies obtained. Over 48 hours patient remained hemodynamically stable without recurrence of epistaxis. Hematology consulted.

Key diagnostic results:
- VWF antigen 0.32 IU/mL (low)
- VWF ristocetin cofactor activity 0.30 IU/mL (low)
- Activity/antigen ratio ~0.94 (proportionate decrease)
- Factor VIII 0.40 IU/mL (mildly reduced)
- ABO blood group O
- PT/INR normal; APTT mildly prolonged at ~40–42 sec
Findings consistent with quantitative deficiency of VWF and clinical phenotype compatible with von Willebrand disease type 1.

Diagnosis at discharge:
Probable von Willebrand disease, type 1 (quantitative VWF deficiency) — confirmed by low VWF antigen and RiCof activity with proportionate reduction and compatible bleeding history.

Discharge medications/interventions:
- Desmopressin recommended for use in minor mucosal bleeding and as perioperative prophylaxis per hematology (patient provided instructions; dosing and administration to be coordinated with hematology for outpatient use).
- No blood products administered during admission.

Follow-up and recommendations:
1) Outpatient hematology appointment arranged in 1–2 weeks for repeat VWF antigen, RiCof, factor VIII testing at clinical baseline and for VWF multimer analysis/RIPA at a specialized coagulation laboratory with on-site processing.
2) Family members, particularly first-degree relatives, advised to consider evaluation given likely autosomal dominant inheritance of types 1/2 VWD.
3) Patient counseled that VWF is an acute-phase reactant; repeat testing when not stressed or febrile is important for definitive classification. Blood group O may lower baseline VWF levels and will be considered in interpretation.
4) Seek urgent care/return to ED for recurrent or heavy bleeding, syncope, or signs of anemia.

Provider:
Hematology consulted; primary team managing discharge and outpatient arrangements.

Admission Note
Chief complaint: Oliguria and lightheadedness x 3 days.

History of present illness:
72-year-old man with history of hypertension on lisinopril presents with 3 days of watery diarrhea and decreased oral intake. Patient reports progressive lightheadedness, weakness, and markedly decreased urine output over the last 24 hours. No fever, no dysuria, no flank pain, no hematuria. No recent exposure to radiographic contrast. Takes lisinopril daily; denies recent NSAID use per patient. Baseline outpatient serum creatinine one month ago 1.0 mg/dL.

Vital signs:
T 36.7 C, HR 102 bpm, BP 96/60 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 98% RA.

Physical exam:
General: alert, mildly ill-appearing.
HEENT: dry mucous membranes.
Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no gallop.
Lungs: clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Abdomen: soft, non-tender.
Extremities: no peripheral edema.
GU: no suprapubic fullness, no palpable bladder.
Skin: no rash.

Relevant data on arrival:
Serum BMP: Na 136 mEq/L, K 5.2 mEq/L, Cl 100 mEq/L, HCO3 20 mmol/L, BUN 45 mg/dL, creatinine 2.2 mg/dL (baseline 1.0 mg/dL).
Urine output: <0.3 mL/kg/h over first 6 hours in ED.
Urinalysis: specific gravity elevated, no hematuria, no significant protein, bland sediment.
Urine electrolytes: urine sodium 12 mEq/L; FENa calculated ~0.4%.
EKG: sinus tachycardia, no peaked T waves, no acute ischemic changes.
Renal ultrasound (STAT): both kidneys normal size, no hydronephrosis.

Assessment / Plan:
Primary diagnosis: Acute kidney injury — prerenal etiology (volume depletion) complicated by ACE inhibitor use. Meets KDIGO/RIFLE criteria for Injury (creatinine >2.0 x baseline).
Supporting data: rise in serum creatinine from baseline 1.0 to 2.2 mg/dL within 48 hours; elevated BUN/Cr ratio; low urine sodium and low FENa; bland sediment; no hydronephrosis on renal ultrasound; history of decreased oral intake and diarrhea; hypotension on exam.
Complications to monitor for: hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, progression to intrinsic injury/ATN if not reversed.

Initial management:
- Hold lisinopril immediately.
- IV isotonic crystalloid bolus 1 L normal saline now, then reassess; consider additional boluses and maintenance infusion titrated to urine output and hemodynamics.
- Insert straight Foley for accurate I&O and hourly urine output monitoring.
- Cardiac monitoring for hyperkalemia; serial BMP q6h initially.
- Avoid NSAIDs and other nephrotoxins.
- If no improvement after appropriate volume resuscitation or if evidence of intrinsic renal injury, obtain nephrology consult and consider further evaluation (urine microscopy, consideration of biopsy if indicated).
- Dialysis only if refractory hyperkalemia, volume overload, severe acidosis or uremia develop.

Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
Clinical status:
Patient received initial 1 L NS bolus in ED and an additional 1 L overnight with maintenance fluids started. Subjective improvement in lightheadedness. Vitals: BP 110/68 mmHg, HR 86 bpm, afebrile.

Intake/output:
Urine output improved to 1.2 mL/kg/h over the past 12 hours after fluids and Foley placement.

Labs:
BMP 08:00 — Na 134, K 4.6, Cl 100, HCO3 22, BUN 30, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL. Trend demonstrates improving renal function and lowering BUN. No hyperkalemia on repeat labs. EKG unchanged.

Urine studies:
Urinalysis remains bland; no RBC casts. Urine sodium rose compared with admission.

Assessment:
Acute kidney injury due to prerenal azotemia from volume depletion, now improving with volume resuscitation. No evidence of obstructive (postrenal) cause on ultrasound. No signs of intrinsic renal disease at this time.

Plan:
- Continue isotonic fluids; reduce infusion rate to keep euvolemic and target urine output ≥0.5 mL/kg/h.
- Continue hold on lisinopril; document to withhold ACE inhibitor until volume replete and creatinine returns to baseline.
- Continue cardiac monitoring until K stable and <5.0.
- BMP q12h while improving, then daily.
- No nephrology consult required at this time given clinical improvement, but will obtain if creatinine plateaus/worsens or sediment changes.
- Patient education regarding avoidance of NSAIDs and need to hold ACE inhibitors during volume depletion.

Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
Clinical status:
Patient ambulatory on unit floor, reports resolution of dizziness. Vitals stable: BP 122/74 mmHg, HR 78 bpm.

Intake/output:
Urine output steady at 0.8–1.0 mL/kg/h after fluids; Foley removed with adequate spontaneous voiding.

Labs:
BMP 07:30 — Na 136, K 4.2, Cl 102, HCO3 24, BUN 18, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (near baseline). Electrolytes normalized.

Exam:
Euvolemic on exam, mucous membranes moist, no orthostatics.

Assessment:
Acute kidney injury (prerenal) due to dehydration with likely ACE inhibitor contribution — renal function returned to baseline with appropriate volume repletion. No evidence of intrinsic or postrenal injury. No dialysis required.

Plan:
- Discontinue IV fluids; encourage oral hydration.
- Continue to hold lisinopril. Discuss with outpatient PCP regarding rechallenge once euvolemic and creatinine remains stable; consider resumption only with close monitoring.
- Advise strict avoidance of NSAIDs.
- Arrange outpatient labs: BMP in 3–7 days and earlier if symptoms recur.
- Provide education on sick-day medication management (hold ACE inhibitors during acute volume loss/illness) and when to seek care (oliguria, syncope, palpitations suggesting hyperkalemia).
- Plan discharge later today if stable.

Discharge Summary
Admission date: Hospital Day 1
Discharge date: Hospital Day 3
Admitting diagnosis: Acute kidney injury — prerenal azotemia secondary to volume depletion in setting of ACE inhibitor use.
Hospital course summary:
- 72-year-old man with HTN on lisinopril presented with 3 days of diarrhea, hypotension, and oliguria. Admission labs: creatinine 2.2 mg/dL (baseline 1.0), BUN 45 mg/dL, K 5.2 mEq/L. Urine studies consistent with prerenal physiology (low urine sodium, low FENa, bland sediment). Renal ultrasound showed no hydronephrosis. Treated with isotonic crystalloid resuscitation, Foley placement for accurate I&O, cardiac monitoring, serial BMPs, and immediate holding of lisinopril. Renal function improved to creatinine 1.1 mg/dL by hospital day 3, urine output normalized, electrolytes normalized. No evidence of intrinsic kidney disease or obstruction; dialysis not required. Discharged in stable condition.

Discharge diagnoses:
1) Acute kidney injury, prerenal (volume depletion + ACE inhibitor effect) — resolved to baseline.
2) Hypertension — lisinopril held.

Discharge medications and instructions:
- Continue home medications except lisinopril held. Do not take ACE inhibitor until follow-up with PCP or nephrology and repeat BMP showing stable creatinine.
- Avoid NSAIDs.
- Maintain oral hydration; increase fluid intake during illnesses with vomiting/diarrhea and seek medical care early.
- Return to ED for decreased urine output, syncope, severe weakness, chest pain, or palpitations.

Follow-up:
- BMP with PCP or lab in 3–7 days; earlier if symptoms recur.
- PCP follow-up within 1 week to reassess antihypertensive regimen and decision on restarting ACE inhibitor. Consider nephrology referral if creatinine fails to remain stable or worsens.

Prognosis:
AKI from prerenal azotemia has resolved with volume repletion. Patient advised that AKI increases risk of future chronic kidney disease and to avoid nephrotoxins and maintain hydration during intercurrent illnesses.

Admission Note — ICU
HPI:
68-year-old male with COPD and chronic hypertension (on metoprolol) admitted for acute exacerbation of COPD with hypercapnic respiratory failure requiring endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation prior to transfer to this ICU. No history of liver disease. No known drug allergies. Indication for ICU sedation: ventilator synchrony and facilitation of weaning. Plan to use dexmedetomidine infusion as primary sedative to minimize respiratory depression and to potentially reduce duration of mechanical ventilation and delirium risk.

Physical exam:
Gen: intubated, sedated, afebrile.
VS: on arrival — BP 142/78 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, RR ventilator-controlled, SpO2 95% on FiO2 0.40, PEEP 5 cmH2O.
HEENT: ETT in place, pupils equal, reactive.
Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs.
Lungs: diminished breath sounds bilaterally with diffuse wheeze.
Abdomen: benign.
Neuro: sedated, withdraws to noxious stimuli.
Extremities: no edema.

Assessment and Plan:
1) Acute hypercapnic respiratory failure due to COPD exacerbation — intubated and mechanically ventilated.
   - Sedation: start dexmedetomidine IV infusion for light sedation to facilitate ventilator synchrony and expedite weaning. Rationale: alpha-2 agonist sedation with minimal respiratory depression; may reduce time to extubation and neurocognitive dysfunction compared with other sedatives.
   - Start without a loading dose to reduce risk of hypotension/bradycardia. Initial infusion 0.2 mcg/kg/hr, titrate q30–60 min to clinical effect; anticipated range 0.2–0.8 mcg/kg/hr.
   - Monitor continuous ECG, BP q15 min until stable. Avoid bolus dosing owing to transient arrhythmia/blood pressure effects described.
   - Hold home metoprolol while on dexmedetomidine given potential for enhanced bradycardic and hypotensive effects with concomitant agents that lower HR/BP.
   - Obtain baseline BMP, LFTs (dexmedetomidine metabolized hepatically; use caution if abnormal).
2) Ventilator management: lung-protective strategy, assess readiness for spontaneous breathing trial when clinically appropriate.
3) Analgesia: opioid PRN for pain; note dexmedetomidine has some analgesic effects and may reduce opioid requirements.
4) Monitoring: serial neuro checks for agitation/delirium; minimize benzodiazepines/propofol if possible given dexmedetomidine adjunctive benefit.
5) Family updated regarding plan and expected short-term use of dexmedetomidine for sedation.

Hospital Day 2 — ICU Progress Note
Subjective: No new complaints (intubated).
Objective:
- Sedation: on dexmedetomidine infusion. Current rate 0.6 mcg/kg/hr overnight.
- Vitals this AM: BP ranged 88–120 systolic overnight after uptitration; HR 56–76 bpm. SpO2 96% on FiO2 0.35.
- Cardiac monitoring: sinus bradycardia to 56 bpm noted; no high-grade AV block or other arrhythmia on telemetry.
- LFTs returned within reference range.
Assessment:
1) Sedation with dexmedetomidine — overall effective for light sedation and ventilator synchrony. No respiratory depression observed (ventilatory parameters stable, oxygenation adequate).
2) Hemodynamics — transient hypotension (SBP nadir 88 mmHg) and bradycardia after uptitration to 0.8 mcg/kg/hr. This is consistent with known biphasic BP effect and HR reduction associated with drug, and more likely when combined with AV nodal slowing agents.
Plan:
- Decrease dexmedetomidine infusion to 0.5 mcg/kg/hr; reassess q30–60 min.
- IV bolus 250 mL crystalloid given when SBP 88 mmHg; responded with SBP to 110 mmHg.
- Continue to hold metoprolol while on infusion.
- Continue continuous telemetry; if persistent bradycardia or hypotension recur, further decrease or discontinue infusion and consider alternative sedation strategy.
- Assess readiness for spontaneous breathing trial later today if hemodynamics and ventilatory parameters stable.
- Continue routine ICU labs; repeat LFTs if clinical change.

Hospital Day 3 — ICU Progress Note
Subjective: N/A (patient awake, following simple commands once sedation lightened).
Objective:
- Sedation: dexmedetomidine infusion decreased overnight to 0.2 mcg/kg/hr and stopped this morning.
- Vitals: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, SpO2 97% on FiO2 0.30.
- Neurologic: awake, follows simple commands, no agitation or signs of delirium.
- Respiratory: passed spontaneous breathing trial this AM; weaned to minimal pressure support and extubated to supplemental oxygen by nasal cannula.
- Cardiac monitoring: no arrhythmia, no AV block.
Assessment:
1) Successful wean and extubation after light sedation with dexmedetomidine. No respiratory depression observed after infusion; patient arousable and cooperative after stopping infusion consistent with terminal half-life of drug (drug has elimination half-life ~2–3 hours).
2) Hemodynamics stable off infusion. No recurrent hypotension or bradycardia after discontinuation.
Plan:
- Transfer from ICU to step-down unit later today for continued pulmonary care and monitoring.
- Resume home antihypertensive (metoprolol) tomorrow morning if clinically stable and BP tolerated, given prior hold while on dexmedetomidine.
- Continue inhaled bronchodilator therapy, pulmonary toilet, and incentive spirometry.
- No benzodiazepines started; minimize additional sedatives given recent course.
- Follow-up with pulmonology and primary care arranged.

Discharge Summary (Hospital course 3 days)
Admission diagnosis: Acute COPD exacerbation with hypercapnic respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation.
Hospital course summary:
- Day 0: Intubated for respiratory failure; admitted to ICU. Initiated dexmedetomidine IV infusion (no loading dose) for light sedation to facilitate ventilator synchrony and reduce need for other sedatives. Baseline LFTs normal.
- Day 1–2: Dexmedetomidine titrated to achieve sedation goals. Patient experienced transient hypotension and bradycardia after uptitration consistent with known biphasic BP response and HR reduction associated with the drug; infusion rate reduced and brief crystalloid given with good response. Metoprolol held while on infusion because dexmedetomidine can enhance effects of agents that lower heart rate and blood pressure.
- Day 3: Dexmedetomidine infusion discontinued; patient aroused and underwent spontaneous breathing trial and successful extubation. No respiratory depression noted at any time. No arrhythmias or AV block observed on telemetry. Hemodynamics stable off infusion.
Disposition and follow-up:
- Transferred to floor and discharged home on hospital day 3 in stable condition with outpatient follow-up arranged with pulmonology and primary care.
Medications on discharge:
- Resume home medications including metoprolol (to be restarted as above) — instructed to resume with primary care follow-up.
- Continue inhaled bronchodilators per home regimen and as directed by pulmonology.
Instructions/precautions:
- Educated patient and family regarding ICU course and use of dexmedetomidine for short-term sedation; explained potential transient effects on blood pressure and heart rate and rationale for holding beta blocker during infusion.
- Return precautions for worsening dyspnea, chest pain, syncope, or altered mental status.
Provider signature: Attending physician, ICU team.

1. Admission Note
HPI:
72-year-old male with 3 months of progressive fatigue, exertional dyspnea, and easy bruising. Reports several episodes of nonpurulent upper respiratory infection in prior 2 months. No overt GI or intracranial bleeding. Past medical history notable for non-Hodgkin lymphoma treated with cyclophosphamide-containing chemotherapy and localized radiation 6 years ago. No tobacco or occupational benzene exposure reported. Symptoms prompted clinic CBC showing pancytopenia; admitted for workup and transfusion.

Physical exam:
VS: afebrile, HR 88, BP 128/72, RR 16, SpO2 98% RA.
General: pale, fatigued.
HEENT: conjunctival pallor.
Lungs: clear.
Cardiac: regular, no murmur.
Abdomen: soft, non-tender, no hepatosplenomegaly on palpation.
Skin: scattered ecchymoses on forearms, no petechial bleeding.
Neuro: nonfocal.

Initial data:
CBC: Hgb 7.8 g/dL, MCV 102 fL, WBC 2.0 x10^9/L, ANC 0.8 x10^9/L, platelets 42 x10^9/L.
Peripheral smear: dysplastic neutrophils, macrocytosis, thrombocytopenia; no circulating blasts identified.
Basic metabolic panel within baseline; LFTs normal.
Plan obtained for bone marrow biopsy and cytogenetics.

Assessment/Plan:
- Working diagnosis: myelodysplastic syndrome (likely therapy-related given prior alkylating agent exposure) presenting with symptomatic anemia, neutropenia, and thrombocytopenia.
- Immediate: transfuse packed red blood cells ×2 units for symptomatic anemia. Hold invasive procedures until post-transfusion. Platelet transfusion not given (no active major bleeding; platelets >20–30k).
- Infection risk: neutropenia with mild URI symptoms — start empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics per neutropenic protocol while cultures pending; implement neutropenic precautions.
- Diagnostic: schedule bone marrow biopsy with cytogenetics and flow cytometry to determine blast percentage and chromosomal abnormalities.
- Hematology consult placed. Discussed treatment options including supportive care (transfusions, medications to stimulate erythropoiesis), hypomethylating agents (azacitidine/decitabine), lenalidomide, and potential for allogeneic stem-cell transplant (not likely candidate given age/comorbidities). Plan to initiate hypomethylating agent after marrow results reviewed.
- Counseling: discussed risk of progression to acute myelogenous leukemia (~30%) and long-term risk of transfusional iron overload; will monitor ferritin and consider iron chelation if indicated.
- Patient consented to plan.

2. Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
Clinical course:
- Patient received 2 units PRBCs on admission evening. Post-transfusion Hgb 9.6 g/dL.
- Remained afebrile overnight; reported improved dyspnea on exertion.
- No bleeding events.

Labs/Diagnostics:
- Bone marrow biopsy performed this morning.
- Preliminary marrow report: hypercellular marrow with trilineage dysplasia, increased apoptosis, myeloblasts 8% (below threshold for AML). Flow cytometry consistent with myelodysplasia.
- Cytogenetics sample sent; results pending.
- Blood cultures obtained on admission; patient remained afebrile while cultures incubate.

Assessment/Plan:
- Continue neutropenic precautions. Continue empiric antibiotics pending culture results; will reassess at 48 hours if afebrile and cultures negative.
- Hold platelet transfusion; monitor for bleeding. Give platelet transfusion if active bleeding or platelets fall <10–20 x10^9/L per protocol.
- Hematology recommends initiation of hypomethylating therapy; will await cytogenetics but plan to begin azacitidine inpatient tomorrow given symptomatic cytopenias and marrow findings.
- Continue supportive measures: transfuse PRBCs as needed. Monitor ferritin; plan baseline ferritin and serial monitoring.
- Discussed with patient the likely diagnosis of therapy-related MDS given prior cyclophosphamide exposure and marrow morphology; prognosis and treatment options reviewed again. Patient agreeable to start azacitidine.

3. Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
Clinical course:
- Patient started first cycle of azacitidine this morning per hematology protocol (initiation discussed with patient). Tolerated infusion without immediate adverse effects.
- Afebrile, hemodynamically stable, no new symptoms.
- Platelets stable at 38 x10^9/L, WBC 1.9 x10^9/L, ANC 0.7 x10^9/L, Hgb 9.2 g/dL.
- Blood cultures have no growth at 48 hours; antibiotics discontinued after discussion with infectious disease and hematology.

Labs/Diagnostics:
- Cytogenetics returned: deletion involving chromosome 5 detected (loss of chromosome 5 region). No cytogenetic evidence of chromosome 7 loss or 11q23 translocation identified.
- This cytogenetic abnormality is consistent with an alkylator-associated pattern in the setting of prior therapy.

Assessment/Plan:
- Diagnosis confirmed: myelodysplastic syndrome (therapy-related) with multilineage dysplasia, 8% blasts, cytogenetic abnormality (loss of chromosome 5).
- Continue azacitidine; patient to receive remaining outpatient cycles per hematology (first dose given inpatient). Discussed goals: improve blood counts, reduce transfusion dependence, and delay progression to AML. Reviewed potential role of lenalidomide and immunosuppressive therapy for certain subtypes; will consider based on response and additional risk stratification.
- Allogeneic stem-cell transplant discussed as potentially curative but not recommended at this time due to age and comorbidity profile; palliative/definitive options to be revisited if younger donor/fitness changes.
- Plan for discharge tomorrow with hematology follow-up within 1 week for continued azacitidine administration and transfusion planning.
- Counseling provided regarding transfusion-related iron overload; baseline ferritin sent and plan for serial monitoring with consideration of iron chelation if chronic transfusion required.

4. Discharge Summary
Admission diagnosis:
- Myelodysplastic syndrome (therapy-related) with symptomatic pancytopenia.

Hospital course:
- Admitted for evaluation and management of symptomatic anemia, neutropenia, and thrombocytopenia.
- Received 2 units PRBCs with improvement in hemoglobin from 7.8 to 9.6 g/dL.
- Bone marrow biopsy demonstrated hypercellular marrow with trilineage dysplasia and 8% myeloblasts (below WHO threshold for AML).
- Cytogenetics demonstrated loss of chromosome 5.
- First cycle of hypomethylating therapy (azacitidine) administered inpatient and tolerated without immediate complications.
- Empiric antibiotics initiated for neutropenia on admission; blood cultures showed no growth at 48 hours and antibiotics were discontinued.
- No bleeding complications; platelets remained stable without transfusion requirement during stay.
- Hematology consulted; diagnosis and treatment plan discussed with patient.

Discharge condition:
- Afebrile, hemodynamically stable, improved energy, no active bleeding. Hgb 9.2 g/dL, WBC 1.9 x10^9/L, platelets 38 x10^9/L.

Discharge medications and instructions:
- Continue outpatient hypomethylating therapy (azacitidine) as scheduled with hematology infusion clinic. First outpatient infusion appointment arranged.
- Supportive measures: return for PRBC transfusion if symptomatic anemia recurs; immediate evaluation for fever or signs of infection.
- Monitor for bleeding; seek urgent care for new or worsening bleeding.
- No routine antimicrobial prophylaxis prescribed on discharge (per ID/hematology consensus) given afebrile status; will reassess if neutropenic complications occur.

Follow-up:
- Hematology clinic follow-up in 1 week for ongoing therapy and transfusion planning.
- CBC monitoring prior to each azacitidine cycle; ferritin baseline obtained; will monitor serially and consider iron chelation if chronic transfusion dependence develops.
- Discussed prognosis including risk of progression to acute myelogenous leukemia (~30% lifetime risk) and average survival estimates; goals of care and transplant candidacy reviewed. Patient declines aggressive transplant evaluation at this time given age and preferences.

Disposition:
- Home with family support. Hematology to coordinate ongoing care.

Admission Note — Hospital Day 1

HPI:
48-year-old female with 18 months of progressive lower urinary tract symptoms presenting with acute worsening of suprapubic pain and urinary frequency. Symptoms: constant suprapubic pressure/pain worse with bladder filling, frequent daytime voids (~hourly), nocturia x2–3, urinary urgency, intermittent burning with voiding, and dyspareunia. Symptoms have not responded to prior short courses of antibiotics prescribed outpatient. No fever, gross hematuria, or systemic symptoms. Past history notable for IBS and depressive symptoms. Non-smoker. No prior urologic surgery. Patient reports intermittent stress-related flares and worsening quality of life. Prior urinalyses previously reported “no growth.” She requests definitive evaluation.

Physical exam:
Vitals: afebrile, hemodynamically stable.
Abdomen: soft, nondistended. Suprapubic tenderness to palpation without rebound.
Pelvic: external genitalia without lesions. Mild pelvic floor tenderness on exam. No adnexal masses. No costovertebral angle tenderness.
Neurologic: grossly nonfocal.

Assessment:
Likely interstitial cystitis / bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS) — chronic suprapubic pain, urinary frequency/urgency, nocturia, dyspareunia; history of prior negative cultures and lack of response to antibiotics. Differential includes overactive bladder, recurrent UTI/occult infection, endometriosis (less likely given exam), and bladder malignancy (to be excluded). IC is a diagnosis of exclusion per guidelines.

Plan:
- Admit for evaluation and pain control; proceed with diagnostic workup per AUA guidance.
- Urinalysis and urine culture obtained now. Expect culture to be negative; will await final.
- Start analgesia: acetaminophen + ibuprofen scheduled with PPI for gastric protection.
- Begin medical therapy for suspected IC: start oral pentosan polysulfate and low-dose amitriptyline at night for neuropathic/modulatory benefit.
- Trial of H1 antihistamine nightly given mast cell/histamine hypothesis.
- Plan diagnostic cystoscopy under anesthesia with bladder hydrodistention tomorrow to evaluate for mucosal inflammation/Hunner lesions and to perform therapeutic hydrodistention; will consider intravesical GAG-replenishing instillation and local treatment of any Hunner lesion found.
- Do not start long-term antibiotics; Kegel exercises not recommended.
- Provide education on lifestyle measures: dietary triggers, smoking cessation (patient not a smoker), stress reduction, and referral for psychological support as needed.
- Discuss possible future options if refractory: bladder-focused procedures, nerve stimulation, or surgical options.
- Consent obtained for cystoscopy with hydrodistention.

Hospital Day 2 — Progress Note

Events since admission:
Patient underwent cystoscopy under anesthesia with bladder hydrodistention earlier today.

Operative findings/procedures:
- Cystoscopy: bladder capacity at hydrodistention was reduced. Focal mucosal ulceration identified on posterior bladder wall consistent with a Hunner lesion. Diffuse mucosal erythema and glomerulations noted.
- Performed therapeutic hydrodistention. Local cauterization/fulguration of the Hunner lesion performed. Instillation of intravesical GAG-replenishing solution (single instillation at procedure) administered via catheter at end of procedure.

Post-procedure course:
- Patient recovered from anesthesia without complication.
- Immediate reduction in suprapubic pain reported compared with pre-procedure baseline (subjective improvement).
- No hematuria beyond expected mild postoperative spotting. Urine culture pending.

Medications:
- Continued acetaminophen + ibuprofen with PPI.
- Continued oral pentosan polysulfate and nightly amitriptyline.
- Continue nightly H1 antihistamine.
- No antibiotics started; will only start if culture positive or concern for infection.

Assessment/Plan:
- Confirmed diagnosis: Interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome with Hunner lesion identified on cystoscopy.
- Therapeutic hydrodistention and focal fulguration completed; intravesical GAG instillation given.
- Continue current medical regimen. Monitor voiding pattern and pain scores.
- Encourage bladder diary; begin education on dietary triggers and stress management.
- Discussed potential next steps if symptoms recur: repeated intravesical therapy, course of nerve stimulation (neuromodulation) or surgical options. Referral planned to outpatient pelvic pain/urology clinic for consideration of neuromodulation if needed.
- Await urine culture results.

Hospital Day 3 — Progress Note

Clinical status:
- Urinary frequency decreased modestly; patient reports less severe suprapubic pain and improved sleep last night compared with pre-procedure baseline.
- Voiding diary inpatient shows increased voided volumes with fewer voids per day compared with admission.
- No fever, hemodynamically stable. No worsening hematuria.

Laboratory:
- Urine culture returned: no growth.
- Other inpatient labs stable; no leukocytosis.

Assessment/Plan:
- Diagnosis reaffirmed: IC/BPS with Hunner lesion. Culture-negative course supports nonbacterial IC.
- Pain controlled on current regimen. No indication for antibiotics.
- Continue oral pentosan polysulfate and nightly amitriptyline. Continue H1 antihistamine trial.
- Continue PRN acetaminophen/ibuprofen with PPI for gastric protection. Advise to limit NSAID duration and use PRN.
- Arrange discharge and outpatient follow-up with urology in 2 weeks; referral to pelvic pain/urogynecology for consideration of long-term neuromodulation and multidisciplinary care including psychological support. Provide patient education materials on dietary modification and stress reduction strategies.
- Patient counseled that Kegel exercises and long-term antibiotics are not recommended for IC.
- Discharge planning: patient tolerating oral intake, pain adequately controlled, able to ambulate and void.

Discharge Summary

Admission diagnosis:
- Interstitial cystitis / bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS) with Hunner lesion.

Hospital course:
- Admitted for evaluation of chronic suprapubic pain, urgency/frequency and dyspareunia refractory to prior outpatient measures.
- Urinalysis/culture obtained on admission; culture final: no growth.
- Diagnostic cystoscopy with bladder hydrodistention performed on hospital day 2. Findings: reduced bladder capacity, focal Hunner lesion on posterior wall, diffuse mucosal erythema and glomerulations.
- Therapeutic hydrodistention performed with focal fulguration of Hunner lesion and single intravesical GAG-replenishing instillation.
- Post-procedure course uncomplicated. Patient reported symptomatic improvement in pain and frequency. No evidence of infection.
- Multimodal medical therapy started and continued: oral pentosan polysulfate, low-dose nightly amitriptyline, trial of nightly H1 antihistamine, scheduled acetaminophen + ibuprofen with PPI for gastric protection as needed.

Discharge condition:
- Improved compared with admission; pain controlled on oral regimen, voiding frequency reduced, ambulatory, afebrile.

Discharge medications:
- Pentosan polysulfate, oral — continue as outpatient.
- Amitriptyline, low-dose nightly — continue.
- H1 antihistamine nightly — trial.
- Acetaminophen and ibuprofen as needed for pain; take with prescribed PPI for gastric protection.
- No antibiotics prescribed.

Follow-up and recommendations:
- Urology clinic follow-up in 2 weeks (earlier if symptoms worsen or hematuria/fever develop).
- Referral to pelvic pain/urogynecology for multidisciplinary management and consideration of neuromodulation if symptoms persist.
- Recommendations: lifestyle modifications (identify and avoid dietary triggers, stress reduction techniques, psychological support referral). Smoking cessation counseling provided (patient not a smoker).
- Counseling provided that long-term antibiotics and Kegel exercises are not recommended for IC.
- Educated patient on chronic nature of IC, potential for recurrence, and additional procedural options (repeat intravesical therapy, nerve stimulation, or surgery) if refractory.

Problem list on discharge:
1. Interstitial cystitis / bladder pain syndrome with Hunner lesion — treated with hydrodistention, fulguration, intravesical GAG instillation; outpatient pentosan polysulfate and amitriptyline.
2. Chronic pelvic pain and urinary frequency — improved; follow-up arranged.
3. IBS and depressive symptoms — coordination with primary care/behavioral health as outpatient.

Patient verbalized understanding of diagnosis, treatment, and follow-up plan.

1) Admission Note
HPI:
28-year-old female, non-smoker, PMH notable for motion sickness, underwent elective laparoscopic ovarian cystectomy earlier today under general endotracheal anesthesia. Intraoperative course reported use of volatile anesthetic technique with nitrous oxide and intraoperative opioids for analgesia. In PACU developed persistent nausea with several episodes of emesis/retching in first hour post-op and inability to tolerate PO. No prior hospital admissions. No known drug allergies.

Risk assessment:
Apfel risk factors present: female, non-smoker, history of motion sickness, anticipated postoperative opioid use → Apfel score 4 (estimated PONV risk ~80%). Noted that use of volatile anesthetic and intraoperative opioids increased risk; TIVA with propofol would have reduced risk but was not used.

Physical exam:
Vitals: stable, afebrile. General: mildly distressed by nausea. HEENT: moist mucosa. Lungs: clear to auscultation. Heart: regular, no murmur. Abdomen: soft, surgical sites clean, minimal tenderness. Neuro: alert, no focal deficit. No signs of dehydration beyond transient tachycardia not present.

Assessment / Plan:
Primary problem: Postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV), high-risk profile.
Plan:
- Immediate pharmacologic therapy: IV ondansetron and IV dexamethasone administered in PACU (ondansetron + dexamethasone is first-line combination therapy).
- Optimize intravascular volume: administer additional IV crystalloid bolus to address perioperative fasting and reduce PONV risk.
- Implement opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia: scheduled acetaminophen and IV/PO NSAID as tolerated; limit additional opioids.
- Monitor for recurrent emesis; if persistent or refractory, consider second-line agents targeting alternate receptors (phenothiazine such as prochlorperazine; antihistamine such as diphenhydramine) or placement of scopolamine patch (note adverse effects: dry mouth, blurry vision; avoid handling patch transfer to eye).
- Consider neurokinin-1 receptor antagonist (aprepitant) if nausea/vomiting remain refractory despite above measures.
- Avoid droperidol if prolonged QT or if EKG concern due to QT prolongation risk.
- NPO until nausea controlled; attempt small clear liquids once symptoms improve.
- Continue routine post-op monitoring and surgical care.

2) Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
Subjective:
Patient reports initial improvement after ondansetron + dexamethasone but had recurrence of nausea and one episode of vomiting overnight when attempting oral liquids. Denies severe abdominal pain, no fever.

Objective:
Vitals stable. Exam: abdomen soft, surgical incisions intact. Mild dry mucous membranes. No focal neuro findings.

Assessment:
Refractory PONV despite initial standard prophylaxis and fluid optimization. Continued high-risk features present.

Plan:
- Administered prochlorperazine for persistent nausea (phenothiazine effective for opioid-associated PONV). Informed patient about potential sedation and extrapyramidal symptoms; monitoring in place.
- Placed scopolamine transdermal patch behind ear for longer-acting anticholinergic antiemetic effect (patient is young; counselled on dry mouth/visual effects and patch handling precautions).
- Continued additional IV crystalloid supplementation to ensure intravascular volume optimization.
- Continued opioid-sparing analgesia: scheduled acetaminophen and NSAID; held PRN opioids unless severe breakthrough pain.
- If symptoms persist despite above (ongoing vomiting or inability to tolerate PO), plan to administer aprepitant (NK1 antagonist) given multimodal pharmacotherapy targeting different emetic pathways.
- Continue NPO status until tolerating clear liquids; reassess for PO tolerance later today.

3) Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
Subjective:
Patient reports marked improvement in nausea after addition of prochlorperazine and scopolamine patch. Able to tolerate clear liquids this morning and advanced to soft diet without vomiting. Pain controlled on scheduled acetaminophen and NSAID; minimal opioid requirement.

Objective:
Vitals within normal limits. Tolerating oral intake. Surgical wounds clean. No neurologic deficits. No extrapyramidal symptoms observed.

Assessment:
PONV resolving with multimodal therapy (ondansetron + dexamethasone initially, followed by prochlorperazine and scopolamine patch) and optimization of fluids and analgesia. No need for NK1 antagonist at this time.

Plan:
- Continue scopolamine patch in place per manufacturer duration; counsel patient on patch-care and potential side effects.
- Continue scheduled acetaminophen and NSAID for analgesia; avoid routine opioids to minimize recurrence of PONV.
- Provide oral ondansetron PRN for breakthrough nausea if needed.
- Advance diet as tolerated. Plan discharge today if stable and tolerating PO with controlled pain and no recurrent emesis.

4) Discharge Summary
Admission diagnosis: Postoperative nausea and vomiting following elective laparoscopic ovarian cystectomy.

Hospital course:
28-year-old female with history of motion sickness, non-smoker, underwent laparoscopic ovarian cystectomy under general anesthesia with volatile anesthetic and intraoperative opioids. Developed significant PONV in PACU. Apfel score 4 (female, non-smoker, history of motion sickness, postoperative opioid use) indicating high risk (~80%). Initial management included IV ondansetron and IV dexamethasone and supplemental IV crystalloids. Due to recurrence, received prochlorperazine and had a scopolamine transdermal patch placed. Opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia (scheduled acetaminophen and NSAID) implemented; opioid use minimized. No droperidol was used given QT considerations. Patient improved, tolerated PO, and had no further emesis on hospital day 3.

Discharge condition:
Stable, tolerating soft diet, nausea controlled, pain controlled with non-opioid regimen.

Discharge medications/instructions:
- Continue scheduled acetaminophen and NSAID per surgical team instructions for analgesia; avoid routine opioids when possible.
- Ondansetron oral as needed for breakthrough nausea.
- Keep scopolamine patch in place as instructed; avoid touching eyes after handling patch; expect possible dry mouth or transient visual blurring.
- Monitor wound for signs of infection; standard post-op wound care.
- Follow up with surgical team in 7–10 days or sooner if concerns.
- Return to ED for persistent vomiting, inability to tolerate PO, uncontrolled pain, fever, or wound concerns.

Risk factor counseling:
Explained that patient had multiple risk factors for PONV (female sex, non-smoking status, history of motion sickness, perioperative opioid exposure, and use of volatile anesthetics), and that a TIVA approach with propofol and further opioid-sparing measures may reduce risk in future procedures.

1. Admission Note
- Chief complaint: Diffuse petechial rash, new-onset gum bleeding and epistaxis x 2 days.
- HPI: 38-year-old female with 2 days of spontaneous bruising and petechiae on bilateral lower extremities and trunk. Noted bleeding from gums after tooth brushing and intermittent epistaxis requiring pressure. Denies trauma, melena, hematuria, heavy menses, or neurologic symptoms. No recent heparin exposure, quinine use, new medications, or herbal supplements. Reports a mild viral-type upper respiratory illness ~10 days prior that resolved. No personal or family history of bleeding disorders. No constitutional symptoms other than fatigue. No prior history of thrombocytopenia.
- Vitals on arrival: T 36.8°C, HR 92, BP 118/72, RR 16, SpO2 99% RA.
- Physical exam: Awake, comfortable. Conjunctiva without icterus. Oral mucosa: petechiae and small hematomas on buccal mucosa; active oozing from gingiva. Nose: small anterior clot, no brisk arterial bleed. Skin: diffuse petechiae on bilateral lower extremities and scattered ecchymoses on arms and trunk. No lymphadenopathy. Abdomen soft, non-tender, no organomegaly; spleen not palpable. Neuro exam nonfocal.
- Initial labs: CBC notable for platelet count 6,000/µL (confirmed on repeat ED specimen). Hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL, WBC 7.4 x10^3/µL. Peripheral smear: marked thrombocytopenia with presence of large platelets, no schistocytes identified. Coagulation studies (PT/PTT) within reference range.
- Assessment/Plan:
  1) Presumptive immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) presenting with severe thrombocytopenia and mucosal bleeding. Rationale: isolated severe thrombocytopenia, mucosal bleeding, large platelets on smear, absence of splenomegaly, no other cytopenias.
  2) Immediate management for severe bleeding and platelets <10,000/µL:
     - Start high-dose IV corticosteroid (methylprednisolone IV) for immunosuppression.
     - Administer intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) given active mucosal bleeding and very low platelet count.
     - Transfuse platelets (single-donor platelet transfusion) to temporize active bleeding while awaiting response to immune therapies.
  3) Diagnostic evaluation to exclude secondary causes: order HIV and hepatitis C serologies (per guidelines), review medication history for quinine/heparin, consider peripheral smear (done), and defer bone marrow biopsy unless no response or age >60 or diagnostic uncertainty.
  4) Do not perform bleeding time (not recommended). Monitor CBC q6–12h initially and observe for recurrent bleeding. Admit to monitored bed.
  5) Blood bank: patient is Rh-positive. Anti‑D considered for Rh-positive with functional spleen but withheld at this time given response plan and potential hemolytic risk; discuss if inadequate response.
  6) Hematology consulted for management and follow-up planning.
- Procedures: Platelet transfusion and IVIG administered in ED prior to transfer to floor.

2. Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
- Subjective: Patient reports improvement in gum bleeding and no further epistaxis overnight. No new bruising. Tolerating oral intake.
- Vitals: stable.
- Exam: Oral mucosa with decreased oozing. Petechiae persist but no expansion of ecchymoses. No splenomegaly.
- Labs: Platelet count this morning 34,000/µL. Hemoglobin stable at 12.4 g/dL. WBC unchanged. Repeat peripheral smear: thrombocytopenia improving with large platelets.
- Assessment/Plan:
  1) ITP with good initial response to IV corticosteroid + IVIG and platelet transfusion. Platelet count rose from 6,000 to 34,000/µL and bleeding has abated.
  2) Continue IV → transition plan: continue corticosteroid therapy and plan to transition to oral prednisone when stable. No further platelet transfusion indicated at present given rising counts and absence of bleeding.
  3) HIV and HCV serologies pending. Review of medications unchanged—no quinine/heparin exposure identified.
  4) Anti-D therapy still an option for Rh-positive patients; hold given clinical improvement and consideration of hemolysis risk. Discussed with hematology; will reserve anti‑D for inadequate response.
  5) Counsel patient on bleeding precautions. Continue CBC monitoring q12–24h. No bone marrow biopsy indicated at this time given brisk response and age <60.
  6) Plan: continue current therapy, hematology to follow daily. If platelets ≥50,000/µL and stable without bleeding, consider discharge with oral steroid taper and outpatient hematology follow-up.

3. Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
- Subjective: Patient asymptomatic regarding bleeding. Denies headaches, abdominal pain, melena, hematuria.
- Vitals: stable.
- Exam: No active mucosal bleeding. Petechiae fading. No new ecchymoses. Spleen not palpable.
- Labs: Platelet count 78,000/µL. Hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL. HIV and HCV serologies returned negative.
- Assessment/Plan:
  1) ITP with excellent hematologic response to IVIG + corticosteroid; count now 78,000/µL and no bleeding.
  2) Transitioned to oral prednisone with plan for gradual taper. Explained high relapse risk with steroid reduction (60–90% may relapse during taper).
  3) Discharge planning: patient education on bleeding precautions, return precautions for signs of bleeding or neurologic symptoms. Arrange hematology clinic follow-up in 1 week. Discussed second-line options should relapse occur or if thrombocytopenia becomes chronic, including thrombopoietin receptor agonists, rituximab, and splenectomy; these were discussed as possible future therapies if refractory to first-line measures.
  4) No bone marrow biopsy indicated at this time given response and negative initial evaluation for secondary causes.
  5) Discharge medications: oral prednisone taper (dosing and taper plan recorded in discharge instructions). No anti‑D administered. No further IVIG planned inpatient.

4. Discharge Summary
- Admission date: Hospital Day 1. Discharge date: Hospital Day 3.
- Admission diagnosis: Severe immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) with mucosal bleeding.
- Course in hospital: Patient presented with isolated severe thrombocytopenia (platelets 6,000/µL) and mucosal bleeding. Peripheral smear showed large platelets without schistocytes. Coagulation studies normal. Given active bleeding and platelet count <10,000/µL, patient received IV corticosteroid therapy, IVIG, and platelet transfusion with hematology involvement. Rapid clinical and laboratory response was observed: platelets rose to 34,000/µL by hospital day 2 and 78,000/µL by hospital day 3, with cessation of mucosal bleeding. HIV and HCV serologies were negative. No evidence of splenomegaly or other cytopenias. No new exposures to medications associated with secondary thrombocytopenia identified.
- Discharge condition: Stable, no bleeding, platelet count 78,000/µL.
- Discharge medications/instructions:
  - Prednisone oral with taper schedule (provided to patient).
  - Instructions on bleeding precautions (avoid NSAIDs, avoid contact sports, use soft toothbrush, report any bleeding, neurologic symptoms, or large bruises).
  - Return precautions: persistent or recurrent bleeding, hematuria, melena, severe headache, focal neurologic deficits.
- Follow-up:
  - Hematology clinic appointment in 1 week for platelet check and management of steroid taper. Contingency plan discussed if platelet count declines: consideration of thrombopoietin receptor agonist therapy, rituximab, anti‑D (if appropriate), or splenectomy for refractory disease.
- Prognosis/education: Discussed that many patients respond to initial therapy but relapse is common during steroid reduction (60–90% risk). Emphasized need for outpatient monitoring and prompt re-evaluation if bleeding recurs.

1. Admission Note
   HPI:
   - 14-year-old male with sudden onset severe left testicular pain beginning ~2 hours prior to arrival. Pain woke him from sleep. Associated nausea and one episode of emesis. No antecedent trauma. No dysuria, frequency, or fever. No prior similar episodes. No past medical history of note. No known testicular masses or cryptorchidism in history.

   Physical Exam:
   - General: Alert, uncomfortable from pain.
   - Vitals: afebrile, hemodynamically stable.
   - Abdomen: soft, nondistended, mild lower abdominal tenderness without guarding.
   - Genitalia/scrotum: left hemiscrotum erythematous, warm, and markedly tender. Left testicle noted to be higher than expected and oriented more horizontally. Elevation of the left testicle worsened the pain. Right testicle normal in position and nontender. No external skin laceration or palpable mass. No urinary findings on exam.

   Assessment & Plan:
   - Impression: Acute left testicular torsion, high clinical suspicion based on classic sudden unilateral testicular pain, high-riding horizontal left testis, and associated nausea/vomiting. Time of onset ~2 hours — within window for salvage.
   - Plan:
     1. Immediate IV opioid analgesia for pain control.
     2. Attempt manual detorsion in ED to restore blood flow if feasible.
     3. Notify urology for urgent surgical exploration and definitive management (detorsion ± orchiopexy).
     4. Proceed to operating room emergently; given classic presentation and short symptom duration, no delay for preoperative imaging.
     5. Consent obtained for surgical exploration with possible orchiopexy or orchiectomy depending on intraoperative viability.

2. Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
   - Post-op day 0 → now in-house post-op day 1.
   - Operation (prior evening): Emergent scrotal exploration by urology. Intraoperative findings: torsion of left spermatic cord with compromised blood flow; testis returned to viable appearance after surgical detorsion. Bilateral orchiopexy performed for fixation. No orchiectomy required.
   - Post-op course overnight: Hemodynamically stable. Pain controlled with IV opioid PRN and scrotal support. No nausea/vomiting after procedure. Scrotal dressing clean, minimal swelling. No signs of wound infection or ongoing ischemia.
   - Exam today: Afebrile, vitals stable. Left scrotal swelling and tenderness improving. Right scrotum without abnormality.
   - Plan:
     1. Continue analgesia as needed with transition to oral opioids planned when tolerating PO.
     2. Scrotal support and elevation.
     3. Monitor wound, neurovascular status of testes, and urine output.
     4. Early ambulation and advance diet as tolerated.
     5. Anticipate discharge tomorrow if stable and pain controlled; arrange urology outpatient follow-up.

3. Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
   - Post-op day 2.
   - Subjective: Patient reports significant improvement in pain; tolerating oral intake; no further emesis.
   - Objective: Afebrile, stable vitals. Scrotal edema present but decreasing. Surgical site clean and dry. Testicular position appropriate bilaterally; no erythema beyond expected postoperative change.
   - Assessment: Uncomplicated postoperative course following emergency detorsion and bilateral orchiopexy for left testicular torsion. Testis remained viable intraoperatively; no orchiectomy required.
   - Plan:
     1. Discharge today with short course oral opioid for breakthrough pain and instructions for scrotal support and limited activity.
     2. Wound care instructions and signs/symptoms to return for (increasing pain, fever, wound drainage).
     3. Urology clinic follow-up in 1–2 weeks.
     4. Counseling provided re: prognosis: favorable given prompt detorsion within 6 hours; discussion of potential long-term risks (uncertain impact on fertility, theoretical risks including reperfusion injury and immune-mediated effects) and reassurance that bilateral fixation performed to reduce recurrence risk.

4. Discharge Summary
   - Admission diagnosis: Acute left testicular torsion.
   - Hospital course:
     - Presented with sudden severe left testicular pain with nausea/vomiting ~2 hours after onset.
     - In ED received IV opioid analgesia and urgent surgical consultation.
     - Taken emergently to OR for scrotal exploration. Surgical detorsion of the left testis restored viability. Bilateral orchiopexy performed. No orchiectomy required.
     - Postoperative course uncomplicated. Pain controlled; patient ambulated and tolerated oral intake. No signs of wound infection or persistent ischemia.
   - Condition at discharge: Stable, pain markedly improved, surgical wounds clean and dry.
   - Discharge medications: Short course oral opioid analgesic for breakthrough pain (use as directed).
   - Discharge instructions:
     - Continue scrotal support and elevation. Avoid strenuous activity and heavy lifting until cleared by urology.
     - Return to ED for recurrent or worsening scrotal pain, fever, or wound drainage.
     - Urology follow-up in 1–2 weeks.
   - Prognosis: Good, given timely detorsion and fixation within the 6-hour salvage window. Counseling provided regarding possible but uncertain long-term effects on fertility and rare complications (reperfusion injury, immunologic effects); bilateral orchiopexy performed to decrease recurrence risk.

Admission Note
HPI: 38-year-old female admitted postoperatively following laparoscopic appendectomy earlier today for acute appendicitis. In PACU she received an opioid analgesic for acute postoperative pain and subsequently became somnolent with shallow respirations. Naloxone was administered in PACU with prompt improvement in level of consciousness and respiratory rate. Transferred to the surgical ward for observation and pain control. Patient reports ongoing incisional pain, currently 7/10. No history of chronic opioid therapy, no history of substance use disorder reported.

Physical exam: Vitals stable on arrival. General: somnolent but arousable to voice, appropriate conversation when awake. HEENT: pupils reactive. Lungs: clear to auscultation, breathing spontaneously. Cardiovascular: regular rate and rhythm. Abdomen: laparoscopic incision sites clean, soft, mild tenderness RLQ without distension. Neuro: grossly intact; no focal deficits. Skin: surgical dressings intact.

Assessment & Plan:
1. Acute postoperative pain s/p laparoscopic appendectomy. Received opioid in PACU with transient opioid-induced respiratory depression reversed with naloxone. Plan: minimize further opioid exposure; institute multimodal analgesia using scheduled acetaminophen and an NSAID (ibuprofen) as first-line for acute pain. Reserve short-acting opioid for breakthrough pain at the lowest effective dose with close nursing monitoring for sedation and respiratory depression. Avoid co-prescription or co-administration of benzodiazepines given increased risk of respiratory depression.
2. Observation after naloxone reversal. Continuous respiratory and mental status monitoring for 24 hours on ward; pulse oximetry per nursing protocol. Naloxone available at bedside; avoid routine repeat opioid doses without documented assessment.
3. Bowel function monitoring. Educate regarding risk of opioid-induced constipation; will treat if constipation develops (naloxegol is available as specific therapy for opioid-induced constipation).
4. Patient education re: risks of opioids — sedation, respiratory depression, constipation, tolerance and physical dependence with longer-term use — and plan for short-duration opioid therapy only.
5. DVT prophylaxis and routine postop orders per surgery team.

Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
Subjective: Pain better controlled overnight with scheduled acetaminophen and ibuprofen. Used one rescue short-acting opioid dose for breakthrough pain early morning; no recurrent somnolence. Complains of decreased bowel movements since surgery; mild constipation.

Objective: Vitals stable. Awake and interactive. Respirations normal. Abdomen: incisions clean, minimal tenderness. Ambulating with assistance.

Assessment & Plan:
1. Postoperative pain — improved on multimodal regimen. Continue scheduled acetaminophen and ibuprofen. Continue rescue short-acting opioid only for severe breakthrough pain; limit frequency and lowest effective dose. Monitor mental status and respiratory rate after any opioid administration.
2. Opioid-induced constipation — initiated naloxegol for symptom management with instruction to report stool frequency and character. Continue to encourage oral intake and mobilization.
3. Observation: no recurrence of opioid-induced respiratory depression since PACU event. Continue regular monitoring and avoid sedating co-medications.
4. Counseling reinforced about avoiding long-term opioid use; explained risk of tolerance and physical dependence if opioids are continued beyond the acute period.

Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
Subjective: Patient reports satisfactory pain control on scheduled non-opioid regimen. No further need for rescue opioid overnight. Bowel movements have resumed since initiation of naloxegol.

Objective: Vitals stable. Alert and oriented x3. Ambulating independently. Surgical sites clean and dry. No signs of respiratory compromise.

Assessment & Plan:
1. Pain control adequate with acetaminophen + NSAID; no additional opioid doses required in the last 24 hours. Given clinical improvement and absence of ongoing severe pain, plan to discontinue in-hospital rescue opioid use.
2. Opioid-induced constipation resolved after naloxegol; will discontinue naloxegol prior to discharge and advise as PRN outpatient option if constipation recurs while taking opioids.
3. No further episodes of sedation or respiratory depression after initial PACU event.
4. Discharge planning: short, limited-duration outpatient opioid prescription only if needed for breakthrough pain, explicit instructions to use acetaminophen/NSAID first, avoid benzodiazepines, monitor for sedation/respiratory symptoms, and to stop opioid as soon as pain is controlled. Arrange surgical clinic follow-up in 1 week and primary care follow-up for pain reassessment if opioids required beyond the immediate postoperative period.

Discharge Summary
Admission diagnosis: Acute appendicitis; status post laparoscopic appendectomy.

Hospital course:
- Patient underwent laparoscopic appendectomy earlier same day. In PACU received an opioid analgesic for acute postoperative pain and developed opioid-induced respiratory depression (somnolence, shallow respirations) which promptly reversed after administration of naloxone.
- Transferred to the floor for observation. Transitioned to multimodal analgesia consisting of scheduled acetaminophen and an NSAID (ibuprofen). Received one brief rescue dose of a short-acting opioid on hospital day 2 for breakthrough pain without recurrent sedation.
- Developed opioid-associated constipation shortly after surgery; treated with naloxegol with return of bowel function.
- No further episodes of respiratory depression. Pain controlled on non-opioid regimen by hospital day 3. Patient ambulatory, tolerating diet, incision sites healing.

Discharge condition: Stable. Pain controlled with non-opioid analgesics. Ambulating independently. Bowel function present.

Discharge medications and instructions:
- Acetaminophen: scheduled for pain as directed.
- Ibuprofen: scheduled for pain as directed (take with food).
- Limited, short-duration prescription for a short-acting opioid for breakthrough pain only (use only if acetaminophen/NSAID ineffective). Use lowest effective dose and discontinue as soon as pain controlled.
- Do NOT combine opioid with benzodiazepines or other sedating medications due to increased risk of respiratory depression.
- If excessive sleepiness, difficulty breathing, or inability to awaken occurs, seek immediate medical attention. Naloxone reversed prior event in hospital; emergency care is required for recurrent overdose.
- If constipation recurs while using opioids, contact provider; naloxegol is an effective targeted therapy for opioid-induced constipation.

Follow-up:
- Surgical clinic follow-up in 1 week.
- Primary care follow-up as needed for pain management and reassessment if opioid therapy is required beyond the immediate postoperative period.

Counseling provided regarding risks of opioids including sedation, respiratory depression, constipation, tolerance and physical dependence with longer-term use; advised preferred reliance on acetaminophen and NSAIDs and reassessment at follow-up.

1) Admission Note
HPI:
4-year-old previously healthy male brought by parents for 2 weeks of progressive fatigue, decreased appetite, intermittent low-grade fever, increasing bruising on extremities, and worsening left leg pain limiting ambulation. No prior radiation or chemotherapy exposure. No known genetic syndromes in family. No recent travel. Parents report several episodes of epistaxis and one week of nightly low-grade fevers.

Physical exam:
General: tired-appearing child, alert, cooperative but reluctant to ambulate.
Vitals: afebrile on arrival, HR and BP within age-appropriate limits, RR normal, O2 sat 98% on room air.
HEENT: conjunctival pallor; no conjunctival hemorrhage.
Neck: multiple small, mobile cervical lymph nodes palpable (~1–2 cm).
Cardiac: regular, no murmurs.
Lungs: clear to auscultation.
Abdomen: palpable hepatomegaly and splenomegaly (liver and spleen edge palpable).
Musculoskeletal: tenderness to palpation of left femur without erythema or swelling.
Skin: scattered petechiae on lower extremities and ecchymoses on arms.
Neurologic: no focal deficits; no neck stiffness; cranial nerves grossly intact.

Assessment / Plan:
Impression: Suspected acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) based on history of fatigue, pallor, fever, bruising, bone pain, lymphadenopathy, and hepatosplenomegaly.

Immediate orders and plan:
- Obtain STAT CBC with differential, peripheral blood smear, coagulation studies, basic metabolic panel, and uric acid.
- Obtain blood cultures given intermittent fevers.
- Arrange urgent bone marrow aspiration/biopsy for definitive diagnosis and cytology.
- Initiate contact isolation precautions per institutional protocol.
- Admit to pediatric oncology service for diagnostic workup and initiation of therapy.
- If bone marrow confirms ALL, plan initiation of multi-agent induction chemotherapy aimed at remission and intrathecal chemotherapy for CNS prophylaxis.
- Baseline lumbar puncture scheduled at time of initial intrathecal therapy to assess CSF for leukemic involvement.
- Discuss case with pediatric hematology/oncology attending; obtain consent from parents for procedures and treatment plan.
- Transfusion support to be made available based on CBC results.

2) Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
Overnight course: Patient remained hemodynamically stable. No fever overnight. Blood cultures pending.

New data:
- CBC: significant anemia and thrombocytopenia; WBC elevated with circulating lymphoblasts on smear.
- Coagulation studies within acceptable range for procedure.
- Bone marrow aspiration/biopsy performed this morning. Preliminary report: marrow packed with lymphoblasts consistent with acute lymphoblastic leukemia.
- Lumbar puncture performed for CSF evaluation; CSF cytology preliminary negative for malignant cells.

Physical exam:
As above; pain improved with acetaminophen and short-term oral analgesic regimen; able to bear partial weight.

Assessment / Plan:
- Diagnosis: Acute lymphoblastic leukemia confirmed by bone marrow examination.
- Initiated multi-agent induction chemotherapy today per pediatric oncology protocol (induction regimen started inpatient). Intrathecal chemotherapy administered at time of lumbar puncture for CNS prophylaxis given high risk of CNS relapse and limited CNS penetration of systemic agents.
- Continue monitoring CBC, metabolic panel, and uric acid. Monitor for treatment-related complications including cytopenias and infectious risk.
- Supportive care: transfusion thresholds and blood product availability discussed; administer PRBC/platelet transfusion if clinically indicated.
- Family counseled regarding diagnosis, treatment goals (induction to achieve remission followed by prolonged therapy), need for frequent outpatient visits, and possible future therapies including radiation if CNS involvement is later identified or stem cell transplant for recurrence.
- Obtain baseline genetic and cytogenetic studies on marrow specimen; results pending and will guide risk stratification and further therapy planning.
- Pain control as needed. Continue oncology nursing care and frequent labs.

3) Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
Clinical course: Patient tolerated induction chemotherapy doses administered on Day 1 without immediate complications. No fever today. Pain controlled on oral regimen. Ambulation improving.

New data:
- Repeat CBC early today unchanged from prior; expected cytopenias ongoing. No evidence of tumor lysis clinically; metabolic profile within normal limits.
- Blood and CSF cultures remain negative at 48 hours.
- Preliminary cytogenetic and molecular studies pending; family informed that results may take several days and will influence subsequent treatment intensity.
- No CNS involvement detected on initial CSF cytology; however, intrathecal chemotherapy will be continued per protocol for CNS prophylaxis.

Physical exam:
Stable from prior exam. Petechiae persist. No new focal neurologic signs.

Assessment / Plan:
- Continue induction chemotherapy per protocol with scheduled doses and monitoring.
- Continue intrathecal chemotherapy per CNS prophylaxis schedule.
- Continue close monitoring of blood counts; transfuse PRBCs or platelets as indicated.
- Infection precautions maintained. Start empiric antimicrobial therapy only if febrile neutropenia develops.
- Social work and child life consults engaged to support family during hospitalization and to coordinate outpatient therapy scheduling.
- Plan to discharge home when clinically stable, afebrile, pain controlled, and family able to comply with outpatient oncology follow-up and labs. Reinforce education about signs of infection, bleeding, and when to seek immediate care.

4) Discharge Summary
Admission diagnosis:
- Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL), newly diagnosed (bone marrow confirmed).

Hospital course:
- 3-day inpatient admission for diagnostic confirmation and initiation of therapy.
- Presentation: 2 weeks of fatigue, pallor, intermittent fevers, easy bruising, bone pain, lymphadenopathy, and hepatosplenomegaly.
- Diagnostic testing: CBC with differential and peripheral smear demonstrated anemia, thrombocytopenia, and circulating lymphoblasts. Bone marrow aspiration/biopsy confirmed marrow infiltration by lymphoblasts consistent with ALL. Lumbar puncture performed; CSF cytology negative for leukemic cells.
- Treatment administered: Multi-agent induction chemotherapy initiated per pediatric oncology protocol. Intrathecal chemotherapy administered at time of lumbar puncture for CNS prophylaxis given risk of CNS relapse and limited CNS penetration of systemic agents.
- Supportive care: Pain control achieved; no transfusions required during this admission. No infectious complications developed; blood and CSF cultures remained negative. Metabolic parameters stable without evidence of tumor lysis.
- Consults: Pediatric hematology/oncology consulted and leading care. Social work and child life provided family support and education.
- Genetics/cytogenetics: Specimens sent; results pending and will inform risk stratification and further therapy planning.

Condition at discharge:
- Clinically stable, afebrile, pain controlled on oral medication, hemodynamically stable, able to ambulate with improvement in leg pain.
- Family verbalized understanding of diagnosis, inpatient course, and outpatient plan. Discharge teaching provided regarding signs of infection, bleeding, and when to present to the ED.

Discharge medications:
- Analgesic as needed (per discharge instructions). No chemotherapy dosing for home; further chemotherapy to be administered in outpatient oncology clinic per protocol.

Follow-up:
- Outpatient pediatric oncology appointment scheduled in 48–72 hours for continuation of induction therapy and lab monitoring.
- Return precautions: fever, new bleeding, worsening pain, difficulty breathing, neurologic changes (headache, vomiting, lethargy, neck stiffness, cranial nerve symptoms) — go to ED immediately.

Prognosis and counseling:
- Family counseled that initial goal is induction of remission with multi-agent chemotherapy followed by prolonged therapy to reduce relapse risk. Intrathecal chemotherapy is being used to reduce central nervous system relapse risk. Discussed that if disease recurs after standard therapy, additional options such as stem cell transplantation are considered. Genetic/cytogenetic results pending and will further guide prognosis and intensity of therapy.

Admission Note
HPI:
34-year-old male referred for evaluation of left scrotal fullness and infertility. Symptoms present for ~18 months: dull ache and sensation of scrotal heaviness, worse after standing and exertion. Reports difficulty conceiving with partner for 18 months. Also notes decreased libido and intermittent fatigue. No history of scrotal trauma, surgery, urinary symptoms, hematuria, or constitutional symptoms.

Physical exam:
General: well-appearing, afebrile.
Genital: Left-sided palpable mass of tortuous veins superior to the left testis consistent with "bag of worms," visible on inspection — clinically grade 3 varicocele (visible without manipulation). Left testicle noticeably smaller than right (>10% volume discrepancy). Right scrotum without varicocele.

Imaging:
Scrotal duplex ultrasound obtained: pampiniform plexus veins on left dilated to ≥3.0 mm with spontaneous reflux at rest that increases with Valsalva. Findings consistent with Sarteschi grade IV varicocele and ipsilateral testicular hypotrophy.

Assessment / Plan:
- Left clinical varicocele (grade 3 / Sarteschi grade IV) with ipsilateral testicular hypotrophy and infertility; symptoms of scrotal heaviness and low libido suggestive of impaired testicular endocrine function.
- Discussed management options: observation vs repair. Indications for repair present (palpable varicocele with abnormal fertility and testicular hypotrophy; symptomatic).
- Plan: microsurgical subinguinal varicocelectomy to ligate dilated veins while preserving the testicular artery and lymphatics to minimize recurrence and hydrocele risk. Patient consented for procedure. Surgery scheduled today.

Hospital Day 2 Progress Note (Post-op day 0)
Procedure performed: Microsurgical subinguinal varicocelectomy, left side.
Intraoperative findings: Multiple dilated veins of the pampiniform plexus identified and ligated. Testicular artery and lymphatic channels identified and preserved. No injury to adjacent structures noted.
Estimated blood loss minimal.

Postoperative course:
- Patient monitored in recovery and returned to ward in stable condition.
- Surgical site: subinguinal incision clean, no active bleeding or expanding hematoma. Scrotal support applied.
- Pain: controlled with routine measures.
- No evidence of acute hydrocele or testicular compromise on bedside assessment.
- Plan: Continue observation overnight, scrotal support, discharge criteria review tomorrow.

Hospital Day 3 Progress Note (Post-op day 1)
Clinical status:
- Patient remains hemodynamically stable, afebrile, ambulating with minimal discomfort.
- Incision clean and dry, dressing intact. No erythema, drainage, or expanding hematoma. Scrotal edema minimal.
- Neurovascular exam of left testis grossly intact.

Assessment / Plan:
- Uncomplicated immediate postoperative course after left microsurgical subinguinal varicocelectomy.
- Continue scrotal support. Advise avoidance of strenuous activity; return to non-strenuous activity in a few days; restrict heavy lifting and contact sports for 1–2 weeks.
- Discussed expected outcomes: improvement in semen parameters may occur over months; in men with preoperative hypogonadism total testosterone has been shown to increase after repair. Advised watch for complications (progressive swelling suggesting hydrocele, severe or worsening pain, signs of wound infection, or neurologic symptoms).
- Plan discharge today with outpatient follow-up in 2 weeks for wound check and counseling regarding fertility follow-up (repeat semen analysis as indicated) and endocrine assessment.

Discharge Summary
Admission diagnosis:
1) Left clinical varicocele (grade 3 / Sarteschi grade IV)
2) Left testicular hypotrophy
3) Infertility — referred for varicocele repair

Procedure:
