Left microsurgical subinguinal varicocelectomy — ligation of dilated pampiniform plexus veins with preservation of testicular artery and lymphatics.

Hospital course:
Patient admitted for elective varicocele repair. Operative course uncomplicated. Multiple dilated spermatic veins were identified and ligated using microsurgical technique. Testicular artery and lymphatic channels preserved. Immediate postoperative course uneventful. No acute complications noted (no hematoma, no hydrocele, wound clean). Pain controlled. Patient ambulated and tolerated activity as tolerated.

Condition at discharge:
Stable for discharge on postoperative day 1.

Discharge instructions:
- Scrotal support in place; continue until follow-up.
- Avoid heavy lifting and strenuous exercise for 1–2 weeks; non-strenuous activity may be resumed within a few days.
- Monitor incision for increasing pain, redness, drainage, progressive scrotal swelling (possible hydrocele), or neurologic symptoms; seek prompt evaluation if these occur.
- Follow-up in 2 weeks for wound check and clinical review. Arrange repeat semen analysis and endocrine evaluation (serum total testosterone) as outpatient per fertility plan; improvements in semen parameters and testosterone can occur over ensuing months after repair.

Expected outcomes and risks:
- Repair performed to address symptoms, testicular hypotrophy, and infertility. Microsurgical subinguinal approach chosen for lowest recurrence and reduced hydrocele risk by preserving lymphatics and artery.
- Counselled regarding possible complications including hydrocele formation and nerve injury, though none observed during hospitalization.

Provider:
Attending physician (de-identified)

Admission Note (HPI, Physical Exam, A/P)
HPI:
62-year-old male, immediate postoperative from coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG), transferred intubated to CVICU for postoperative management. Intraoperatively, while on the ventilator prior to transfer, continuous capnography had shown baseline end-tidal CO2 (PETCO2) ~36 mmHg with a normal Phase III plateau. During mediastinal manipulation there was an abrupt fall in PETCO2 to 8 mmHg accompanied by loss of pulses. Team initiated CPR per ACLS. Continuous capnography was used to confirm endotracheal tube placement and to monitor CPR quality. After two cycles of high-quality CPR there was a sudden rise in PETCO2 to 30 mmHg coincident with return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) and restoration of palpable pulses. The patient was stabilized on the ventilator and transferred to CVICU intubated and sedated. Intraoperative team documented concern for an acute intraoperative circulatory event (air or thromboembolism or abrupt decrease in cardiac output) given the sudden capnographic change.

Physical Exam:
General: Intubated, sedated male on mechanical ventilation.
HEENT: Endotracheal tube in place; taped at 23 cm at teeth.
Lungs: Bilateral breath sounds present, equal.
CV: Heart rate and rhythm supported by infusions; pulses present after ROSC.
Extremities: Warm, no focal deficits noted (limited exam due to sedation).
Neuro: Sedated, not arousable to commands.
Lines: Endotracheal tube, arterial line, central venous access, chest drains as placed by surgical team.

Assessment and Plan:
1) Postoperative status s/p CABG with intraoperative cardiac arrest event detected by capnography (abrupt PETCO2 fall to 8 mmHg) with subsequent ROSC (PETCO2 rise to 30 mmHg). Capnography confirmed ETT placement and provided ongoing monitoring of ventilation and perfusion. Plan: continue continuous capnography and pulse oximetry in CVICU; monitor PETCO2 trends as an indirect measure of cardiac output and ventilation.
2) Mechanical ventilation, sedated and hemodynamically supported. Continue ventilator settings as per anesthesia report; lung-protective strategy. Daily assessment for weaning; use capnography to assess ventilation frequency and adequacy during weaning trials.
3) Hemodynamic monitoring. Maintain arterial and central access; titrate vasoactive support as needed; use PETCO2 changes to alert for acute drops in cardiac output or circuit/airway issues.
4) Concern for intraoperative embolic event versus transient circulatory collapse. Surgical/cardiac team informed. Further diagnostic workup will be directed by surgical team; no immediate imaging ordered by ICU at this time.
5) DVT/PE prophylaxis and other routine postoperative care as per cardiac surgery protocol.
6) Family updated on events and plan.

Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
Summary:
Patient remained intubated, sedated overnight in CVICU. No recurrent loss of pulses. Continuous capnography and pulse oximetry in place.

Clinical status:
- Neuro: Sedated; no new focal deficits evident.
- Respiratory: On ventilator. PETCO2 trending 32–35 mmHg with preserved Phase III plateau on capnogram. No evidence of disconnection or circuit leak. Oxygenation adequate on FiO2 0.4 per anesthesia/vent settings.
- Cardiovascular: Hemodynamically supported; pulses present. No further episodes of abrupt PETCO2 decrease. Arterial pressures stable on current vasoactive regimen.
- Procedural/lines: ETT position routinely verified; capnography used to reconfirm after any repositioning/suctioning with stable PETCO2 readings.

Assessment and Plan:
1) Post-ROSC postoperative monitoring. PETCO2 has stabilized in low 30s mmHg, consistent with adequate ventilation and improved perfusion compared with intraoperative nadir. Continue continuous capnography and pulse oximetry; alerts in place for abrupt PETCO2 drops (will prompt immediate assessment for airway, ventilator circuit, or circulatory collapse).
2) Continue mechanical ventilation and sedation. Begin daily spontaneous breathing trial assessment tomorrow if PETCO2 and oxygenation remain stable.
3) Continue hemodynamic support and monitoring. Use trends in PETCO2 as adjunct to arterial pressure and cardiac output assessment. Notify team for any sudden PETCO2 decline.
4) Routine postoperative care: DVT prophylaxis, chest tube management, labs per protocol.
5) Family updated regarding stable status and plan for stepwise wean.

Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
Summary:
Patient evaluated for ventilator wean. Continuous capnography continued to guide assessment of ventilation and perfusion.

Clinical status:
- Respiratory: Patient passed spontaneous breathing trial. Prior to extubation, capnography continued to show PETCO2 33–36 mmHg with normal waveform morphology and intact Phase III plateau. Endotracheal tube position reconfirmed by capnography prior to extubation maneuvers and immediately post-extubation via nasal sampling for a short interval to monitor for hypoventilation. Oxygenation stable on supplemental oxygen (pulse oximetry > 95%).
- Cardiovascular: Hemodynamically stable without vasopressor escalation. No recurrent abrupt PETCO2 changes.
- Neuro: Awake and following commands post-sedation washout.

Assessment and Plan:
1) Extubated successfully today after SBT. Immediate post-extubation capnography sampling demonstrated PETCO2 in mid-30s mmHg and regular respiratory frequency, indicating adequate alveolar ventilation. Continue pulse oximetry and supplemental oxygen; monitor clinically and with capnography for hypoventilation during the first 24 hours.
2) Continue cardiac monitoring on stepdown unit once criteria met. Capnography no longer required continuously but will be available for procedural sedation and as needed for respiratory monitoring.
3) Continue routine postoperative care and physiotherapy. Plan for transfer to stepdown unit later today if stable.
4) Family updated; plan for discharge planning pending continued stability.

Discharge Summary
Admission diagnosis: Postoperative management following CABG complicated by intraoperative cardiac arrest detected by capnography with subsequent ROSC.

Hospital course:
Patient is a 62-year-old male who underwent CABG. Intraoperatively he experienced abrupt fall in end-tidal CO2 from ~36 mmHg to 8 mmHg with loss of pulses. CPR was initiated and capnography was used to confirm endotracheal tube position and to monitor CPR effectiveness. After two cycles of CPR there was a sudden rise in PETCO2 to ~30 mmHg consistent with ROSC; palpable pulses and hemodynamic improvement followed. Patient was transferred to CVICU intubated and sedated for postoperative management with continuous capnography and pulse oximetry.

Over the next 48 hours PETCO2 trended steadily to 32–36 mmHg with preserved Phase III capnogram morphology, reflecting restoration of ventilation–perfusion relationships and improved circulatory status. No further abrupt PETCO2 declines occurred. The patient underwent daily assessments; passed a spontaneous breathing trial on hospital day 3 and was extubated. Post-extubation monitoring including brief capnography sampling demonstrated stable PETCO2 and respiratory rate. Hemodynamics remained stable; no recurrent arrhythmias or circulatory collapse documented. The surgical team considered intraoperative embolic event versus transient intraoperative circulatory collapse as possible etiologies; no further acute interventions required in ICU.

Condition at discharge:
- Vital signs stable off vasoactive support.
- Ambulation and oxygenation adequate on room air or minimal supplemental oxygen as per stepdown protocol.
- Neurologic baseline intact.

Discharge instructions and follow-up:
- Follow up with cardiothoracic surgery clinic in 7–10 days.
- Return for any signs of respiratory distress, recurrent syncope, chest pain, or wound concerns.
- Outpatient monitoring and cardiac rehabilitation as arranged.

Clinical notes regarding capnography:
Capnography was instrumental in intraoperative detection of abrupt circulatory collapse (marked fall in PETCO2), in confirming endotracheal tube position, in monitoring CPR effectiveness and in identifying ROSC (sudden rise in PETCO2). Continuous PETCO2 monitoring also guided ventilator weaning and post-extubation assessment of ventilation.

Admission Note — Day 0

HPI:
62-year-old male with acute onset fever, rigors, progressive confusion and weakness over 24 hours. Family reports prior lower abdominal pain and dysuria earlier in day of admission. On arrival noted to be hypotensive and tachycardic. Shortly after presentation developed oozing from peripheral IV sites and new gross hematuria. No history of anticoagulant use. No recent surgery or known malignancy. Working diagnosis: sepsis with acute coagulopathy consistent with disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC).

Physical exam:
VSS: T 38.9 C; HR 112; BP 86/52; RR 22; SpO2 95% RA.
General: ill-appearing, somnolent but arousable.
HEENT: mucosal bleeding noted at gingiva.
Skin: petechiae on bilateral lower extremities; oozing from IV sites.
Lungs: clear to auscultation.
Cardiac: tachycardic, no murmur.
Abdomen: mild suprapubic tenderness.
Extremities: cool periphery.
Neuro: oriented x1, sluggish but moves all extremities.

Initial labs:
CBC: WBC 18.2 K, Hgb 10.8 g/dL, Platelets 28 K/mm3 (marked thrombocytopenia).
Coagulation: PT/INR prolonged (INR 2.3), aPTT prolonged.
Fibrinogen: low at 80 mg/dL.
D-dimer / FDPs: markedly elevated.
Chemistry: Cr 2.0 mg/dL (baseline unknown), lactate elevated.
UA: gross hematuria.

Assessment:
1) Acute disseminated intravascular coagulation, most consistent with acute DIC secondary to sepsis. Laboratory pattern: thrombocytopenia, low fibrinogen, prolonged INR/PT/aPTT, elevated D-dimer/FDPs. Clinical bleeding (mucosal bleeding, hematuria, oozing from IV sites) and hypotension. Pathophysiology consistent with sepsis-related exposure of tissue factor and endotoxin-mediated activation of coagulation with excess thrombin generation, widespread fibrin deposition and consumption of platelets/coagulation factors with secondary fibrinolysis.
2) Septic shock — presumed bacterial etiology as trigger for DIC.
3) Acute kidney injury, likely multifactorial (hypotension, microvascular thrombosis).

Plan:
- Admit to ICU for close monitoring and management of sepsis and DIC.
- Treat underlying sepsis (therapy directed toward underlying condition).
- Blood component therapy for active bleeding and coagulopathy:
  - Platelet transfusion given emergently (1 pooled platelet unit) for platelets 28 K with active bleeding.
  - Fresh frozen plasma administered for correction of prolonged INR and ongoing bleeding.
  - Cryoprecipitate given for fibrinogen replacement.
- Avoid heparin (acute, rapidly developing DIC; heparin more appropriate for slowly developing forms).
- Frequent monitoring: CBC, platelets, fibrinogen, PT/INR, aPTT, D-dimer, renal function, urine output.
- Supportive care for hemodynamics and organ perfusion as needed.
- Communicate prognosis: DIC associated with high morbidity and variable mortality; anticipate need for serial reassessment and possible additional transfusion depending on ongoing bleeding and labs.

Hospital Day 2 — Progress Note

Subjective/Events:
Patient remains in ICU. Bleeding from IV sites decreased after transfusion but hematuria persists. Mental status improving but still somnolent. Remains intermittently hypotensive earlier in course; hemodynamics stabilized overnight with supportive measures.

Objective:
Vitals: T 38.1 C, HR 98, BP 102/64, RR 18.
Labs today:
- Platelets 75 K/mm3 after platelet transfusion.
- Fibrinogen 150 mg/dL after cryoprecipitate.
- INR improved to 1.6 following FFP.
- D-dimer / FDPs remain markedly elevated.
- Creatinine 1.9 mg/dL (slight improvement).
- Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL.

Assessment:
1) Acute DIC, partial laboratory and clinical response to blood component therapy. Improvement in platelets, fibrinogen, INR; ongoing elevated fibrin degradation products consistent with ongoing fibrinolysis and prior widespread clotting.
2) Sepsis with improving hemodynamics; underlying process being managed.
3) AKI — stable to slightly improved.

Plan:
- Continue treatment directed at underlying sepsis.
- Continue close laboratory monitoring every 6–8 hours; repeat platelet transfusion if platelets fall or bleeding recurs.
- Continue cryoprecipitate/FFP as indicated by fibrinogen/INR and bleeding status.
- Do not initiate heparin given acute presentation.
- Monitor for organ dysfunction (renal, respiratory, neurologic) and for new thrombotic events or bleeding.
- Discuss prognosis with family; note that rates of death in DIC are variable and significant.

Hospital Day 3 — Progress Note

Subjective/Events:
Patient more alert, able to answer questions. Hematuria substantially improved. No new bleeding from mucosa or IV sites.

Objective:
Vitals: T 37.6 C, HR 86, BP 118/72, RR 16.
Labs:
- Platelets 115 K/mm3.
- Fibrinogen 220 mg/dL.
- INR 1.3.
- D-dimer/FDPs remain elevated but trending down from admission.
- Creatinine 1.6 mg/dL.
- Hemoglobin 9.5 g/dL.

Assessment:
1) Acute DIC secondary to sepsis — clinically and biochemically improving after directed treatment of underlying condition and blood component therapy (platelets, cryoprecipitate, FFP). Persistent elevated fibrin degradation products reflect prior fibrin formation and ongoing fibrinolysis but improving trend.
2) Sepsis improving with restoration of perfusion and stabilization of vital signs.
3) AKI improving.

Plan:
- Transition care toward step-down once stable.
- Continue to monitor CBC, fibrinogen, coagulation studies daily while inpatient and until normalization or clear downtrend.
- No anticoagulation indicated (acute DIC).
- Arrange continued management of underlying infection and follow-up for renal function.
- Prepare for discharge planning if continued improvement.

Discharge Summary — Hospital Day 4

Admission diagnosis:
- Acute disseminated intravascular coagulation secondary to sepsis.
- Acute kidney injury, likely secondary to sepsis and hypoperfusion.

Hospital course:
Patient presented with fever, hypotension, altered mental status and bleeding (mucosal bleeding, hematuria, oozing from IV sites). Initial laboratory evaluation demonstrated thrombocytopenia, low fibrinogen, prolonged INR/PT/aPTT, and markedly elevated D-dimer/FDPs consistent with acute DIC. Patient admitted to ICU.

Management focused on treating the underlying septic process and supportive correction of coagulopathy/bleeding. Blood component therapy administered in the acute phase: platelet transfusion for thrombocytopenia with active bleeding, cryoprecipitate for low fibrinogen, and fresh frozen plasma for correction of INR in the setting of bleeding. Hemodynamic and organ support provided as needed. Heparin was not used given the acute, rapidly developing nature of the DIC (heparin is described as potentially useful only in slowly developing forms). Over 72 hours the patient demonstrated clinical improvement: cessation of active mucocutaneous bleeding, stabilization of vital signs, and improving mentation. Laboratory trends showed rising platelets (28 K → 115 K), fibrinogen normalization (80 → 220 mg/dL), and correction of INR (2.3 → 1.3). D-dimer/FDPs remained elevated but decreased from peak values.

Discharge condition:
Alert, hemodynamically stable, no active bleeding, urine output adequate, renal function improving though not yet at baseline.

Discharge plan:
- Continue outpatient management of underlying infectious process as arranged by primary team.
- Outpatient follow-up with primary care and hematology recommended for reassessment of coagulation profile and renal function.
- Return precautions: new or recurrent bleeding (mucosal bleeding, hematuria, melena), sudden chest pain, shortness of breath, new focal neurologic deficits, or worsening mental status — all may represent complications of DIC (thrombosis or hemorrhage) and require urgent evaluation.
- Prognosis discussion: DIC is associated with significant morbidity and variable mortality; risk of organ failure and death can be substantial depending on underlying cause and response to treatment.

Final diagnoses:
1) Acute disseminated intravascular coagulation — improved with directed therapy and blood component replacement.
2) Sepsis — presumed source treated during hospitalization.
3) Acute kidney injury — improving.

Medications/procedures given in hospital: platelet transfusion, cryoprecipitate, fresh frozen plasma, supportive care directed at underlying sepsis.

Follow-up appointments:
- Primary care within 48–72 hours.
- Hematology within 1 week for coagulation follow-up.

Admission Note — Day 0
HPI: 68-year-old F with history of recurrent urinary tract infections and type 2 diabetes presents with 48 hours of fever, rigors, right flank pain, dysuria, urinary frequency and urgency, and nausea. Wife reports patient more confused than baseline today. No vomiting, no hematuria reported. Denies indwelling catheter use. Postmenopausal; prior UTIs treated with short courses of oral antibiotics.

Physical exam: T 38.9°C, HR 102, BP 132/78, RR 16, SpO2 97% on room air. Alert but mildly disoriented to date. Abdomen soft, suprapubic tenderness. Right costovertebral angle tenderness to percussion. No suprapubic mass. No skin breakdown.

Labs/diagnostics: CBC WBC 14.2 K/µL with left shift. Basic metabolic panel within baseline for diabetes. Urinalysis: marked pyuria and bacteriuria. Urine culture obtained. Blood cultures obtained given fever/systemic signs.

Assessment/Plan:
- Working diagnosis: acute pyelonephritis (upper urinary tract infection), likely ascending bladder infection given history; E. coli most likely but culture pending.
- Admit for IV antibiotic therapy for complicated/upper UTI given age, diabetes, systemic symptoms and mild confusion.
- Continue monitoring vitals, mental status, urine output.
- Obtain urine culture and blood cultures (already sent).
- Pain control PRN; glycemic control as per inpatient protocol.
- Discussed with patient/family expected course: re-evaluate at 48–72 hours; if no improvement will proceed with further diagnostic testing.
- Counseling re: recurrent UTI prevention options to be reviewed prior to discharge (vaginal estrogen for postmenopausal women and/or consideration of methenamine).

Hospital Day 2 — Progress Note
Subjective: Reports less dysuria and decreased urgency. Fever overnight to 38.1°C once; less malaise. Appetite improved.

Objective: Afebrile this morning. HR 88. BP 128/76. Mental status near baseline. Right CVA tenderness improved. WBC 11.1 K/µL. Urine culture incubation pending; preliminary Gram stain reported gram-negative rods in urine.

Assessment/Plan:
- Clinical improvement on IV antibiotics consistent with appropriate response for pyelonephritis.
- Continue IV antibiotic therapy.
- Continue to monitor culture results and blood cultures.
- Reassess for transition to oral therapy if afebrile and clinically improved for 24–48 hours.
- Reinforce monitoring for worsening: recurrent fever, flank pain, altered mental status.
- Continue diabetic glucose management.

Hospital Day 3 — Progress Note
Subjective: Patient ambulatory, reports minimal dysuria, no flank pain, no fever. Appetite near baseline.

Objective: Afebrile x >36 hours. HR 78, BP 126/72. Mental status at baseline. Right CVA tenderness resolved. WBC 8.9 K/µL. Urine culture final: Escherichia coli isolated. Blood cultures negative to date.

Assessment/Plan:
- Acute pyelonephritis due to E. coli with good clinical response to therapy.
- Patient clinically stable and afebrile; meets criteria to transition from IV to oral antibiotics to complete a longer oral course for complicated UTI.
- Discharge planning: complete total antibiotic course as outpatient (to complete planned duration), follow-up with PCP in 48–72 hours and sooner if recurrent fever or worsening symptoms.
- Preventive measures discussed given history of recurrent UTIs and postmenopausal status: offer trial of topical vaginal estrogen; methenamine may be considered if recurrences persist. Patient agreeable to discuss with PCP/gynecology.
- Provide urine culture result and reinforce return precautions.

Discharge Summary
Admission date: Day 0. Discharge date: Day 3.

Principal diagnosis: Acute pyelonephritis (upper urinary tract infection) — Escherichia coli.

Hospital course: 68-year-old postmenopausal woman with diabetes and history of recurrent UTIs admitted with 2 days of fever, right flank pain, dysuria and confusion. Urinalysis showed pyuria and bacteriuria. CBC showed leukocytosis. Urine culture grew E. coli; blood cultures negative. Patient was treated with intravenous antibiotics with clinical improvement: afebrile >36 hours, resolution of flank pain and normalization of white blood cell count. Transitioned to oral antibiotics to complete outpatient therapy.

Discharge condition: Stable, afebrile, mental status at baseline, pain controlled.

Discharge medications: Oral antibiotic to complete prescribed total course (patient instructed to complete full course).

Follow-up: PCP visit in 48–72 hours for clinical review and culture result discussion. Consider gynecology/PCP discussion regarding vaginal estrogen for prevention of recurrent UTIs; methenamine discussed as alternative if recurrent infections continue.

Return precautions: Return to ED or clinic for recurrent fever, worsening flank pain, new onset vomiting, inability to tolerate oral intake, or new significant confusion.

Provider: Attending physician on service.

1. Admission Note
HPI:
58-year-old male brought from home with 3 days progressive dyspnea, productive cough and subjective fevers. Over the prior 12 hours developed increasing work of breathing and somnolence. On arrival to ED noted to be tachypneic and hypoxemic despite supplemental oxygen.

Physical exam:
General: somnolent but arousable.
Airway: oropharynx patent.
Respiratory: tachypnea with accessory muscle use, diffuse crackles on auscultation.
Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm.
Neuro: GCS approximately 10 (opens to voice, confused, localizes to pain).
Extremities: no edema.

Diagnostics (on arrival):
ABG on FIO2 0.60: PaO2 55 mm Hg, PaCO2 52 mm Hg, pH 7.28.

Assessment and Plan:
- Acute hypoxemic and hypercapnic respiratory failure in the setting of presumed severe pneumonia. Indications for tracheal intubation met: PaO2 <60 mm Hg on FIO2 ≥50% and rising PaCO2 with acidemia.
- Proceeded with orotracheal tracheal intubation after administration of general anesthetic and neuromuscular-blocking drug for airway protection and ventilatory support.
  - Technique: video laryngoscope used to identify vocal cords. Endotracheal tube passed between the vocal cords into the trachea under direct visualization. Balloon cuff inflated just proximal to distal end of tube, tube secured to face. Connected to mechanical ventilator for controlled ventilation.
  - Immediate post-intubation: improved ventilation and oxygenation noted. No evidence of esophageal intubation or aspiration. No dental injury or mucosal laceration observed.
- Plan: continue mechanical ventilatory support, serial ABGs to guide ventilator settings and weaning, daily assessment for readiness to wean/extubate. Keep alternative airway strategies immediately available if needed (including surgical airway). Monitor for complications of intubation (aspiration pneumonitis, tube displacement).

2. Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
Clinical course:
- Patient remains intubated and sedated on mechanical ventilation. No new neurologic decline.
- Endotracheal tube position unchanged; cuff inflated and secured. No signs of cuff leak.
- Respiratory status: FIO2 weaned from 60% to 40% as oxygenation improved.
- ABG on FIO2 0.40: PaO2 78 mm Hg, PaCO2 44 mm Hg, pH 7.36.
- No evidence of aspiration, no visible traumatic injury from intubation.

Assessment and Plan:
- Respiratory failure improving on ventilatory support. Continue current ventilator support with attempts at gradual reduction of FIO2 and support as tolerated.
- Maintain sedation to facilitate ventilator synchrony. Continue routine tube care, securement, and cuff pressure monitoring.
- Reassess for spontaneous breathing trial tomorrow if ABGs and clinical parameters remain stable.
- Keep alternative airway equipment available.

3. Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
Clinical course:
- Patient clinically improved with continued ventilator support. Hemodynamically stable.
- Passed spontaneous breathing trial this morning while intubated.
- Proceeded with extubation: balloon cuff deflated and endotracheal tube removed (tracheal extubation/decannulation of non-surgical airway). Patient observed closely post-extubation for airway patency and adequate ventilation.
- Post-extubation: patient maintaining spontaneous respirations without stridor or signs of airway obstruction. Oxygenation stable on supplemental oxygen by face mask; able to protect airway and clear secretions.

Assessment and Plan:
- Successful extubation with no immediate complications. Continue monitoring for delayed airway compromise or aspiration. Encourage pulmonary toilet and mobilization.
- Plan for step-down of respiratory support and discharge planning if clinical stability persists.

4. Discharge Summary
Admission diagnosis: Acute hypoxemic/hypercapnic respiratory failure due to presumed severe pneumonia requiring airway protection and mechanical ventilation.

Hospital course summary:
- Presented with progressive dyspnea and hypoxemia; ABG on FIO2 0.60 showed PaO2 55 mm Hg, PaCO2 52 mm Hg with acidemia.
- Underwent orotracheal tracheal intubation after administration of general anesthetic and neuromuscular-blocking drug. Video laryngoscope used; endotracheal tube passed between the vocal cords into the trachea under direct visualization. Balloon cuff inflated and tube secured. Connected to mechanical ventilator.
- No immediate complications of intubation (no esophageal intubation, no aspiration, no dental injury or mucosal laceration).
- Over 48 hours oxygenation and ventilation improved; FIO2 weaned to 40% and ABG normalized. Patient passed spontaneous breathing trial and was successfully extubated on hospital day 3. No post-extubation airway obstruction or respiratory compromise observed.

Condition at discharge:
- Respiratory status stable on room air/supplemental oxygen as tolerated; maintaining airway and adequate ventilation without tracheal tube.
- No evidence of complications related to intubation or extubation.

Disposition:
- Discharged home in stable condition with outpatient follow-up arranged.

Procedures during admission:
- Orotracheal endotracheal intubation with cuff inflation and mechanical ventilation (placement confirmed by direct visualization of tube passing between vocal cords). Tracheal extubation performed on hospital day 3.

Complications:
- None identified.

1. Admission Note
HPI:
76-year-old male transferred from clinic for evaluation of progressive fatigue x 3 weeks, episodic nasal and gingival oozing, intermittent blurred vision and headaches, and 8 lb weight loss. Patient reports new distal paresthesias in both feet. No fevers or night sweats. Longstanding history of farming occupation with chronic pesticide exposure. No known personal history of malignancy. No bleeding diathesis previously.

Physical exam:
VS: afebrile, HR 84, BP 132/76, RR 16, SpO2 98% RA.
General: thin, alert, in no acute distress.
HEENT: pale conjunctivae; small amount of gingival oozing; nasal mucosa without active large hemorrhage.
Neck: several small, mobile cervical lymph nodes ~1–2 cm.
Cardiac: regular, no murmurs.
Lungs: clear.
Abdomen: spleen palpated ~3 cm below left costal margin; no hepatomegaly.
Skin: no petechiae, no ecchymoses.
Neuro: intact cranial nerves; decreased vibration sense in bilateral feet; motor strength 5/5.

Initial studies (on arrival):
CBC: Hgb 8.6 g/dL, Hct 26%, WBC 3.9 K/uL, platelets 92 K/uL.
Chemistry: creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, electrolytes within normal limits, total protein 9.8 g/dL, albumin 3.0 g/dL.
SPEP/Immunoglobulins: large monoclonal IgM spike; quantitative IgM 6.5 g/dL. Immunofixation pending.
ESR 78 mm/hr. LDH elevated at 420 U/L.
Cryoglobulins: sample sent.
Coagulation: PT/INR and aPTT within reference range.
Peripheral smear: rouleaux present; no circulating blasts.
Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT ordered.

Assessment and Plan:
76-year-old man with anemia, thrombocytopenia, elevated total protein and a prominent monoclonal IgM spike, presenting with bleeding, visual disturbance and paresthesias — findings most consistent with Waldenström macroglobulinemia (WM) with possible hyperviscosity features. Differential includes other IgM-producing lymphoproliferative disorders and plasma cell dyscrasias; skeletal survey to exclude lytic disease.

Plan:
- Hematology/oncology consult obtained.
- Bone marrow biopsy and aspirate scheduled for tomorrow for morphology, flow cytometry and cytogenetics.
- Order serum immunofixation, quantitative immunoglobulins (complete), beta-2 microglobulin, cryoglobulins, direct antiglobulin, rheumatoid factor, cold agglutinin titer.
- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to evaluate lymphadenopathy and organomegaly.
- Skeletal survey to help distinguish WM from multiple myeloma.
- Monitor closely for worsening visual symptoms, headaches or neurologic change consistent with progressive hyperviscosity; emergent hematology intervention to be arranged if clinical hyperviscosity develops.
- Supportive measures as needed; no disease-directed therapy started inpatient pending definitive diagnosis and multidisciplinary discussion.
- Discussed prognosis with patient: disease is indolent, incurable but treatable; outpatient therapy planning anticipated.

2. Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
Events since admission:
- Bone marrow biopsy and aspirate performed this morning without complication.
- CT chest/abd/pelvis completed: scattered small cervical, axillary and inguinal lymph nodes up to 2.5 cm; moderate splenomegaly (spleen 15 cm). No liver enlargement.
- Skeletal survey completed: no lytic lesions identified.
- Patient remains hemodynamically stable. No progression of visual symptoms or new neurologic deficits. Gingival oozing decreased with local measures.

Laboratory updates:
- Immunofixation: monoclonal IgM confirmed.
- Beta-2 microglobulin elevated.
- Cryoglobulins positive (preliminary).
- Direct antiglobulin test pending.

Assessment and Plan:
Findings continue to support Waldenström macroglobulinemia: symptomatic monoclonal IgM paraprotein with bone marrow sampling pending flow/cytology confirmation. CXCR4 and MYD88 mutation testing sent with marrow specimen. Continue observation for hyperviscosity. Hematology recommends outpatient discussion of treatment options after final pathology and mutation results, given current hemodynamic stability and absence of acute hyperviscosity requiring immediate intervention. Continue monitoring CBC, LDH, and clinical status. Educated patient on return precautions (worsening vision, severe headache, confusion, increased bleeding).

3. Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
Results:
- Bone marrow biopsy pathology: hypercellular marrow with interstitial and nodular infiltrate composed of lymphoplasmacytoid cells and small lymphocytes; morphology consistent with Waldenström macroglobulinemia.
- Flow cytometry (bone marrow): clonal B-cell population expressing CD20, CD22, CD40, CD52 and surface IgM. Findings concordant with WM immunophenotype.
- Molecular testing: MYD88 L265P mutation detected. CXCR4 mutation also detected (report per molecular lab).
- Final labs: Hgb 8.4 g/dL, platelets 88 K/uL, IgM 6.5 g/dL, LDH 430 U/L, ESR 82 mm/hr, beta-2 microglobulin elevated. Coagulation studies final within normal limits.
- Cryoglobulins positive; rheumatoid factor negative; cold agglutinin titer pending.

Clinical status:
Patient remains clinically stable. Visual blurring intermittent but not progressive; no focal neurologic deficits; no new bleeding beyond minimal mucosal oozing controlled with local measures.

Assessment and Plan:
Confirmed diagnosis: Waldenström macroglobulinemia with symptomatic IgM paraproteinemia and positive MYD88 and CXCR4 mutations. Patient does not meet criteria for emergent intervention for hyperviscosity at this time; symptomatic but stable. Discussed diagnosis and prognosis with patient and family: indolent lymphoplasmacytoid/plasma cell B-cell disorder, incurable but treatable; CXCR4 mutation can be associated with symptomatic hyperviscosity and high marrow activity — will factor into therapeutic planning.

Disposition planning:
- No acute inpatient therapy initiated. Hematology recommends expedited outpatient hematology/oncology clinic visit within 3–5 days to review results and select initial treatment strategy.
- Arrange outpatient labs (CBC, CMP, IgM level, LDH) prior to clinic visit.
- Provide close outpatient surveillance plan and clear return precautions for signs of hyperviscosity or hemorrhage.
- Continue local measures for mucosal bleeding; no transfusion given while hemodynamically stable and without active major bleeding.
- Plan discharge tomorrow if stable.

4. Discharge Summary
Admission diagnosis:
- Waldenström macroglobulinemia presenting with symptomatic IgM paraproteinemia (anemia, mucosal bleeding, visual disturbance) and bone marrow involvement.

Hospital course:
- Admitted for evaluation of suspected IgM lymphoproliferative disorder.
- Diagnostic workup completed: CBC, chemistry, SPEP/immunofixation, quantitative immunoglobulins, cryoglobulins, ESR, LDH, beta-2 microglobulin, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, skeletal survey.
- Bone marrow biopsy and aspirate performed; pathology and flow cytometry consistent with Waldenström macroglobulinemia (lymphoplasmacytoid infiltration; clonal B cells positive for CD20, CD22, CD40, CD52, surface IgM).
- Molecular testing: MYD88 L265P mutation detected; CXCR4 mutation detected.
- Patient remained hemodynamically stable during hospitalization. No evidence of acute hyperviscosity syndrome requiring emergent intervention. No transfusions or disease-specific therapy administered inpatient.
- Hematology/oncology consulted and participated in diagnostic interpretation and disposition planning.

Discharge condition:
- Stable for discharge. Vital signs within normal limits. No progressive visual decline or neurologic compromise. Gingival bleeding controlled with local measures.

Discharge instructions and follow-up:
- Follow up with hematology/oncology clinic in 3–5 days for treatment planning (urgent appointment arranged). Patient instructed to bring recent lab results (CBC, IgM level, LDH) to visit.
- Return to ED or contact hematology urgently for any of: worsening visual changes, new or worsening severe headache, confusion, focal neurologic deficits, new or uncontrolled bleeding, syncope, or rapid worsening fatigue.
- Outpatient testing arranged: repeat quantitative IgM, CBC, CMP, coagulation tests, and final cold agglutinin titer as indicated.
- Patient counseled on diagnosis: indolent lymphoplasmacytoid B-cell disorder characterized by monoclonal IgM production; disease is treatable though not curable; treatment options and sequencing to be reviewed by hematology.

Medications at discharge:
- No new systemic hematologic therapies initiated inpatient. Continue home medications (none new).

Primary team:
Hematology/Oncology consulted; primary admitting service coordinated discharge and outpatient handoff.

1. Admission Note
HPI:
- Presented from home with progressive urinary urgency, frequency, nocturia and urge incontinence. Patient reports urinary frequency for several years with recent worsening over 6 months. Currently voiding approximately 10–12 times per day, awakening 2–3 times at night to void, and experiencing 1–3 episodes of involuntary leakage per day associated with sudden urgency. Symptoms interfere with sleep and activities of daily living.
- Denies dysuria, flank pain, or gross hematuria. No fever or systemic symptoms.
- Risk factors: overweight, regular caffeine intake (several caffeinated beverages/day), chronic constipation. History notable for type 2 diabetes (reported as poorly controlled). No prior urologic surgery. No indwelling catheter.

Physical exam:
- General: Alert, comfortable at rest.
- Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no suprapubic fullness on inspection/palpation.
- Cardiopulmonary: Clear, no distress.
- Genitourinary (external): No external skin breakdown. No visible blood.
- Neurologic: Grossly intact per bedside screening; able to ambulate independently.

Assessment/Plan:
Primary diagnosis: Overactive bladder (OAB) — symptomatic urgency, urinary frequency (>7–8/day), nocturia, and urge incontinence consistent with OAB.
Differential: Urinary tract infection, bladder stone, bladder cancer, neurologic causes. UTI and hematuria typically present with pain/hematuria which patient denies; will rule out with testing.
Plan:
- Diagnostics: Urinalysis and urine dip to rule out urinary tract infection and hematuria. Ask patient to begin bladder diary documenting times, volumes (if available), urgency and leakage events, and fluid/caffeine intake.
- Initiate first-line management: behavioral and lifestyle measures — bladder training (scheduled voiding with progressive interval increases), pelvic floor exercises (Kegels) instruction provided, fluid management counseling including reduction of caffeine and moderate overall fluid intake, weight loss counseling and constipation management advice.
- Arrange referral to pelvic floor physical therapy outpatient.
- Discuss pharmacologic options if behavioral measures insufficient. Antimuscarinic agents (e.g., oxybutynin, tolterodine, solifenacin, etc.) and β3 agonists (mirabegron, vibegron) reviewed with patient; risks including common antimuscarinic side effects and possible long-term association with dementia discussed. Decision deferred pending UA results and response to initial behavioral measures.
- If refractory to above after adequate trial, discuss third-line options: tibial nerve stimulation, botulinum toxin injections to the bladder, implantable sacral nerve stimulation devices. Urinary catheters and major surgery generally not recommended.
- Plan close follow-up and reassessment during admission.

2. Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
Status:
- Patient stable overnight. No fever, hemodynamically stable.

Interval history:
- Continued urgency and frequency. Completed first 24 hours of bladder diary; documents 12 voids, 3 nocturia episodes, 2 urge incontinence episodes. Reports adherence to bladder training schedule and limiting caffeine since admission.

Exam:
- Unchanged from admission. No suprapubic tenderness. No new focal neurologic deficits.

Results:
- Urinalysis negative for leukocyte esterase, nitrites, and gross hematuria — no evidence of urinary tract infection on UA.

Assessment/Plan:
- OAB confirmed clinically after UA excluded infection. Continue behavioral measures: reinforce bladder training schedule, pelvic floor exercises instruction. Continue fluid and caffeine reduction counseling; provide written bladder diary template for ongoing use.
- Given symptom burden and patient preference for more rapid symptom control, and after counseling regarding benefits and potential side effects (including dry mouth, constipation, and long-term cognitive risk with antimuscarinics), start pharmacologic therapy with an antimuscarinic agent (chosen agent documented and initiated). Monitor for anticholinergic side effects.
- Arrange outpatient pelvic floor physical therapy appointment prior to discharge.
- Educated patient regarding third-line therapies if inadequate response to conservative and pharmacologic measures.
- Discharge planning in progress assuming continued stability.

3. Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
Status:
- Patient reports modest symptomatic improvement since medication start: urgency less intense, diary today shows 9 voids and 2 nocturia episodes, one fewer incontinence event compared with prior days. Mild dry mouth reported; no constipation or vision changes.

Exam:
- Stable. No suprapubic tenderness. Ambulating independently.

Assessment/Plan:
- Partial clinical response to combined behavioral therapy and antimuscarinic medication. Side effect (dry mouth) currently mild and tolerable.
- Continue current antimuscarinic medication and behavioral program. Emphasize pelvic floor exercises and constipation management to reduce bladder irritation.
- Provide education on long-term risks of antimuscarinics and discuss alternative β3 agonist therapy if side effects become intolerable or if there are concerns about long-term anticholinergic burden.
- Plan discharge today with outpatient follow-up and contingency plans if symptoms recur or worsen.

4. Discharge Summary
Admission diagnosis:
- Overactive bladder (urgency-frequency syndrome with urge incontinence).

Hospital course:
- Admitted for evaluation and management of progressive urinary urgency, frequency (>8/day), nocturia and urge incontinence. Urinalysis obtained on admission was negative for infection and hematuria. Bladder diary recorded demonstrating high daytime frequency and nocturia. Initiated first-line behavioral interventions (bladder training, pelvic floor exercises instruction, fluid and caffeine modification, weight loss and constipation counseling) and arranged outpatient pelvic floor physical therapy. After discussion of risks and benefits, antimuscarinic pharmacotherapy was initiated due to symptom burden and patient preference. Patient had partial symptomatic improvement during hospital stay with decreased frequency and fewer incontinence episodes and reported only mild, tolerable dry mouth.

Procedures/tests:
- Urinalysis: negative for infection/hematuria.
- Bladder diary completed inpatient.

Medications on discharge:
- Continue antimuscarinic agent initiated in hospital (patient instructed on administration and possible side effects). (Medication name recorded in chart.)
- Continue non-pharmacologic measures: bladder training schedule, pelvic floor exercises, caffeine reduction, moderate fluid intake, weight loss strategies, and measures to manage constipation.

Discharge instructions and follow-up:
- Continue bladder diary at home and bring to follow-up.
- Continue pelvic floor exercises as instructed; attend outpatient pelvic floor physical therapy (appointment arranged/recommended).
- Follow up with urology/urogynecology clinic in 4–6 weeks to reassess symptoms and medication tolerance. If inadequate response to conservative measures and medication, plan discussed for further evaluation including urodynamic testing and/or cystoscopy and consideration of third-line therapies (tibial nerve stimulation, botulinum toxin A bladder injections, or sacral nerve stimulation devices).
- Advised that urinary catheters and major surgery are generally not recommended for OAB and reserved for select cases.
- Return precautions: fever, new flank pain, gross hematuria, inability to void, or intolerable medication side effects.

Prognosis:
- OAB is a chronic condition; many patients improve with behavioral therapy and/or medications. Approximately a subset achieve complete dryness on antimuscarinic therapy; long-term management and periodic reassessment will be required.

Admission Note — Day 0
HPI:
72-year-old male found by family choking on solid food (large piece of meat) while eating. Witnessed inability to speak and progressive respiratory distress. Family performed back blows and abdominal thrusts with temporary relief but patient lost consciousness. EMS called. No history of recent trauma; no neck injury suspected at scene.

Prehospital interventions:
- Abdominal thrusts and back blows performed by bystanders prior to EMS arrival.
- On EMS arrival patient was unresponsive with absent effective ventilation. Head-tilt/chin-lift performed (no concern for cervical spine injury).
- Oropharyngeal airway inserted to maintain supraglottic patency.
- Direct laryngoscopy performed with visualization of foreign material in oropharynx/trachea; endotracheal intubation performed (cuffed tracheal tube placed) to secure airway and reduce aspiration risk.
- Mechanical ventilation initiated en route.

ED Arrival vitals:
HR 98, BP 138/82, SpO2 96% on ventilator FiO2 40%, RR controlled by ventilator, T 36.7°C.

Physical exam:
General: Intubated, sedated male on ventilator.
Head/Neck: Endotracheal tube in place, cuff palpated inflated. No cervical step-offs or signs of trauma.
Lungs: Breath sounds present bilaterally, equal, no obvious wheeze. Minimal coarse secretions heard.
Cardiac: RRR, no murmurs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender.
Neuro: sedated; pupils equal and reactive to light.

Assessment and Plan:
Primary problem: Acute airway obstruction due to aspirated/aspirating food bolus with loss of airway protective reflexes → advanced airway needed.
- Airway secured with cuffed endotracheal tube (infraglottic technique) to re-establish gas exchange and reduce ongoing aspiration risk.
- Continue mechanical ventilation and sedation until return of airway protective reflexes.
- Suctioning of oropharynx and ETT as needed to remove particulate material; avoid blind finger sweep given risk of further impaction/aspiration (note: some organizations advocate finger sweep after unconsciousness but modern protocols generally recommend against).
- Monitor for signs of aspiration of gastric contents/blood; maintain cuff pressure to minimize aspiration risk.
- Head of bed elevated to reduce risk of aspiration.
- If difficulty in ventilation or failed intubation, surgical airway (cricothyrotomy/tracheotomy) contingency prepared.
Disposition: Admit to ICU for airway monitoring, mechanical ventilation, and serial reassessment for extubation readiness.

Hospital Day 2 — Progress Note
Status:
- Patient now more alert with light sedation held for assessment. Spontaneous respiratory efforts present on spontaneous breathing trial.
- Cough and gag reflexes returned; able to phonate when ventilator support briefly reduced (indicates patent upper airway when extubated).
- Oropharyngeal suctioning performed twice overnight to clear residual particulate matter.

Exam:
- Awake, following commands.
- Lungs: bilateral breath sounds, improved aeration, minimal secretions after suctioning.
- Endotracheal tube: cuff intact and inflated; tolerated brief cuff deflation trial without desaturation.

Assessment:
- Improving neurologic status and return of airway protective reflexes following resolution of obstruction.
Plan:
- Perform cuff leak assessment and spontaneous breathing trial; if adequate cuff leak and patient tolerates SBT with intact cough/gag, plan extubation to protect airway.
- Continue suctioning PRN.
- Reinforce aspiration precautions post-extubation (keeping head-of-bed elevated, small bites, supervision during meals).
- No cervical spine precautions necessary; head-tilt/chin-lift appropriate if needed in future events.

Hospital Day 3 — Progress Note
Status:
- Extubated successfully this morning after meeting criteria: sustained spontaneous ventilation, intact cough/gag, adequate oxygenation on low FiO2.
- Post-extubation: patient speaking in full sentences, no stridor, no respiratory distress.
- Tolerating small sips of clear liquids without choking.

Exam:
- Airway: patent; able to speak (indicates airway patency).
- Lungs: clear to auscultation bilaterally, no signs of aspiration on exam.
- Neuro: alert and oriented x3.

Assessment:
- Successful liberation from mechanical ventilation; airway obstruction resolved.
Plan:
- Observe for 24 hours for delayed airway edema/aspiration symptoms.
- Provide discharge education regarding prevention of future choking episodes and basic airway maneuvers.
- Discharge planning pending stable observation period.

Discharge Summary — Hospital Day 3
Admission diagnosis:
- Foreign body airway obstruction (food bolus) with loss of airway protective reflexes; required advanced airway management.

Hospital course:
- Patient presented after witnessed choking event with initial inability to speak and subsequent loss of consciousness. Bystander basic maneuvers (back blows, abdominal thrusts) performed.
- On EMS arrival head-tilt/chin-lift used (no cervical spine concern). Oropharyngeal airway placed. Direct laryngoscopy and cuffed endotracheal tube placed to secure airway and reduce aspiration risk.
- Admitted to ICU on mechanical ventilation. Serial suctioning performed to clear particulate matter.
- By hospital day 2 patient regained cough and gag reflexes; tolerated spontaneous breathing trial.
- Extubated hospital day 3 after successful cuff leak and SBT; post-extubation exam stable with normal speech and no stridor. Tolerating oral intake.

Discharge condition:
- Afebrile, hemodynamically stable, breathing comfortably on room air, speaking normally, tolerating oral intake.

Discharge instructions:
- Avoid large bites; chew food thoroughly; eat slowly and avoid talking while eating.
- Education provided on recognition and response to choking: encourage coughing if able, perform back blows and abdominal thrusts for conscious adults; when unconscious, emergency services should be activated and trained rescuers may perform advanced airway interventions. Advised against routine blind finger sweeps in conscious patients due to risk of further impaction; note that opinions vary regarding finger sweep once patient is unconscious.
- Return precautions: new or worsening dyspnea, stridor, recurrent choking, fever, persistent cough, or any new neurologic symptoms → return to ED immediately.

Follow-up:
- Primary care follow-up in 1 week for reassessment and reinforcement of swallowing precautions. Consider referral for swallowing evaluation if recurrent or persistent choking episodes occur.

Procedures performed:
- Endotracheal intubation with cuffed tracheal tube (infraglottic airway) — successful.
- Suctioning of oropharynx/ETT — performed as needed.

Disposition: Discharged home in stable condition.

Admission Note — Day 0
Chief complaint: Progressive thoracolumbar back pain and fatigue x 6 weeks.

History of present illness:
68-year-old man with 6 weeks of progressively worsening midline back pain, worst in thoracolumbar region, constant, worse with activity, partially relieved by rest. Reports increasing fatigue and exertional dyspnea over same interval. No focal limb weakness, no bowel/bladder dysfunction. Oliguria for 4 days and decreased oral intake for 1 week. No recent travel. Past medical history notable for obesity and hypertension. No prior hematologic malignancy documented. No known family history of plasma cell disorders. Denies smoking or recent significant radiation exposure.

Physical exam:
General: alert, oriented, appears fatigued.
Vitals: afebrile, hemodynamically stable.
HEENT: mucous membranes dry.
Cardiac: regular, no murmurs.
Lungs: clear to auscultation.
Abdomen: soft, non-tender.
Musculoskeletal: marked tenderness to palpation over T12–L2 spinous processes. No gross spinal deformity. No focal neurologic deficit; strength 5/5 in all extremities; sensation intact.
Skin: no petechiae or ecchymoses.

Initial studies (summary of pertinent results obtained in ED/inpatient admission):
- CBC: normocytic, normochromic anemia documented.
- Basic metabolic panel: elevated serum creatinine consistent with renal impairment; serum calcium elevated above reference.
- Serum protein electrophoresis: monoclonal spike (m-spike) detected.
- Serum free light chains: marked elevation with abnormal light-chain ratio.
- Chest/abdominal radiographs and dedicated thoracolumbar radiographs/CT: multiple lytic bone lesions in thoracolumbar spine with collapse at T12.
- Urinalysis: proteinuria present.
- Immunoglobulin quantification: relative suppression of non-monoclonal immunoglobulins consistent with impaired normal humoral immunity.

Assessment / Plan (admission):
Impression: Probable multiple myeloma presenting with symptomatic bone disease (thoracolumbar pain, lytic lesions), normocytic normochromic anemia, renal impairment likely related to monoclonal protein / light-chain burden, and hypercalcemia.

Immediate management:
- Admit to hematology/oncology service.
- Initiate high-dose corticosteroid therapy (dexamethasone) for disease control.
- Aggressive intravenous hydration for renal support and hypercalcemia management.
- Administer intravenous bisphosphonate for skeletal disease and hypercalcemia.
- Analgesia for pain control; consult radiation oncology for consideration of targeted radiation to symptomatic T12 lesion.
- Nephrology consult for renal impairment and guidance on supportive renal measures.
- Obtain bone marrow biopsy for diagnostic confirmation and cytologic characterization.
- Continue close monitoring of calcium, renal function, and urine output.
- Counsel patient regarding diagnosis differential (multiple myeloma vs plasmacytoma/smoldering disease) pending biopsy; discuss need for systemic therapy and possible autologous stem cell transplant evaluation after induction.
- Infection precautions and baseline immunoglobulin levels obtained; plan to consider immunoglobulin replacement if hypogammaglobulinemia with recurrent infections.

Hospital Day 2 — Progress Note
Subjective: Patient reports modest improvement in back pain with scheduled analgesics and bedside positioning. Increased urine output after IV fluids. No fevers, no new neurologic symptoms.

Objective:
- Vitals stable.
- Pain improved from severe to moderate with therapy.
- Labs: creatinine down from initial value after IV fluids; serum calcium trending toward normal after bisphosphonate and hydration. Hemoglobin remains low, consistent with normocytic anemia.
- Bone marrow biopsy performed today; specimen sent to pathology.
- No signs of infection on exam.

Assessment:
- Multiple myeloma remains leading diagnosis. Early response to steroids and supportive measures noted with improvement in calcium and renal function.
- Pain from vertebral lesion partially controlled; radiation oncology consulted and recommended short course of focal radiotherapy to T12 for local disease control and pain relief.
- Hematology to arrange initiation of disease-directed systemic therapy once biopsy and staging results confirmed; plan for induction therapy followed by evaluation for autologous stem cell transplant in appropriate candidate.
- Immunoglobulin panel shows reduced polyclonal immunoglobulins despite monoclonal protein; no active infections at this time. IVIG to be considered for recurrent infections or if clinically indicated after initiating therapy.

Plan:
- Continue dexamethasone.
- Continue IV fluids until euvolemic and renal function stabilized.
- Continue monthly bisphosphonate infusions (first given inpatient).
- Proceed with focal radiotherapy to T12 tomorrow per radiation oncology.
- Await final pathology from bone marrow; discuss systemic therapy regimen and sequencing with patient once pathology confirmed.
- Discharge planning to begin once stable with outpatient hematology/oncology follow-up arranged within one week.

Hospital Day 3 — Progress Note
Subjective: Patient reports further reduction in pain following first fraction of radiation. Denies fevers, cough, dysuria.

Objective:
- Vitals stable, afebrile.
- Neurologic exam remains intact; no new deficits.
- Labs: creatinine stable and improved compared to admission. Serum calcium in normal range. Hemoglobin stable at low level.
- Pathology final report received: bone marrow biopsy demonstrates clonal plasma cell proliferation consistent with multiple myeloma. Immunophenotype consistent with monoclonal plasma cell population; correlates with serum m-spike and elevated free light chains.
- Imaging and labs reviewed: multiple lytic lesions, anemia, renal impairment, hypercalcemia — meet criteria for symptomatic multiple myeloma requiring systemic therapy.

Assessment:
- Confirmed diagnosis: Multiple myeloma (symptomatic) — presenting with bone disease, anemia, renal impairment, hypercalcemia.
- Clinical status improved on supportive measures: hydration, corticosteroid, bisphosphonate, and focal radiation.
- No infectious complications during hospitalization.

Plan / Discharge preparations:
- Start induction systemic therapy as outpatient with hematology/oncology; patient counseled that systemic therapy + steroids is standard initial approach and that autologous stem cell transplant will be considered after response to induction.
- First outpatient infusion of disease-directed therapy to be arranged by hematology within 5–7 days.
- Continue monthly bisphosphonate therapy — schedule outpatient infusion.
- Radiation oncology to complete short-course focal radiation to T12 as inpatient/outpatient per plan.
- Nephrology follow-up arranged for ongoing renal monitoring; avoid nephrotoxins; hydration instructions provided.
- Provide education on infection risk; instruct patient to seek urgent care for fever or signs of infection. Discuss possibility of immunoglobulin replacement if recurrent infections or significant hypogammaglobulinemia after therapy initiation.
- Prescriptions: short steroid taper instructions and oral analgesics for breakthrough pain.
- Arrange social work and transplant coordinator referral for stem cell transplant evaluation and fertility/advanced care planning discussions as appropriate.
- Discharge condition: stable for discharge.

Discharge Summary
Admission date: Day 0
Discharge date: Day 3
Primary diagnosis: Multiple myeloma (Ig monoclonal protein with clonal bone marrow plasma cells) presenting with symptomatic bone lesions, anemia, renal impairment, and hypercalcemia.

Hospital course summary:
- Presented with 6 weeks of progressive thoracolumbar pain, fatigue, and oliguria.
- Initial workup demonstrated normocytic normochromic anemia, elevated serum creatinine, hypercalcemia, serum m-spike on protein electrophoresis, elevated serum free light chains with abnormal ratio, and multiple lytic lesions on imaging.
- Bone marrow biopsy confirmed clonal plasma cell proliferation diagnostic of multiple myeloma.
- Managed acutely with intravenous hydration, high-dose corticosteroid (dexamethasone), intravenous bisphosphonate for skeletal disease and hypercalcemia, analgesia, and focal radiation to symptomatic T12 lesion.
- Renal function and serum calcium improved with therapy. No infectious complications occurred inpatient.
- Multidisciplinary consultations completed: hematology/oncology, radiation oncology, nephrology. Social work and transplant coordination initiated.

Discharge medications and instructions:
- Continue outpatient hematology/oncology follow-up within 5–7 days to initiate induction systemic therapy.
- Continue monthly bisphosphonate infusions (first dose given inpatient; outpatient schedule arranged).
- Complete radiation course as arranged by radiation oncology.
- Short steroid taper instructions provided.
- Analgesics PRN; avoid NSAIDs given renal considerations.
- Maintain oral hydration; avoid nephrotoxins; follow nephrology recommendations.
- Return precautions: fever, new neurologic deficits (weakness, numbness, bowel/bladder changes), worsening pain, decreased urine output — return to ED immediately.

Follow-up:
- Hematology/oncology clinic appointment scheduled for initiation of systemic therapy and transplant evaluation.
- Nephrology follow-up scheduled for renal monitoring.
- Radiation oncology follow-up per treatment plan.

Prognosis and counseling:
- Patient informed that multiple myeloma is treatable but generally not curable; goals are disease control, symptom relief, preservation of renal function, and evaluation for autologous stem cell transplant after induction therapy. Discussed infection risk and possible need for immunoglobulin replacement if clinically indicated.

76 y/o man with painless gross hematuria, high pretest probability for urothelial carcinoma given smoking history. Plan:

Discharge Summary

Discharge date: Day 2
Admission diagnosis: Gross hematuria; bladder tumor.
Procedures: Flexible cystoscopy with blue light (hexaminolevulinate) and transurethral resection of bladder tumor (TURBT) with sampling of underlying muscle.

76-year-old man admitted for evaluation of painless gross hematuria. Underwent cystoscopy with blue light and TURBT on hospital day 0. Signature:
[De-identified attending physician]

Admission Note
HPI:
Adult patient presented to ED with progressive dyspnea, hypoxemia, productive cough and fever over 48 hours. Rapid deterioration of respiratory status with impending respiratory failure necessitated definitive airway. No prior history available on arrival. Given hemodynamic borderline status and need for rapid sequence induction for tracheal intubation, single bolus etomidate chosen for induction because of rapid onset and favorable cardiovascular profile.

Physical exam (pre-intubation):
- General: anxious, tachypneic.
- Vitals: tachycardic, borderline low-normal BP, hypoxemic on supplemental oxygen.
- Chest: diffuse crackles.
- Neuro: alert but dyspneic.
- Extremities/IV site: no erythema or induration prior to injection.

Procedure:
- Rapid sequence induction and orotracheal intubation performed in ED using single bolus etomidate for induction. Noted transient venous burning at injection site and brief myoclonic skeletal muscle movements during induction. Intubation successful; oxygenation and ventilation secured. No significant hypotension post-induction.

Assessment & Plan:
1) Acute hypoxemic respiratory failure requiring endotracheal intubation. Secured airway with RSI using etomidate (selected for rapid onset and minimal impact on arterial pressure). Monitor ventilatory status and sedation needs.
2) Note adverse effects observed with etomidate: venous pain on injection and transient skeletal muscle movements. Documented in procedure note.
3) Recognize risk of etomidate-associated reversible adrenal suppression via 11β-hydroxylase inhibition. In the context of suspected severe infection, single bolus may suppress cortisol for up to 72 hours. Plan: monitor hemodynamics and clinical status closely; obtain baseline random serum cortisol and consider ACTH stimulation testing if hemodynamic instability or other signs of adrenal insufficiency develop. Discussed that some authors advise prophylactic stress-dose corticosteroids in this setting.
4) Supportive care and ongoing evaluation of infectious source; plan morning cortisol and reassess.

Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
Course overnight: patient remained intubated and mechanically ventilated. Hemodynamics stable without new pressor requirement. No delayed injection-site complications.

Exam:
- Sedated, mechanically ventilated, vitals stable.
- IV site: mild residual tenderness at etomidate injection site; no erythema, no thrombophlebitis.

Labs/procedures:
- Random serum cortisol returned low compared with expected stress response. ACTH stimulation test ordered.

Assessment & Plan:
1) Post-etomidate adrenal suppression suspected given low random cortisol 24 hours after single induction dose — consistent with documented pharmacologic inhibition of adrenal steroidogenesis after etomidate. Continue close hemodynamic monitoring.
2) Given concern for adrenal suppression in setting of severe infection and in accordance with literature suggesting prophylactic corticosteroids may be used, started stress-dose hydrocortisone (IV) to cover potential adrenal insufficiency pending ACTH stimulation results. Rationale: single bolus etomidate can blunt adrenal response for up to 72 hours; prophylaxis advised by some authors in at-risk patients.
3) Continue mechanical ventilation and supportive care. Monitor for known etomidate-associated effects (nausea/vomiting, thrombophlebitis); none clinically significant noted.
4) Await ACTH stimulation results; if blunted response confirmed, plan to continue steroid coverage for anticipated 48–72 hour period of suppression and reassess.

Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
Course: Patient remains intubated but showing improved oxygenation and reduced ventilator support requirements. Hemodynamics remain stable; no vasopressors required.

Exam:
- Improved respiratory parameters.
- IV/injection site: no signs of superficial thrombophlebitis.

Labs:
- ACTH stimulation test demonstrates blunted adrenal response consistent with etomidate-associated suppression. This effect is recognized in the literature and can persist for up to 72 hours after a single bolus.

Assessment & Plan:
1) Adrenal suppression secondary to single-dose etomidate confirmed by blunted ACTH stimulation. Continued stress-dose hydrocortisone advised per prior plan to cover period of suppression (up to 72 hours post-exposure). Continue to monitor clinical status and hemodynamics.
2) Respiratory status improving; continue ventilatory wean as tolerated. Anticipate extubation once criteria met.
3) Monitor for etomidate-related complications (nausea/vomiting, thrombophlebitis); none observed.
4) Plan to discontinue hydrocortisone after passage of expected suppression window and clinical stability; reassess adrenal function if clinically indicated.

Discharge Summary
Hospital course:
- Patient admitted with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure secondary to presumed severe pulmonary infection. Required rapid sequence induction and endotracheal intubation in ED.
- Etomidate selected for induction due to rapid onset and favorable cardiovascular profile. Noted immediate adverse effects: venous pain on injection and brief myoclonic movements.
- Over first 72 hours post-induction, random cortisol was low and ACTH stimulation testing showed blunted response consistent with etomidate-induced reversible inhibition of adrenal steroidogenesis (11β-hydroxylase inhibition). This effect is described to persist up to 72 hours after a single bolus.
- Given concern for adrenal suppression in the setting of severe infection, stress-dose hydrocortisone was administered prophylactically and continued through the expected period of suppression with clinical monitoring. No evidence of adrenal crisis developed. No superficial thrombophlebitis or persistent injection-site complications observed.
- Respiratory status improved; patient successfully extubated prior to discharge and remained hemodynamically stable without vasopressor support.

Disposition and follow-up:
- Hydrocortisone discontinued after clinical stabilization and completion of anticipated suppression window. Outpatient follow-up recommended with primary care and consideration of endocrine evaluation if symptoms suggest persistent adrenal dysfunction.
- Counseling provided regarding the transient adrenal effects of etomidate and instructions to seek care for prolonged fatigue, hypotension, or other concerning symptoms.

Final diagnoses:
1) Acute hypoxemic respiratory failure requiring endotracheal intubation.
2) Exposure to single bolus etomidate for RSI with transient adrenal suppression (biochemically confirmed by low cortisol and blunted ACTH stimulation).  
3) Transient injection-site pain and brief myoclonic movements at time of etomidate administration.

Medications given in hospital (relevant to case):
- Single bolus etomidate for induction (ED).
- Stress-dose hydrocortisone administered during period of suspected/confirmed adrenal suppression; discontinued after clinical recovery and expected suppression window.

Admission Note — Day 1

History of present illness:
- 52-year-old Caucasian male presents with 3–4 months of progressive fatigue, diffuse arthralgias (hands and knees, worse in morning), decreased libido, and unintentional 6-lb weight loss. Reports intermittent right upper quadrant fullness and epigastric discomfort, and darker skin compared with 1 year ago per patient. No fever, jaundice, or overt bleeding. No history of multiple transfusions. Drinks alcohol socially (1–2 drinks/week). Family history notable for a father with “liver disease” in his 60s. No known prior liver disease or diabetes prior to recent symptoms.

Physical exam:
- General: tired-appearing middle-aged man, NAD.
- Skin: diffuse hyperpigmentation/bronzing of exposed and non-exposed skin.
- HEENT: mucous membranes moist, no scleral icterus.
- Cardiac: RRR, no murmurs.
- Lungs: CTAB.
- Abdomen: soft, nonperitoneal; liver palpable 3 cm below right costal margin, smooth to mildly nodular; no splenomegaly.
- MSK: tenderness and decreased range of motion at bilateral 2nd–4th MCP joints; no effusions.
- Genitourinary: testes appear small for age by palpation; no focal neurologic deficit.

Assessment / Plan:
1) Suspected iron overload (hereditary hemochromatosis highest on differential) given bronze skin, hepatomegaly, arthropathy, decreased libido. Pathophysiology consistent with excess iron deposition causing oxidative stress and organ injury.
2) Labs ordered: CBC, CMP, AST/ALT, alkaline phosphatase, bilirubin, INR, fasting glucose, HbA1c, ferritin, serum iron, TIBC, transferrin saturation, hepatitis serologies. Obtain HFE genetic testing (C282Y/H63D) and refer for iron studies processing.
3) Imaging: RUQ ultrasound to evaluate liver morphology; consider fibrosis assessment.
4) Cardiac screen: 12-lead ECG in ED; consider echocardiogram if symptoms of heart failure or arrhythmia develop.
5) Endocrine assessment: obtain fasting glucose and HbA1c now; refer to endocrinology for glucose management if abnormal.
6) Counseling: discussed hereditary nature and need for family screening; avoided definitive inpatient iron-reduction therapy pending confirmation and specialist input.
7) Admit for expedited workup and monitoring.

Exam:

2) New diagnosis of diabetes mellitus likely secondary to iron deposition in pancreatic islets — consult endocrinology for initial management and glycemic counseling; insulin therapy not started inpatient; will arrange outpatient follow-up.
3) Liver findings concerning for chronic liver disease related to iron deposition. Hepatology consult placed for assessment, staging (fibrosis assessment) and discussion of definitive iron-reduction therapy. Arrange for hepatology to consider further fibrosis assessment (transient elastography or biopsy as indicated).

Hospital Day 3 — Progress Note

Exam:

Results:

1) Hereditary hemochromatosis type 1 confirmed by HFE C282Y homozygosity in the setting of marked iron overload (ferritin 1,200 ng/mL, transferrin saturation 95%), clinical bronze skin, hepatomegaly, arthropathy, and secondary diabetes.
