Discharge Summary

Admission diagnosis:
- Hereditary hemochromatosis (HFE C282Y homozygous) with iron overload causing hepatic involvement, secondary diabetes mellitus, and arthropathy.

Hospital course:
- Admitted for expedited evaluation of suspected iron overload after presentation with fatigue, arthralgia, bronze skin, hepatomegaly, and decreased libido.
- Initial workup demonstrated elevated transaminases, ferritin 1,200 ng/mL, transferrin saturation 95%. RUQ ultrasound showed enlarged heterogeneous liver with borderline nodularity. Hepatitis serologies negative. HFE genetic testing returned C282Y/C282Y confirming hereditary hemochromatosis type 1. Cardiac screening (ECG) showed normal sinus rhythm; no clinical heart failure during admission.
- Endocrine evaluation initiated for newly identified diabetes (fasting glucose 156 mg/dL, HbA1c 7.6%) consistent with secondary diabetes due to pancreatic iron deposition.
- Consults: Hepatology and Endocrinology arranged as outpatient. Genetic counseling referral and family screening recommended.

Discharge condition:
- Stable, ambulatory, no acute complications.

Discharge medications and instructions:
- Continue home medications (none newly started in hospital aside from PRN acetaminophen).
- Avoid excess dietary/parenteral iron; limit alcohol intake.
- Follow up with hepatology within 1 week to arrange iron-reduction therapy and liver fibrosis staging.
- Follow up with endocrinology within 2 weeks for diabetes management.
- Arrange genetic counseling and recommend iron studies/genetic testing for first-degree relatives.
- Return to ED for chest pain, syncope, new shortness of breath, jaundice, or significant worsening symptoms.

Follow-up and preventive care:
- Hepatology: initiation of standard iron-reduction therapy and monitoring (to be determined by hepatology), fibrosis assessment, surveillance for cirrhosis complications as indicated.
- Endocrinology: formal diabetes management plan and assessment for endocrine complications (hypogonadism evaluation as indicated).
- Cardiology evaluation if arrhythmia or heart failure symptoms develop.

Pathophysiologic note:
- Patient’s presentation is consistent with iron accumulation causing tissue injury via oxidative stress (Fenton chemistry) with deposition most prominent in liver and pancreas, leading to hepatic dysfunction and secondary diabetes. Family screening recommended due to hereditary pattern of HFE-related hemochromatosis.

1. Admission Note
HPI:
68-year-old male admitted for elective right total hip arthroplasty. Preoperative interview: reports chronic mild anemia and treated hypertension for which he takes clonidine. Denies chronic alcohol use; admits to isolated stimulant use (cocaine) within 24 hours prior to admission. No other significant comorbidities reported. Patient understands anesthesia plan and consents.

Physical exam:
Vitals: afebrile, BP 134/78 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, SpO2 98% RA. Cardiovascular and pulmonary exam without acute findings. Airway exam: Mallampati II, adequate mouth opening. Peripheral perfusion intact.

Assessment & Plan:
- Perioperative anesthesia considerations reviewed with surgical team:
  - Age-related decrease in MAC: patient is 68; MAC declines ~6% per decade such that inhalational potency requirement will be reduced compared with a 40-year-old. Plan to account for age-adjusted sevoflurane requirement.
  - Medications/substances affecting MAC:
    - Clonidine (alpha-2 agonist) will decrease MAC — anticipate lower volatile requirement.
    - Recent stimulant use (acute cocaine) increases MAC — will plan for higher anesthetic requirement during induction/incision and use adjuncts to mitigate.
  - Physiologic factors to monitor: maintain normothermia (avoid hyperthermia which would increase MAC) and avoid profound hypotension (MAP <40 mmHg decreases MAC and confounds dosing). Correct anemia if clinically significant.
- Anesthetic plan:
  - Induction: propofol IV (acknowledged to decrease MAC) for induction and facilitate endotracheal intubation.
  - Maintenance: sevoflurane with nitrous oxide carrier (nitrous oxide MAC ~104% and will be used to reduce required sevoflurane fraction via additive MAC effect). Target end-tidal sevoflurane adjusted for age. Use fentanyl (opioid) and dexmedetomidine infusion (alpha-2 agonist) intraoperatively to reduce inhalational requirement and blunt autonomic responses.
  - Targeting intraoperative autonomic suppression at time of incision by achieving an effective equivalent of MAC-BAR (1.7–2.0 MAC) through combination of volatile concentration, nitrous oxide, and IV adjuncts rather than sole escalation of volatile agent.
  - Emergence: decrease volatile to allow MAC-awake threshold (0.3–0.5 MAC) prior to extubation.
- Orders: NPO, standard monitors, type/cross per OR protocol, hold morning clonidine dose per anesthesia plan, continue to monitor hemoglobin and temperature intraop.

2. Hospital Day 2 Progress Note (Post-op Day 0 / Immediate Postop)
Course overnight/intraoperative summary:
- Induction with propofol; fentanyl bolus given for analgesia. Dexmedetomidine infusion started prior to incision and continued through major portion of case.
- Maintenance: sevoflurane with 50% nitrous oxide carrier. Intraoperative end-tidal sevoflurane averaged approximately 1.2% (age-adjusted target below MAC40 value given adjuncts). Estimated total MAC equivalence at time of incision (sevo + N2O + IV adjunct effect) was sufficient to suppress autonomic response without escalating sevoflurane to concentrations that would be expected for a younger, non-medicated patient. Hemodynamics stable; no MAP <40 mmHg recorded. Normothermia maintained.
- Emergence: sevoflurane titrated down to allow MAC-awake range prior to extubation; extubated in OR without airway complications. No intraoperative awareness reported.

Current status:
- In PACU hemodynamically stable, pain controlled with multimodal analgesia including intermittent opioid dosing. No signs of intraoperative awareness or recall. No nausea/vomiting of significance.
- Labs: hemoglobin unchanged from preop baseline (chronic mild anemia noted preop). No transfusion required.

Plan:
- Continue routine postoperative monitoring. Continue dexmedetomidine infusion discontinued per protocol in PACU; opioids as needed for breakthrough pain. Monitor for any delayed emergence phenomena given intraop MAC adjustments. Maintain normothermia. Mobilize with PT tomorrow.

3. Hospital Day 3 Progress Note (Post-op Day 1)
Interval course:
- Overnight uneventful. Patient ambulatory with assistance. Pain controlled on oral analgesics. No signs of end-organ dysfunction.
- Neurologic status normal. No episodes of hypertension or sympathetic storm postoperatively.

Assessment:
- Uncomplicated postoperative course following general anesthesia with sevoflurane/nitrous oxide and intravenous adjuncts. Age and clonidine contributed to decreased baseline MAC; recent stimulant exposure likely increased anesthetic requirement acutely and was mitigated by use of nitrous oxide and opioid/alpha-2 agonist adjuncts. Intraoperative goals (autonomic suppression at incision using combined MAC equivalents and safe emergence to MAC-awake) achieved.

Plan:
- Continue routine postop care. Transition to home oral analgesics later today. Discharge planning pending PT clearance and pain control.

4. Discharge Summary
Admission diagnosis: Osteoarthritis of right hip; elective right total hip arthroplasty.
Procedure: Right total hip arthroplasty under general anesthesia.
Anesthetic course summary:
- Induction with propofol. Maintenance with sevoflurane plus 50% nitrous oxide. Fentanyl boluses and intraoperative dexmedetomidine infusion used as adjuncts to reduce inhalational requirement and blunt autonomic responses. Age-related reduction in MAC and chronic clonidine use reduced volatile requirement; recent acute stimulant exposure increased required anesthetic but was managed with multimodal approach. Intraoperative hemodynamics stable; normothermia maintained; no MAP <40 mmHg.
- Targeted MAC-BAR equivalent at incision using combination of agents rather than high volatile concentrations. Emergence achieved by titrating volatile down to MAC-awake range (0.3–0.5 MAC); extubated in OR without complication. No awareness reported.

Postoperative course:
- Uncomplicated. Pain controlled with oral analgesics. Ambulated with assistance. No infectious or cardiopulmonary complications.

Discharge medications and instructions:
- Continue prescribed oral analgesics, encourage home exercise per PT, follow-up with orthopedics in 2 weeks. Return precautions reviewed.

Follow-up:
- Orthopedics clinic in 2 weeks. If concerning symptoms (chest pain, severe shortness of breath, fever, uncontrolled pain, wound issues), return to ED or contact clinic.

Clinician: Attending anesthesiologist on service.

Admission Note
Date/time: Hospital Day 0

HPI:
62-year-old F with a 12-year history of seropositive rheumatoid arthritis presents with progressive fatigue, exertional dyspnea and lightheadedness for 6 weeks. No overt GI bleeding, hematuria, melena, hematemesis or recent surgery. Reports worsening arthralgias but no new fevers. Denies weight loss or night sweats. Chronic inflammatory disease well known; therapy has been stable per patient for several months. Patient reports decreased exercise tolerance and pallor noted by family.

Physical exam:
VS: afebrile, BP 118/72, HR 96, RR 16, SpO2 98% RA.
General: pale-appearing female, NAD.
HEENT: conjunctival pallor.
Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmurs.
Lungs: clear to auscultation.
Abdomen: soft, non-tender, no hepatosplenomegaly.
MSK: synovitis and deformities of hands consistent with chronic RA.
Skin: no ecchymoses.

Relevant labs on admission:
- Hemoglobin 9.0 g/dL (female <11.5 g/dL = anemia)
- MCV 88 fL (normocytic)
- WBC and platelets within reference limits
- Ferritin 320 ng/mL (>100 ng/mL supports inflammatory iron sequestration)
- Transferrin saturation 14% (commonly 10–20% in anemia of chronic disease)
- TIBC low/normal (consistent with anemia of chronic inflammation)
- CRP and ESR elevated (support active inflammation)

Assessment/Plan:
Impression:
1) Anemia of chronic disease (anemia of chronic inflammation) secondary to active rheumatoid arthritis. Presentation: normocytic anemia with low/normal TIBC, ferritin elevated (>100 ng/mL) and transferrin saturation in 10–20% range. Mechanism likely IL-6–mediated hepcidin upregulation with ferroportin internalization → iron sequestration, plus inflammatory suppression of erythropoiesis.
2) Symptomatic anemia (fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, lightheadedness).

Plan:
- Admit for evaluation and treatment of symptomatic anemia.
- No evidence of acute blood loss; transfusion not indicated at current Hb and hemodynamics.
- Administer IV iron given inflammatory iron sequestration and symptomatic disease. Discussed evidence: IV ferric carboxymaltose may be more likely to improve anemia than iron sucrose but may carry higher risk of serious adverse events; after risk/benefit discussion, proceed with single dose IV ferric carboxymaltose infusion with appropriate monitoring.
- Do not start erythropoietin at this time (treatment sometimes used for severe/persistent cases; benefit unclear/costly). Will reconsider if anemia persists despite iron repletion and RA control.
- Continue workup to exclude concomitant iron deficiency if clinically suspected: consider stool guaiac if any concern for occult blood; check repeat ferritin/iron indices as indicated.
- Optimize RA management to target inflammation (arrange rheumatology follow-up to consider adjustment of disease-modifying therapy).
- Monitor vitals and watch for infusion reactions during and after IV iron.
- Labs: CBC q24h while inpatient, repeat iron panel after infusion per protocol.
- Patient education provided regarding diagnosis (anemia of chronic inflammation) and rationale for treating underlying RA.

Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
Date/time: Hospital Day 2 morning

Brief interval HPI:
Patient received IV ferric carboxymaltose infusion yesterday without immediate adverse reaction. Overnight remained hemodynamically stable. Subjective improvement in energy noted today vs. admission.

Exam:
VS stable. Conjunctival pallor persists. No signs of allergic reaction. Joint exam unchanged.

Labs:
- CBC 24h post-infusion: Hgb 9.0 g/dL (no acute change expected within 24h)
- Ferritin unchanged (acute rise expected as acute-phase reactant/sequestration)
- Transferrin saturation 14%
- CRP/ESR remain elevated.

Assessment/Plan:
1) Anemia of chronic disease due to active RA — clinically stable after IV iron infusion. Lab pattern unchanged at 24h (expected).
2) No transfusion required.
3) Continue monitoring. No erythropoietin started given unclear additional benefit and plan to reassess after interval follow-up.
4) Arrange outpatient rheumatology appointment within 2 weeks to optimize anti-inflammatory therapy (treating underlying disease is the ideal therapy for anemia of chronic inflammation).
5) Plan repeat CBC and iron indices at 4 weeks to assess response to IV iron and to determine need for further therapy (repeat IV iron vs. consideration of erythropoietin if persistent/severe despite RA control).
6) Discharge planning initiated if clinically stable.

Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
Date/time: Hospital Day 3 morning

Interval HPI:
Patient reports continued subjective improvement in fatigue; ambulating with less dyspnea on exertion. No delayed infusion reactions, no new bleeding.

Exam:
VS stable. No rash, no wheeze, lungs and heart exam unchanged. Mild pallor persists.

Labs:
- CBC stable: Hgb 9.1 g/dL.
- No evidence of hemolysis.
- Inflammatory markers still elevated.

Assessment/Plan:
1) Anemia of chronic disease (normocytic) secondary to rheumatoid arthritis. Clinical improvement after IV ferric carboxymaltose; expected hematologic response will be delayed over weeks if effective.
2) No transfusion needed. No complications from IV iron.
3) Erythropoietin not initiated inpatient; will consider if anemia remains severe/persistent after optimization of inflammation and repeat iron therapy, given uncertain/no clear additional benefit and cost considerations.
4) Discharge pending social/Medicare arrangements and outpatient rheumatology follow-up.

Discharge Summary
Admission date: Hospital Day 0
Discharge date: Hospital Day 3
Admitting diagnosis: Symptomatic anemia due to anemia of chronic disease (anemia of chronic inflammation) in setting of active rheumatoid arthritis
Hospital course:
- 62-year-old female with long-standing RA admitted for symptomatic anemia (Hb 9.0 g/dL) and evaluation.
- Evaluation notable for normocytic anemia, ferritin 320 ng/mL (>100 ng/mL), transferrin saturation 14% (10–20% typical for ACD), low/normal TIBC and elevated inflammatory markers. Findings consistent with anemia of chronic disease from inflammatory cytokine–mediated hepcidin upregulation and iron sequestration; inflammatory suppression of erythropoiesis likely contributory.
- No evidence of acute blood loss or hemolysis.
- Treatment: single inpatient dose IV ferric carboxymaltose administered after discussion of relative efficacy and risks vs. alternatives (moderate-certainty evidence favors ferric carboxymaltose over iron sucrose for improvement in anemia but may carry higher risk of serious adverse events). Infusion tolerated without adverse events.
- Erythropoietin was considered but deferred given unclear additional benefit and plan to reassess after outpatient optimization of RA and repeat labs.
- Clinical status improved subjectively; remained hemodynamically stable throughout stay. No transfusion required.

Discharge condition: improved, hemodynamically stable.

Discharge medications:
- Continue chronic RA medications as previously prescribed (no changes made inpatient). (Outpatient rheumatology to consider adjustment.)
- No new oral iron started inpatient.

Follow-up and recommendations:
- Rheumatology clinic appointment arranged within 2 weeks to optimize anti-inflammatory therapy (treat underlying disease to address the root cause of anemia).
- PCP follow-up in 1 week.
- Labs: CBC and iron indices (ferritin, transferrin saturation, TIBC if available) to be drawn in 4 weeks to assess hematologic response to IV iron and effect of RA management. Earlier labs may be obtained per rheumatology/PCP.
- Consider repeat IV iron if insufficient response and per rheumatology/guidelines; consider erythropoietin for persistent/severe anemia after optimization of inflammation and iron therapy (not initiated inpatient due to uncertain benefit/cost).
- Return precautions: worsening dyspnea at rest, syncope, chest pain, new bleeding or signs of infusion reaction.

Primary discharge diagnosis: Anemia of chronic disease (anemia of chronic inflammation) secondary to rheumatoid arthritis; treated with IV ferric carboxymaltose inpatient.

Admission Note
HPI: 72-year-old male with history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, obesity, and chronic erectile dysfunction presents to ED with 12 hours of inability to pass urine. Patient reports months of progressive lower urinary tract symptoms including urinary frequency, nocturia (waking 2–3× nightly), hesitancy, weak urinary stream and sensation of incomplete emptying. No fever, no gross hematuria. Current medications include metformin and amlodipine for HTN. Family history positive for “prostate problems.” No prior urologic procedures.

Physical exam: T 37.0°C, BP 138/78, HR 82, RR 16, SpO2 98% RA. Abdomen: suprapubic distension and dullness to percussion, suprapubic tenderness. Bladder palpable. External genital exam unremarkable. DRE: enlarged, smooth, non-nodular prostate on bedside exam.

ED course: Foley catheter placed for acute urinary retention; immediate drainage of 900 mL amber urine with marked symptomatic relief.

Labs/imaging: BMP: Na 138, K 4.2, Cr 1.6 mg/dL (unknown baseline). UA: no leukocyte esterase, no nitrites; microscopy pending. Urine sent for culture. PSA sent. Renal ultrasound ordered (result pending).

Assessment/Plan:
1) Acute urinary retention secondary to suspected benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) causing bladder outlet obstruction. Immediate catheter decompression achieved. Continue Foley catheter.
2) Start medical therapy for symptomatic BPH:
   - Terazosin 1 mg PO nightly for rapid symptomatic relief of obstructive symptoms (alpha-blocker).
   - Finasteride 5 mg PO daily (5α-reductase inhibitor) — counseled that clinical benefit and prostate volume reduction expected over months.
3) Monitor renal function and urine output. Repeat BMP tomorrow to document improvement after decompression.
4) Urology consulted for evaluation and planning of trial of void and discussion of definitive options if refractory (surgical removal of part of prostate may be considered).
5) Review outpatient medications: amlodipine may worsen BPH symptoms; recommend PCP review for antihypertensive substitution.
6) Counselled on lifestyle measures (weight loss, decrease caffeine intake, increase physical activity) and discussed that plant/herbal extracts have uncertain benefit per available evidence.
7) Pending: urine culture, PSA, renal ultrasound. Disposition: admit to medicine for monitoring and urology input.

Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
Subjective: Patient reports significant relief of suprapubic pain after catheterization. No fever, no dysuria.

Objective: Vitals stable. Foley draining clear urine, ~2 L in last 24 hours. BMP: Cr 1.2 mg/dL (improved from 1.6). UA unchanged; urine culture pending. Renal ultrasound completed — no hydronephrosis (result reviewed). DRE today again consistent with enlarged, smooth prostate.

Assessment/Plan:
- Acute urinary retention due to BPH, post-decompression with improving renal function.
- Continue terazosin 1 mg PO qHS and finasteride 5 mg PO daily.
- Foley catheter in place; plan trial of void (TOU) tomorrow with urology present.
- Reinforce medication reconciliation: advised coordination with PCP about switching amlodipine given its potential to worsen obstructive symptoms.
- Continue to monitor urine output, BMP. No antibiotics at present given negative UA and afebrile status; will start if culture positive or if clinical evidence of UTI develops.
- Patient educated regarding lifestyle measures again; patient understands limited evidence for phytotherapy and prefers to focus on prescribed therapy.

Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
Subjective: Patient states he feels better, less suprapubic pressure. No fever or new complaints.

Objective: Vitals stable. Removed Foley for trial of void this morning per urology. Patient voided spontaneously with weak stream, voided volume 150 mL. Post-void bladder scan demonstrated residual 220 mL. Patient re-catheterized with 16F Foley without difficulty. Urine culture pending; no leukocytosis on labs.

Assessment/Plan:
- Failed trial of void with high post-void residual consistent with persistent bladder outlet obstruction from BPH.
- Continue indwelling Foley for outpatient management. Continue terazosin 1 mg qHS and finasteride 5 mg daily. Emphasized that finasteride effect delayed over months; terazosin provides more immediate symptomatic benefit.
- Urology recommends outpatient follow-up in 1–2 weeks for catheter management, repeated attempt at voiding, and discussion of definitive options if obstruction persists (surgical removal of part of the prostate may be considered).
- PSA result returned: 5.2 ng/mL. Counseling documented that PSA can be elevated with BPH; urology will interpret and determine need for further evaluation.
- Discussed discontinuation/substitution of amlodipine with PCP due to potential worsening of urinary symptoms.
- Discharge planning: patient stable for discharge with Foley and outpatient urology appointment. Education provided on catheter care and signs/symptoms of infection/obstruction.

Discharge Summary
Admission diagnosis: Acute urinary retention secondary to benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH).

Hospital course:
- Presented with inability to void and suprapubic pain. Foley catheter placed in ED with drainage of 900 mL and prompt symptomatic relief.
- Initial labs showed elevated creatinine 1.6 mg/dL which improved to 1.2 mg/dL after decompression. UA without evidence of infection; urine culture pending. Renal ultrasound showed no hydronephrosis.
- Urology evaluated; DRE consistent with enlarged smooth prostate. Trial of void on hospital day 3 resulted in significant post-void residual (220 mL); Foley reinserted.
- Medical therapy initiated: terazosin 1 mg PO nightly and finasteride 5 mg PO daily. Counseling provided regarding time course of benefit (alpha-blocker rapid, 5α-reductase inhibitor over months).
- PSA = 5.2 ng/mL; noted that PSA may be elevated in BPH and does not by itself indicate increased prostate cancer risk; urology to determine further workup.
- Patient informed about lifestyle measures (weight loss, reduce caffeine, exercise) and advised that herbal therapies have limited supportive evidence.

Discharge condition: Stable, afebrile, pain controlled, catheter draining well.

Discharge medications:
- Terazosin 1 mg PO nightly
- Finasteride 5 mg PO daily
- Continue home medications except discuss amlodipine with PCP for possible substitution (documented recommendation)

Follow-up:
- Urology clinic in 1–2 weeks for catheter management, repeat voiding trial and discussion of further management including possible surgical removal of part of the prostate if obstruction persists.
- Primary care follow-up for antihypertensive review and continued diabetes/weight management.

Return precautions: fever, rigors, worsening suprapubic pain, inability to drain via catheter, gross hematuria, or any signs of systemic infection — return to ED.

Pending results: urine culture — patient instructed to call if positive and to start therapy per urology/PCP instructions.

Admission Note
HPI: 35-year-old woman, G2P1 at 28 weeks gestation, presented to ED after a fall with a full-thickness laceration to the right distal forearm requiring repair. After discussion and obstetric consultation, a peripheral nerve block was chosen to provide local anesthesia for wound repair while avoiding general anesthesia. A lidocaine-based local anesthetic nerve block was administered (chosen because lidocaine is considered safe in pregnancy). Immediately after the block and repair the patient noted dense numbness and inability to actively extend the wrist and digits on the treated side. Over the next hour she developed increasing focal swelling and ecchymosis at the block site. She denied dyspnea, tongue or pharyngeal swelling, chest pain, or neurologic symptoms elsewhere.

PMH: otherwise healthy. No known drug allergies.

Social Hx: noncontributory.

Exam:
- General: alert, in no acute respiratory distress.
- HEENT: no tongue, pharyngeal or laryngeal edema.
- Right forearm/antecubital region: visible ecchymosis and soft tissue swelling at injection site, atraumatic suture line over distal forearm. Area tender to palpation. Blanching not present.
- Neurovascular right upper extremity: diminished light touch and pinprick in radial distribution of forearm and dorsal hand. Motor: 2/5 wrist extension and finger extension on right. Sensation and motor function intact in median and ulnar distributions. Radial and ulnar pulses palpable; capillary refill <2 sec.
- Cardiorespiratory: normal.

Assessment/Plan:
1. Iatrogenic local complication after peripheral nerve block (lidocaine) causing focal hematoma with resultant transient nerve dysfunction (motor and sensory deficit in radial distribution).
   - Rationale: temporal relation to block, local swelling/ecchymosis at injection site, focal deficits limited to expected nerve distribution. No evidence of systemic local anesthetic toxicity (no CNS or cardiac symptoms).
2. Management plan:
   - Admit for observation and serial neurovascular examinations of the right upper extremity q2–4 hours.
   - Conservative management of suspected hematoma: limb elevation, cold compresses, immobilization with a volar splint to limit movement and reduce pain/expansion.
   - Analgesia PRN; minimize additional local anesthetic exposure. Opioid analgesic may be used PRN for acute pain; consider nonopioid analgesics as appropriate.
   - Consult pain medicine for input on ongoing analgesic strategy and prognosis.
   - Surgical consult to evaluate if hematoma appears to be expanding or neurologic deficit worsens (possible evacuation if progressive compression).
   - Obstetric team notified and involved; document fetal status per obstetric guidance.
   - Counseling: explained that motor paralysis/weakness can be induced by nerve block; most patients with peripheral nerve block–related deficits recover over weeks. Reviewed expected recovery timeline (majority improve within 4–6 weeks; small risk of longer-term sequelae). Patient understands plan and risks.

Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
Subjective: Patient reports decreased pain at the repair site. Sensation described as "numbness is less intense." No new respiratory or systemic symptoms.

Objective:
- Right forearm: swelling and ecchymosis stable, no expansion. Wound intact.
- Neurovascular: sensory deficit in radial distribution improving (reported partial return of light touch). Motor: wrist and finger extension improved to 3/5. Distal pulses intact, neurovascular status otherwise stable.
- No signs of infection (no erythema, fever, or purulence).
- No signs of CNS or cardiac toxicity from local anesthetic.

Assessment/Plan:
1. Suspected localized hematoma with resolving nerve dysfunction after lidocaine nerve block.
   - Continue conservative management: elevation, ice, splinting.
   - Continue serial neurovascular checks q4–6 hours while inpatient.
   - Continue PRN analgesia; use opioid sparingly. Avoid further local anesthetic procedures while deficit present.
   - Pain medicine: continue to follow; no indication for additional nerve block. Discussed that repeated local anesthetic blocks for chronic pain are not routinely recommended and do not confer long-term benefit; not applicable here but reviewed.
   - Surgical consult remains on standby should hematoma expand or deficits worsen.
   - Obstetric team continues to follow; no obstetric interventions required at this time.

Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
Subjective: Patient reports further improvement in numbness and strength. Able to actively extend wrist against gravity with less effort.

Objective:
- Right forearm swelling diminishing; ecchymosis resolving.
- Neuro: sensory return ongoing in radial distribution; motor strength wrist/finger extension 4/5. Distal perfusion intact. No new deficits.
- Afebrile, stable vitals, no signs of systemic toxicity or infection.

Assessment/Plan:
1. Improving focal nerve dysfunction related to local hematoma after peripheral nerve block with lidocaine.
   - Continue conservative measures; outpatient management appropriate if stable.
   - Discharge planning today with close outpatient follow-up.
   - Education given: expected course—most patients with peripheral nerve block–related deficits recover within 4–6 weeks; continued improvement possible up to 18 months though rare persistent deficits may occur. Advised to return urgently for any increase in pain, swelling, new or worsening neurologic deficit, signs of infection, or systemic symptoms.
   - Pain control: short course PRN analgesics recommended; use opioids only as needed.
   - Avoid further local anesthetic blocks to the affected region until full evaluation by pain or anesthesia clinic; if future regional anesthesia contemplated, discuss risks and pregnancy considerations with obstetrics/anesthesia.
   - Follow-up arranged with pain medicine and surgery clinic within 1 week; obstetric follow-up as scheduled.

Discharge Summary
Admission diagnosis: Complication of peripheral nerve block (lidocaine) — focal hematoma with transient radial nerve dysfunction following local anesthetic–assisted wound repair of right distal forearm.

Hospital course: Patient underwent lidocaine-based peripheral nerve block for laceration repair after obstetric consultation. Immediately post-procedure she developed dense sensory loss and motor weakness in the radial distribution with local swelling/ecchymosis at the injection site. No signs of systemic local anesthetic toxicity were observed. Managed conservatively with limb elevation, cold compresses, splinting/immobilization, serial neurovascular examinations, and PRN analgesia. Pain medicine and obstetric teams were involved. Surgical intervention was not required as the hematoma remained nonexpanding and neurologic function progressively improved over three days.

Condition at discharge: clinically improved. Motor strength right wrist/finger extension 4/5; sensory deficits improving. No respiratory or systemic symptoms.

Discharge medications and instructions:
- Analgesics PRN (use opioid sparingly; follow dosing instructions given).
- Limb elevation and gentle use of the hand as tolerated; continue splint until clinic follow-up.
- Apply cold compresses intermittently for 48–72 hours.
- Avoid further local anesthetic procedures to the affected limb until re-evaluation.

Follow-up:
- Pain medicine clinic in 5–7 days.
- Surgical clinic PRN if no continued improvement or if signs of expanding hematoma develop.
- Obstetric follow-up per routine; obstetric team informed of course.

Prognosis: Favorable. Counseling provided that most peripheral nerve block–related deficits resolve within 4–6 weeks; majority recover; small risk of longer-term deficit exists. Patient verbalized understanding and the plan.

Admission Note
Chief complaint: Acute onset pleuritic chest pain and shortness of breath.

History of present illness:
47-year-old F presented to ED with sudden onset left-sided pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea for 6 hours. Pain sharp, worse with inspiration. Reported one episode of blood-tinged sputum. Noted swelling and pain in left calf since yesterday. Recent 6.5-hour airplane flight 5 days ago. Takes combined oral contraceptive. No recent surgery or trauma. No prior history of VTE.

Physical exam:
General: alert, uncomfortable with deep breaths.
Vitals: tachycardic (HR 110), RR 24, BP 122/78, afebrile, pulse oximetry 90% on room air.
HEENT: no cyanosis.
Cardiac: tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs, no left parasternal heave.
Neck: no jugular venous distension.
Lungs: clear to auscultation, no wheeze, no pleural rub audible.
Extremities: left calf warm, swollen, tender to palpation; right leg normal.
Skin: no petechiae.

Assessment/Plan:
Working diagnosis: acute pulmonary embolism, likely from left lower extremity DVT in setting of estrogen use and recent long-haul travel.

Diagnostic plan:
- D-dimer sent (result: elevated).
- CT pulmonary angiography ordered to confirm PE.
- Duplex ultrasound of lower extremities to evaluate for DVT.
- Cardiac biomarkers (troponin, BNP) ordered to assess RV strain.

Therapeutic plan:
- Initiated therapeutic anticoagulation with low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) given intact renal function and no high bleeding risk.
- Patient counseled to stop combined oral contraceptive.
- No indication for thrombolysis or surgical thrombectomy at presentation (hemodynamically stable).
- No vena cava filter planned at this time.
- Plan disposition: admit to medicine service for monitoring and anticoagulation transition planning.

Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
Interval history:
Patient reports improvement in chest pain and dyspnea overnight. No new hemoptysis. Ambulated to chair without syncope.

Exam:
Vitals stable; HR 92, RR 18, BP 118/76, SpO2 95% on room air. Left calf tenderness persistent; decreased swelling compared with admission.

Results:
- CT pulmonary angiography: acute bilateral segmental pulmonary emboli, greater burden on left lower lobe segments. No central saddle embolus. Imaging report mentions no large central clot causing hemodynamic compromise.
- Lower extremity duplex: occlusive thrombus in left femoral vein (proximal DVT).
- Troponin: mildly elevated (small rise) consistent with possible RV strain. BNP modestly elevated.
- D-dimer: elevated (as prior).

Assessment/Plan:
Diagnosis: acute venous thromboembolism — left proximal DVT with bilateral segmental PE; submassive physiology suggested by mild troponin/BNP rise but hemodynamically stable.
- Continue therapeutic LMWH.
- Monitor hemodynamics and for signs of bleeding.
- Given stability, no thrombolysis or thrombectomy indicated.
- Discussed anticoagulation options; plan transition to direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) for outpatient therapy for minimum 3 months.
- Arrange patient education regarding anticoagulation, bleeding precautions, and VTE prevention (early mobilization).
- Plan: observe another 24 hours, mobilize, and initiate discharge planning with outpatient follow-up.

Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
Interval history:
Patient ambulatory without dyspnea at rest or with mild exertion. No bleeding or recurrent hemoptysis.

Exam:
Vitals within normal limits; HR 84, RR 16, BP 120/80, SpO2 97% on room air. Left calf improved; no new findings.

Assessment/Plan:
- Continued clinical improvement. No hemodynamic instability or progressive RV dysfunction.
- Transitioned from therapeutic LMWH to a direct-acting oral anticoagulant (DOAC) for outpatient therapy. Patient instructed that anticoagulation duration to be at least 3 months; exact duration to be confirmed with outpatient follow-up.
- No thrombophilia workup inpatient given first VTE; plan for brief outpatient evaluation if clinically indicated or if recurrence.
- No indication for IVC filter placement.
- Provided counseling: discontinue combined oral contraceptive; avoid prolonged immobilization; perform leg exercises during travel; seek immediate care for recurrent dyspnea, chest pain, syncope, or significant bleeding.
- Arranged follow-up with primary care and hematology/vascular medicine within 1–2 weeks. Discharge planned today.

Discharge Summary
Admission diagnosis: Acute pulmonary embolism due to left proximal deep vein thrombosis.

Hospital course:
47-year-old woman on combined oral contraceptive with recent long-haul travel presented with acute pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. Initial evaluation demonstrated tachycardia, tachypnea, hypoxemia on presentation, elevated D-dimer, and left calf swelling. CT pulmonary angiography confirmed bilateral segmental pulmonary emboli. Lower extremity duplex demonstrated an occlusive left femoral vein thrombus. Cardiac biomarkers showed a small troponin rise and modest BNP elevation suggesting RV strain, but patient remained hemodynamically stable throughout hospitalization (submassive physiology). Therapeutic anticoagulation was started with low-molecular-weight heparin. No thrombolytic therapy, thrombectomy, or vena cava filter was indicated. Patient improved clinically over three days, was ambulatory, and had no bleeding complications. Transitioned to a direct oral anticoagulant for outpatient therapy.

Discharge medications and instructions:
- Direct-acting oral anticoagulant (DOAC) prescribed for anticoagulation (minimum recommended duration: 3 months). (Specific agent and dosing provided to patient.)
- Stop combined oral contraceptive.
- Education on signs/symptoms of recurrent VTE and major bleeding; seek urgent care for sudden worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, syncope, or bleeding.

Follow-up:
- Primary care visit arranged in 1 week.
- Hematology/vascular medicine referral for outpatient anticoagulation management and discussion of duration and need for further evaluation if indicated.
- Advice on VTE prevention: early mobilization after travel, leg exercises during prolonged sitting, weight management as appropriate.

Condition at discharge: improved, hemodynamically stable.

1. Admission Note
HPI:
- 58-year-old male referred for management of a newly identified left renal mass. Cross-sectional imaging demonstrates a 3.2 cm enhancing cortical lesion of the midpole of the left kidney consistent with a small renal mass. Patient is otherwise ambulatory, no prior history of dialysis or contralateral kidney disease. Discussed options; patient elected surgical management with intent to preserve renal parenchyma.

Physical Exam:
- Preoperative exam documented; patient appropriate for general anesthesia and laparoscopic approach.

Assessment / Plan:
- Indication: small renal mass (left midpole, 3.2 cm) — candidate for partial nephrectomy (standard of care for masses <4 cm).
- Plan: proceed with laparoscopic partial nephrectomy under general anesthesia. Patient to be positioned on right lateral decubitus (side opposite tumor). Anticipate temporary occlusion of renal blood flow to permit tumor excision with thin rim of normal kidney and subsequent renorrhaphy. Consent obtained covering conversion to radical nephrectomy if indicated, and risks including bleeding, infection, and urinary leak. Perioperative IV fluids and postoperative monitoring of electrolytes and fluid balance planned. Pain control plan: postoperative analgesia as needed.

2. Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
- Operation performed yesterday: laparoscopic partial nephrectomy of left midpole mass under general anesthesia. Three-port laparoscopic approach used. Renal artery temporarily occluded to control blood flow during tumor excision. Tumor excised with a thin rim of normal parenchyma. Renal parenchyma repaired (renorrhaphy). Specimen removed via limited extension of port site for intact specimen retrieval. No intraoperative need to convert to open or to remove entire kidney.
- Immediate post-op course: patient on IV fluids and receiving scheduled pain medication. Monitoring of electrolytes and fluid balance ongoing per standard after nephron-sparing surgery.
- Current status: stable, pain controlled on regimen, no clinical evidence of bleeding, infection, or urinary leak. Plan: continue IV fluids, analgesia as needed, monitor electrolytes and urine output, advance activity as tolerated. Anticipate continued inpatient observation 24–48 hours.

3. Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
- Patient remains hemodynamically stable. Pain controlled on oral/transitioning analgesia. IV fluids being weaned; fluid and electrolyte parameters stable. No clinical signs of hemorrhage, wound infection, or urinary leak.
- Pathology specimen pending; intraoperative course uncomplicated. Partial nephrectomy preserved remaining renal parenchyma; no conversion to radical nephrectomy required.
- Plan: discharge today with instructions for routine wound care and analgesia, return precautions for bleeding, fever, or concerning drainage (urinary leak). Arrange outpatient follow-up with surgical clinic and pathology review.

4. Discharge Summary
Admission diagnosis:
- Left renal mass (3.2 cm), planned nephron-sparing surgery.

Procedures performed:
- Laparoscopic partial nephrectomy, left kidney. Tumor excised with thin rim of normal kidney; renal parenchyma repaired. Specimen removed intact via limited port extension.

Hospital course:
- Operation under general anesthesia uneventful. Renal blood flow was temporarily occluded intraoperatively to facilitate tumor excision and parenchymal reconstruction. Estimated intraoperative blood loss minimal; no intraoperative complications and no need to convert to open or to perform radical nephrectomy. Postoperatively patient received IV fluids and pain medication. Electrolyte and fluid balance monitored. No postoperative bleeding, wound infection, or urinary leak identified during hospitalization.

Final diagnosis:
- Small renal mass of the left kidney — treated with laparoscopic partial nephrectomy.

Condition at discharge:
- Stable. Pain controlled on oral analgesics. Tolerating oral intake. Fluid and electrolytes acceptable for discharge.

Discharge instructions / follow-up:
- Wound care, activity as tolerated. Return immediately for signs of postoperative bleeding, fever, or wound drainage suggestive of infection or urinary leak. Clinic follow-up arranged for surgical review and pathology results. Rationale discussed that partial nephrectomy preserves renal function better than removal of the entire kidney and that common postoperative complications include bleeding, infection, and urinary leak — none observed prior to discharge.

Length of stay:
- 3 days.

1. Admission Note
HPI:
- 28-year-old male pedestrian struck by motor vehicle, brought by EMS. Brief loss of consciousness at scene. Vomited prior to arrival. GCS 7 (E1 V1 M5). Unable to protect airway and high risk for aspiration given recent emesis and unknown NPO status. Decision made for emergent rapid sequence induction and endotracheal intubation for airway protection.

Physical exam on arrival:
- VS: HR 102, BP 138/82, RR 18 (assisted planned), SpO2 94% on non-rebreather prior to intubation.
- HEENT: scalp abrasion right temporal, no obvious facial skeletal destruction.
- Pupils equal, 3 mm, reactive.
- Neck: no step-off, cervical collar in place.
- Cardiac: tachycardic, regular.
- Lungs: clear to auscultation prior to intubation.
- Abdomen: soft, non-distended.
- Neuro: GCS 7 as above. No focal motor asymmetry on gross exam.

Assessment / Plan (pre-intubation):
- Indication: traumatic brain injury with depressed mental status and recent emesis → high aspiration risk and inability to protect airway. Plan for rapid sequence induction (RSI) and endotracheal intubation.
- RSI technique to be used: no manual ventilation after induction until tube secured; cricoid pressure (Sellick) to be applied given aspiration risk. Difficult airway equipment and plan per difficult airway algorithm available; paralytic reversal agent (sugammadex) available if needed.
- Premedication: fentanyl 100 mcg IV to blunt sympathetic response; lidocaine 100 mg IV to attenuate ICP rise and reduce etomidate myoclonus.
- Induction: etomidate 30 mg IV (rapid onset, minimal hemodynamic effect, reduces cerebral blood flow).
- Paralytic: succinylcholine 100 mg IV to achieve rapid neuromuscular blockade.
- Personnel: anesthesia/intubating team, suction at bedside.

Procedure note:
- Preoxygenated. Cricoid pressure applied. Induction with fentanyl and etomidate after lidocaine pretreatment. Succinycholine administered. Direct laryngoscopy attempted. Initial difficulty visualizing cords noted with cricoid pressure in place consistent with possible laryngeal distortion; cricoid pressure was promptly released and laryngoscopy repeated. 8.0 ETT passed with cuff inflated. End-tidal CO2 confirmed. Bilateral breath sounds present. No manual bag-mask ventilation was performed between induction and tube placement.

Post-intubation plan:
- Admission to neuro-ICU, mechanical ventilation, repeat neurologic exam and non-contrast head CT STAT. Monitor for hemodynamic changes (note etomidate potential for transient adrenal suppression). Monitor for allergic reaction to neuromuscular blocker. Continue standard neurotrauma care.

2. Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
- Overnight course: Patient remained intubated and sedated. Hemodynamically stable. No evidence of anaphylaxis or bronchospasm. No further emesis.
- Imaging: Non-contrast head CT completed after intubation — small right convexity acute subdural hemorrhage without midline shift or mass effect.
- Neuro exam: GCS now 8T (E2 VT M6) while sedated; pupils equal and reactive.
- Ventilator: volume-control, settings to maintain adequate oxygenation and normocapnia.
- Medications/procedures: Continued sedation; no additional paralytic doses given. No vasopressor requirement.
- Issues/plan:
  1) Airway: ETT in good position, cuff inflated, continuous capnography. Continue ventilatory support. Maintain NPO status due to aspiration risk.
  2) Neurologic injury: observe in neuro-ICU. Repeat CT head PRN for neurologic change. Maintain head of bed elevation and ICP-appropriate measures. Avoid agents that increase ICP; lidocaine pretreatment given prior to induction per plan.
  3) Etomidate note: documented etomidate given for induction; be aware of possible adrenal suppression — monitor hemodynamics and electrolytes; no evidence of hypotension to date.
  4) Anaphylaxis/difficult airway preparedness: No signs of allergic reaction. Difficult airway tools and paralytic reversal (sugammadex available) remain immediately available if needed.
- Plan: Daily sedation interruption to assess neuro status when clinically appropriate. Continue neuro checks every hour. Maintain ICU-level care.

3. Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
- Overnight and morning: Neurologic exam improved as sedation weaned. GCS 11 (E4 V2 M5) while spontaneously breathing trials initiated. No emesis. Hemodynamics stable.
- Airway management: Performed spontaneous breathing trial; patient protective airway reflexes returning and cough present. Extubation criteria met.
- Extubation procedure: Suctioned oropharynx, cuff deflated, extubated to supplemental oxygen. No immediate stridor or desaturation. Post-extubation SpO2 98% on 2 L NC. No need for reintubation.
- Post-extubation course: Awake, following commands, maintaining airway, communicating. No signs of aspiration or respiratory distress.
- Plan:
  1) Transfer from neuro-ICU to step-down unit later today.
  2) Continue neurologic surveillance; repeat head CT only if decline.
  3) Advance diet as tolerated once swallow screen passed.
  4) Outpatient neurosurgery follow-up arranged.

4. Discharge Summary
- Admission date: [Day 0]. Discharge date: Hospital Day 3.
- Reason for admission: Traumatic brain injury after pedestrian vs motor vehicle collision with depressed mental status and recent emesis; required airway protection.
- Hospital course summary:
  - Presentation with GCS 7 and recent emesis → high aspiration risk and inability to protect airway → emergent rapid sequence induction and endotracheal intubation performed in ED.
  - RSI medications/procedures: Premedication with fentanyl 100 mcg IV and lidocaine 100 mg IV. Induction with etomidate 30 mg IV. Paralysis with succinylcholine 100 mg IV. Cricoid pressure applied during induction; transient laryngeal distortion noted requiring release of cricoid pressure; subsequently successful intubation with 8.0 ETT; end-tidal CO2 confirmed. No manual bag-mask ventilation performed between induction and intubation per RSI technique.
  - Post-intubation CT head: small right convexity acute subdural hemorrhage without shift. Managed non-operatively.
  - ICU course uncomplicated. No hemodynamic instability, no evidence of anaphylaxis to neuromuscular blockade. Etomidate noted in record — monitored for adrenal suppression; no clinical sequelae observed.
  - Sedation weaned on HD3; patient passed spontaneous breathing trial and was successfully extubated without complication.
- Condition at discharge: Neurologically improved, following commands, tolerating oral intake, stable vitals, ambulatory with assistance as tolerated.
- Discharge instructions/plan:
  - Neurosurgery clinic follow-up in 1 week for repeat evaluation and discussion of repeat imaging if indicated or sooner if neurologic decline.
  - Return precautions: new or worsening headache, vomiting, focal weakness, seizure, changes in consciousness, fever, or breathing difficulty — go to ED.
  - No driving or heavy machinery until cleared by neurosurgery.
- Medications at discharge: none started in hospital beyond short-term agents given for RSI and sedation; outpatient med recommendations to be made by neurosurgery if needed.
- Final diagnosis:
  1) Traumatic brain injury with small right acute subdural hemorrhage — non-operative.
  2) Airway protection via rapid sequence induction and endotracheal intubation (etomidate + succinylcholine) — successful.

1. Admission Note
HPI:
68-year-old male presents with 2 days of progressive left calf and thigh pain, swelling and redness. Pain described as throbbing, worse with weight-bearing, causing decreased ambulation. Onset 5 days after elective left total knee arthroplasty. No chest pain, shortness of breath, hemoptysis, or syncope. No prior history of venous thromboembolism. Recent surgery and reduced mobility are noted risk factors for venous thrombosis.

Physical exam:
VS: afebrile, HR 86, BP 132/78, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air.
General: NAD.
Cardiac: RRR, no murmurs.
Lungs: clear to auscultation.
Left lower extremity: calf and thigh swelling compared with right, pitting edema to mid-calf, tenderness along deep venous distribution, warmth and mild erythema, dilated superficial veins. No cyanosis. Peripheral pulses intact. Right lower extremity without swelling or tenderness.
Neuro: grossly intact.

Assessment and Plan:
- Impression: Acute proximal left lower extremity deep vein thrombosis (DVT), likely provoked by recent knee surgery and immobilization.
- Diagnostic data: Wells score consistent with high clinical probability for DVT. D-dimer elevated. Duplex venous ultrasound of left lower extremity demonstrates occlusive thrombus in the popliteal and common femoral veins confirming proximal DVT.
- Plan:
  1) Anticoagulation: initiate parenteral heparin injections immediately and begin oral warfarin. Continue heparin until oral anticoagulation established per inpatient monitoring (bridging required when initiating warfarin).
  2) Symptomatic care: leg elevation, analgesia PRN, encourage early ambulation as tolerated.
  3) Monitor: baseline CBC/chemistry, daily checks for signs of bleeding, and serial coagulation testing to determine when to stop parenteral heparin once oral anticoagulation therapeutic.
  4) Education: counsel patient on risk of pulmonary embolism (embolization risk and symptoms), post-thrombotic syndrome (long-term leg symptoms), and need for outpatient anticoagulation management.
  5) Thrombophilia testing: deferred given a provoked DVT in the postoperative setting.
  6) Disposition: admit for anticoagulation initiation and monitoring with plan for transition to oral therapy and discharge once oral anticoagulation established and patient ambulatory.

2. Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
Subjective:
Patient reports decreased calf pain at rest; still uncomfortable with prolonged standing but able to ambulate short distances with assistance. No chest pain, dyspnea, hemoptysis, or syncope.

Objective:
VS stable. Left leg exam: persistent swelling and tenderness but improved warmth and erythema. No new neurologic deficits. No signs of pulmonary embolism on bedside assessment.

Assessment:
- Acute proximal left lower extremity DVT on heparin bridge to warfarin.
- Clinical improvement in leg symptoms.

Plan:
- Continue heparin injections and warfarin dosing per protocol while monitoring coagulation studies. INR remains below therapeutic threshold; continue bridging.
- Maintain leg elevation, encourage frequent ambulation and mobility as tolerated to reduce stasis.
- Continue analgesics PRN.
- Monitor for bleeding and any cardiopulmonary symptoms suggestive of PE.
- Reinforce education regarding signs/symptoms of PE and limb-threatening complications (e.g., increasing pain, cyanosis) and to notify staff immediately if noted.

3. Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
Subjective:
Patient ambulates independently with minimal assistance. Pain further improved. No cardiopulmonary complaints.

Objective:
VS stable. Left leg: less tenderness, decreased edema compared with admission though still larger than contralateral side. No cyanosis. No signs of PE.

Assessment:
- Acute proximal left lower extremity DVT — responding to anticoagulation.
- Warfarin therapy now therapeutic on coagulation testing; heparin injections discontinued.

Plan:
- Transition care to outpatient warfarin management. Provide anticoagulation education and arrange follow-up with anticoagulation clinic/primary care for monitoring and dosing adjustments.
- Continue mobility and leg elevation. Counsel on exercise and weight management for future VTE prevention.
- Provide return precautions: immediate evaluation for sudden shortness of breath, pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, syncope (possible PE), or worsening leg pain/ cyanosis (phlegmasia cerulea dolens/limb threat).
- Discharge planning arranged for later today.

4. Discharge Summary
Admission diagnosis: Acute proximal left lower extremity deep vein thrombosis (left popliteal and common femoral veins), provoked by recent total knee arthroplasty and postoperative immobilization.

Hospital course:
Patient admitted for management of acute proximal DVT. On admission, clinical probability assessment and elevated D-dimer prompted diagnostic venous duplex ultrasound which confirmed occlusive thrombus in the left popliteal and common femoral veins. Anticoagulation was initiated with parenteral heparin injections and oral warfarin. The patient’s leg pain and swelling improved with anticoagulation, elevation, and ambulation. Heparin was continued until oral anticoagulation was established; once oral therapy reached therapeutic effect, parenteral heparin was discontinued. No evidence of pulmonary embolism during hospitalization. Thrombophilia workup was deferred given a clearly provoked event (recent surgery).

Discharge condition:
Clinically improved, ambulatory with stable vital signs, no cardiopulmonary symptoms.

Medications at discharge:
- Warfarin, continued as oral anticoagulant (patient instructed to follow with anticoagulation clinic for dosing and monitoring). (Parenteral heparin discontinued.)
- Analgesics as needed.

Follow-up and instructions:
- Anticoagulation clinic/primary care follow-up within 3–7 days for INR/anticoagulation management.
- Return to ED immediately for sudden shortness of breath, chest pain, coughing up blood, fainting, or for worsening leg pain, marked increase in swelling, blue discoloration, or other signs concerning for limb threat.
- Counseling provided regarding risk of recurrent venous thromboembolism (approximately 30% risk within 10 years following an initial event), and on prevention strategies: maintain activity and avoid prolonged immobility, weight management, and use of appropriate prophylaxis (pharmacologic anticoagulation and/or intermittent pneumatic compression) for future higher-risk surgeries.
- Patient advised on signs of post-thrombotic syndrome (chronic leg pain, swelling, heaviness, itching, or ulceration) and to report persistent symptoms to outpatient team.

Problem list:
1) Acute provoked proximal left lower extremity DVT — on outpatient warfarin with hematology/anticoagulation clinic follow-up.
2) Postoperative immobilization — counseling provided on early ambulation and VTE prevention for future procedures.

1. Admission Note
HPI:
72-year-old man with history of hypertension and 40 pack-year tobacco use presents with 3 weeks of intermittent gross hematuria and progressive left-sided flank pain. Reports 6 lb unintentional weight loss over 2 months, night sweats and decreased appetite. No history of dialysis or prior renal cystic disease. No fevers at home. Denies dysuria, urinary frequency or prior urologic malignancy.

Physical exam:
T 36.8 C, HR 82, BP 148/86, RR 16, SpO2 98% RA. General: thin, older male in no acute distress. Abdomen: palpable mass in left flank/upper quadrant on deep palpation, non-tender to light touch. No stigmata of chronic liver disease. Cardiopulmonary exam unremarkable. No peripheral lymphadenopathy.

Relevant data on admission:
CBC: Hgb 18.2 g/dL, Hct 54%, platelets 390 x10^9/L. BMP: creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, calcium 11.0 mg/dL. Urinalysis: gross blood, RBCs >100/hpf. Chest x-ray prior to admission without focal pulmonary consolidation. CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast: 7.2 x 6.8 x 6.5 cm enhancing mass of the left kidney arising from the renal cortex, radiographic appearance most consistent with renal cell carcinoma; no obvious renal vein thrombus on imaging, no definite regional adenopathy visualized. CT chest without metastatic nodules.

Assessment and Plan:
- Left renal mass, 7.2 cm, radiographically consistent with renal cell carcinoma (tumour arising from proximal renal tubular epithelium suspected). Risk factors: long-standing hypertension, significant tobacco use.
- Paraneoplastic findings: erythrocytosis (Hgb 18.2) and hypercalcemia (Ca 11.0) likely secondary to tumour hormone production.
Plan:
1. Urology consult placed for management; discuss surgical resection as initial therapy (partial vs complete nephrectomy). Given tumor size and location, radical nephrectomy favored.
2. Pre-operative labs and EKG; baseline creatinine established. Repeat calcium and CBC for trend monitoring.
3. Staging: CT chest already performed and without focal pulmonary metastases. Will obtain formal staging post-op pathology.
4. Discussed with patient that initial definitive management is surgical removal of the affected kidney; if metastasis identified, oncology referral for consideration of targeted therapy or immunotherapy.
5. Continue antihypertensive regimen; symptomatic management as needed. NPO after midnight pending pre-op clearance.
6. Counselled on risks, benefits and alternatives; patient agreeable to surgical plan.

2. Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
Overnight course:
- Hemodynamically stable. No fevers. Pain controlled with oral analgesics.
- Repeat labs this morning: Hgb 18.0 g/dL, Ca 10.8 mg/dL, creatinine 0.95 mg/dL. Urine still grossly positive for blood.

Urology evaluation:
- Urology reviewed imaging and clinical course; consensus to proceed with left radical nephrectomy given tumor size (~7 cm) and peripheral location. No radiographic evidence of venous invasion or distant metastasis.
- Patient counselled regarding surgical procedure, expected hospital course, and potential need for oncologic follow-up if pathology demonstrates aggressive features or metastasis. Informed consent obtained.

Plan:
1. Proceed with left radical nephrectomy tomorrow (Hospital Day 3) in OR. Pre-op antibiotics per surgical protocol.
2. Continue routine perioperative orders, repeat coagulation panel pre-op.
3. Repeat calcium and CBC on arrival to OR; trends to be followed post-operatively given paraneoplastic hypercalcemia and erythrocytosis.
4. Notify oncology post-op if pathology reveals nodal involvement or metastatic disease; discuss immunotherapy/targeted therapy as indicated.
5. Continue home antihypertensive medications; hold morning dose per anesthesia protocol as indicated.

3. Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
Procedure:
- Left radical nephrectomy via open approach performed without intraoperative complications. Estimated blood loss 350 mL. No gross local invasion beyond renal capsule noted intraoperatively. No obvious regional lymphadenopathy. Specimen sent to pathology.

Post-op course (POD 0):
- Hemodynamically stable in PACU. Pain controlled with IV then oral analgesics. Urine output adequate.
- Post-op labs: Hgb 15.2 g/dL, Hct 46%, platelets 370 x10^9/L, Ca 9.6 mg/dL, creatinine 1.0 mg/dL.
- Wound clean and dry. No signs of bleeding or hemodynamic compromise.

Assessment and Plan:
- Status post left radical nephrectomy for presumed renal cell carcinoma. Intraoperative findings: solitary 7–8 cm solid renal mass confined to kidney; no gross venous thrombus or adjacent organ invasion.
- Post-op improvement in hematologic and calcium parameters consistent with removal of hormone-secreting tumor source.
- Plan:
  1. Admit to surgical floor for routine post-operative recovery. Monitor vitals, urine output, and hemoglobin.
  2. Pathology pending; will determine histologic subtype and margin status. If pathology shows metastatic spread or high-risk features, arrange oncology follow-up for consideration of systemic options (immunotherapy/targeted therapy).
  3. Advance diet as tolerated, early ambulation. Continue antihypertensives as appropriate.
  4. Discharge planning anticipated once pain controlled, ambulating, tolerating PO, and labs stable.

4. Discharge Summary
Admission diagnosis:
- Left renal mass suspicious for renal cell carcinoma with paraneoplastic erythrocytosis and hypercalcemia.

Hospital course:
- Admitted for evaluation of gross hematuria and left flank mass. CT abdomen/pelvis demonstrated 7.2 cm enhancing left renal mass consistent with renal cell carcinoma. Paraneoplastic erythrocytosis (Hgb 18.2 g/dL) and hypercalcemia (Ca 11.0 mg/dL) noted on admission.
- Urology consulted and patient taken to OR on Hospital Day 3 for left radical nephrectomy.
- Intraoperative findings: solitary 7–8 cm solid mass confined to the kidney, no gross renal vein or caval thrombus, no adjacent organ invasion.
- Post-operative course uncomplicated. Hemodynamics stable. Post-op labs show falling hemoglobin to 15.2 g/dL and calcium normalized to 9.6 mg/dL. Renal function unchanged from baseline (creatinine 1.0 mg/dL).
- Final pathology: clear cell renal cell carcinoma arising from proximal tubular epithelium, tumor size 7.6 cm, confined to the kidney, surgical margins negative, no lymphovascular invasion identified. No nodal tissue was positive on intraoperative inspection; pathology did not identify involved regional lymph nodes on the specimen submitted.

Discharge condition:
- Stable, ambulating, tolerating diet, pain controlled on oral analgesics.

Discharge medications/instructions:
- Resume home antihypertensive regimen. Continue prescribed oral analgesic as directed. Wound care: keep incision clean and dry; follow-up for suture removal as outpatient.
- Activity: gradual return to normal activity; avoid heavy lifting for 4–6 weeks.
- Return precautions: fever, increasing abdominal pain, bleeding, decreased urine output, or any new neurologic symptoms.

Follow-up:
- Urology clinic follow-up scheduled in 2 weeks for post-op visit and review of final pathology.
- Oncology referral placed for consideration of surveillance versus systemic therapy only if recurrent or metastatic disease identified on follow-up imaging or clinical course. Given current pathology showing organ-confined clear cell RCC with negative margins and no radiographic metastases, prognosis consistent with non-metastatic disease (five-year survival generally favorable compared with metastatic disease).
- Surveillance imaging plan to be arranged by urology per standard post-nephrectomy protocol (CT chest/abdomen at intervals) to monitor for recurrence or metastasis.

Primary surgeon: Urology Service
Discharge date: Hospital Day 3 post-admission.

Admission Note
Date/time: Admission day 0

HPI:
68-year-old male with end-stage right knee osteoarthritis scheduled for elective right total knee arthroplasty. Past medical history notable for moderate COPD with exertional dyspnea; no history of bleeding disorder, no anticoagulation, no known spinal deformity, no prior allergic reaction to local anesthetics or opioids. Patient counseled on anesthesia options and consents to spinal anaesthesia to avoid general anaesthesia and potential respiratory complications related to COPD.

Pre-op assessment:
- Airway: no anticipated difficult airway but spinal favored due to pulmonary status.
- No local skin infection at lumbar site. Platelets/bleeding history reviewed (no coagulopathy reported).
- Patient understands risks including hypotension, bradycardia, post-dural-puncture headache, transient neurological symptoms and rare major complications (nerve injury, spinal epidural hematoma, infection).

Physical exam (pre-op):
- General: alert, in no acute distress.
- Chest: clear to auscultation, mild prolonged expiratory phase consistent with COPD.
- Cardiovascular: regular rate and rhythm, no signs of severe aortic stenosis.
- Neurologic: baseline motor and sensory exam grossly intact in lower extremities.

Anesthesia plan / A/P:
- Proceed with single-shot spinal anaesthesia for right total knee arthroplasty.
- Technique: patient positioned sitting. Midline approach at L3–L4 interspace. Use 26G atraumatic (Braun Atraucan) pencil-point spinal needle to minimize risk of post-dural-puncture headache. Advance needle into subarachnoid space and administer single-shot local anaesthetic with intrathecal opioid for intraoperative anaesthesia and postoperative analgesia.
- Minimal sedation to be provided as needed.
- Intraoperative goals: monitor for and treat expected physiologic effects of neuraxial blockade (sympathetic block) including hypotension and bradycardia; have fluids and supportive measures available.
- Postoperative plan: monitor motor and sensory recovery, assess for headache, nausea, transient neurologic symptoms; multimodal analgesia including intrathecal opioid effect and scheduled NSAIDs.

Hospital Day 2 Progress Note
Postoperative day 1

Course overnight:
- Patient taken to PACU after surgery. Spinal anaesthetic provided adequate surgical anaesthesia and intraoperative course uncomplicated.
- Immediate post-spinal period: patient experienced mild hypotension and transient bradycardia consistent with sympathetic blockade. Treated conservatively with intravenous fluids and supportive care; hemodynamics stabilized without need for escalation.
- Nausea transient and resolved in PACU.
- No evidence of excessive bleeding or local infection.

Current status:
- Vitals stable, hemodynamically stable on ward.
- Pain: well controlled on effect of intrathecal opioid and scheduled NSAIDs; reports tolerable breakthrough pain at surgical site with activity.
- Neurologic: lower extremity motor and sensory exam appropriate for recent neuraxial block with progressive return of motor function; no new focal deficits.
- Headache: denies positional headache or symptoms suggestive of post-dural-puncture headache.
- Ambulation: physical therapy assisted ambulation initiated and tolerated with assistance.

Plan:
- Continue multimodal analgesia with NSAIDs and oral analgesics as required.
- Continue monitoring for delayed complications of spinal anaesthesia (post-dural-puncture headache, transient neurological symptoms, signs of spinal epidural hematoma or infection).
- Advance activity per orthopedics/physical therapy. Plan reassessment tomorrow for discharge readiness.

Hospital Day 3 Progress Note
Postoperative day 2

Course since last note:
- Hemodynamics remained stable overnight; no recurrent hypotension or bradycardia.
- Motor and sensory function returned to baseline expected after spinal anaesthesia.
- No headache, no new neurologic complaints, no signs of wound infection.
- Pain controlled on oral analgesics and scheduled NSAIDs; mobilizing with PT and increasing independence.

Assessment:
- Uncomplicated postoperative recovery following spinal anaesthesia for right total knee arthroplasty.
- Prior mild, transient hypotension and bradycardia attributable to expected sympathetic blockade; resolved with conservative management.
- No evidence of post-dural-puncture headache or transient neurological symptoms.

Plan:
- Discharge today with outpatient follow-up arranged by orthopedics.
- Reinforce activity restrictions and home analgesia plan.
- Instruct patient to seek immediate care for severe positional headache, new or worsening back pain with leg pain, new motor weakness or numbness, bowel/bladder dysfunction, fever, or signs of wound infection.

Discharge Summary
Admission diagnosis:
- Right end-stage knee osteoarthritis.

Procedure:
- Right total knee arthroplasty (orthopedics).

Anesthesia:
- Single-shot spinal anaesthesia performed in sitting position via L3–L4 interspace using a 26G atraumatic (Braun Atraucan) pencil-point spinal needle. Intrathecal local anaesthetic administered with intrathecal opioid for postoperative analgesia. Minimal procedural sedation provided.

Hospital course:
- Intraoperative and postoperative course uncomplicated. The spinal block provided effective motor, sensory and autonomic blockade for surgery. Immediately post-spinal, patient had mild hypotension and transient bradycardia consistent with sympathetic blockade; managed conservatively with intravenous fluids and supportive measures with prompt resolution. Pain control adequate on intrathecal opioid plus scheduled NSAIDs. No post-dural-puncture headache, no transient neurological symptoms, and no signs of infection or hematoma during hospitalization. Physical therapy progressed; patient ambulatory with assistance at discharge.

Discharge condition:
- Hemodynamically stable. Neurologic exam at baseline for age and recent surgery. Pain controlled on oral regimen.

Medications on discharge:
- Continue scheduled NSAIDs and oral analgesics as prescribed by surgical team. (Intrathecal opioid effect expected to wane over first 24–48 hours post-op.)

Follow-up and precautions:
- Orthopedics clinic follow-up as arranged.
- Return to ED or contact surgical team urgently for: severe positional headache (suggestive of post-dural-puncture headache), new or worsening back pain with leg pain, new motor weakness or sensory loss, bowel or bladder dysfunction, fever, or signs of wound infection—these may represent rare but serious complications of neuraxial blockade (spinal epidural hematoma, infection, meningitis, nerve injury).

Signed,
Attending Physician (de-identified)
